concurrent_users.peak_reason: Flash sales, marketing campaigns, seasonal shopping events

transactions_per_hour.peak_context: Peak shopping hours (evening, weekend), promotional events

data_volume_projections.growth_driver: Product catalog expansion, transaction history, customer reviews, order records

server_sizing.architecture_note: Web tier: reverse proxy + static content. App tier: business logic + API. DB tier: primary + read replica. Cache: Redis for sessions and frequently accessed data.

database_servers.storage_per_server: 1000 GB SSD (NVMe for primary, SSD for replica)

indexing_strategy.order_date_index: BTREE on created_date for time-range queries
indexing_strategy.product_search_index: Full-text index on product_name, description, sku

cost_optimization_opportunities: Database query optimization to reduce compute
Content delivery optimization to reduce egress

Password hashing (bcrypt/argon2); database connection pool; session storage in Redis
Database writes on wishlist updates; indexing by customer_id critical; caching wishlist retrieval
High write frequency; Redis session-based storage essential; inventory availability checks; discount/coupon validation
Full-text search indexing (Elasticsearch or similar); query complexity; filter cardinality; pagination; cache product search results
Category tree queries; hierarchical data access; caching category structures; lazy loading subcategories
Large product images; related products queries; reviews loading; CDN caching critical for images; lazy-load reviews
Static content delivery; cached homepage rendering; featured products queries; aggressive browser caching
Email queue processing; attachment storage; conversation threading; archiving old support tickets
Address validation API calls; state/country lookups; form session storage; GDPR compliance for address data
Shipping rate calculations; carrier API integration; weight/dimension calculations; caching carrier rates
Payment method availability lookup; PCI compliance scoping; session state management
Payment gateway integration; SSL/TLS overhead; PCI-DSS tokenization; fraud detection API calls; payment processing latency critical
Order data aggregation; invoice generation; email dispatch; payment confirmation verification
One-time checkout flow; email verification; minimal data persistence; session cleanup
Email verification; password hashing; database writes; duplicate email checks; GDPR consent recording
Credential verification; brute-force protection; session initiation; 2FA integration; Redis session storage
Order history queries; order status updates; return/refund processing; report generation; archiving old orders
Review moderation workflow; spam/abuse detection; rating aggregation; helpful vote tracking; indexing for search
Static XML sitemap generation; caching sitemap; SEO crawl optimization; breadcrumb navigation
Content publishing workflow; scheduled posting; pagination; caching news listings; RSS feed generation
Blog post rendering; comment threading; tag indexing; full-text search; RSS feeds; caching blog listings
Multi-product data aggregation; attribute comparison; session-based comparison lists; caching comparison data
Checkout session initiation; cart validation; payment gateway handoff; order numbering
TOS version management; acceptance tracking; policy enforcement in workflow; audit logging

summary.coverage: All critical user-facing features and key transaction paths

performance_budgets.transaction_name: View Product Catalog with Currency Conversion

component_breakdown.optimization: Use Route53 or Cloudflare with GeoIP routing
CloudFront with 1-year TTL for versioned assets
Nginx with gzip compression; 4 server instances
In-memory currency cache; update every 24 hours

performance_budgets.sla_statement: 99.5% of login attempts shall complete within 1200ms (p95) under standard network conditions. Authentication failure due to system latency (not invalid credentials) shall be < 0.5%.

component_breakdown.optimization: Redis cluster with 3 nodes; replication enabled
Indexed on email; connection pool min 10, max 50
bcrypt cost factor 12; intentional crypto delay for security
JWT token generation; sign with RS256

performance_budgets.sla_statement: 95% of registration attempts shall complete within 1200ms (p95). Welcome/verification email shall be sent asynchronously and not block response time.

component_breakdown.optimization: Client-side validation first (no network); server-side confirmation
Cached in Redis; index on email column
Single INSERT; minimal data; async secondary tasks
bcrypt cost 12; done once at registration
Send to message queue; not in response path

performance_budgets.sla_statement: 99% of add-to-cart operations shall complete within 800ms (p95). Cart must reflect product immediately via AJAX (no full page reload).

component_breakdown.optimization: In-memory catalog; no DB query needed
Redis write; eventual consistency with DB
Return updated cart total; no page reload
Use cached tax rates and shipping rules

performance_budgets.sla_statement: 99% of search queries returning < 1000 products shall respond within 1200ms (p95). Large result sets (> 1000 products) shall respond within 2000ms (p99). Search latency directly correlates with conversion; higher latency = higher bounce rate.

component_breakdown.optimization: Dedicated search cluster; tuned query DSL
Computed from index; no secondary DB queries
React/Vue with virtual scrolling for results
Use cursor-based pagination instead of offset for large pages
Append to existing results; minimal DOM manipulation
Full-page cache in Redis for 1 hour

performance_budgets.sla_statement: 98% of product detail pages shall load within 2200ms (p95). Products are primary revenue driver; latency directly impacts conversion.

component_breakdown.optimization: Cache product details for 24 hours; invalidate on update
Cache homepage for 1 hour; invalidate on content change

performance_budgets.feature_name: Checkout (Address, Shipping, Payment Method, Payment Info, Confirmation)
performance_budgets.sla_statement: 99% of address validation shall complete within 2000ms (p95). Address validation may invoke external API (USPS, UPS); if API slow, show 'Validating...' and accept address anyway after 2000ms timeout.

component_breakdown.optimization: Timeout 2000ms; cache validated addresses for 90 days
Return next step form; minimal data

performance_budgets.feature_name: Checkout (Address, Shipping, Payment Method, Payment Info, Confirmation)
performance_budgets.sla_statement: 95% of shipping rate calculations shall complete within 2500ms (p95). Shipping calculation may invoke 3PL APIs (FedEx, UPS); implement async calculation with estimated rates displayed immediately, actual rates updated asynchronously.

component_breakdown.optimization: Timeout 2000ms; cache per postal code for 24 hours
Return form with cached estimates; update via AJAX when actual rates available

If payment timeout exceeds 5000ms (p99), order marked 'Pending' and customer shown 'Payment Verification in Progress' message; system polls gateway every 10 seconds for 5 minutes.

component_breakdown.optimization: May require user redirect for 3D Secure; timeout 300 seconds
Insert order; reference payment transaction ID
Minimal HTML; load order details asynchronously

performance_budgets.sla_statement: End-to-end guest checkout (5 steps: Address, Shipping, Payment Method, Payment Info, Confirmation) shall complete within 9000ms (p95) total. This is sum of individual step latencies; each step must stay within budget to meet overall target.

component_breakdown.optimization: 10-item batches; database indexed on product_id, created_date
Denormalized rating fields (avg_rating, count_5star, etc.); cache 1 hour
Generate once daily; cache XML for 24 hours

performance_budgets.transaction_name: View Blog Post or News Article

sla_definitions.transaction: Load product page or category page
sla_definitions.sla_statement: 95% of product catalog page requests shall respond within 2200ms (p95) from client click to fully rendered page. Response time measured from client-side: click to DOMContentLoaded. Excludes network jitter > 100ms (measured via latency histogram).
sla_definitions.measurement: Real User Monitoring (RUM) via Datadog, percentage of requests meeting latency target
sla_definitions.alerting: If p95 > 2200ms for > 5 minutes, page alert
sla_definitions.exceptions: International users on slow networks (3G) have relaxed SLA of 6500ms (p95)

sla_definitions.transaction: Execute search query and display results
sla_definitions.sla_statement: 99% of search queries shall respond with results within 1200ms (p95). Queries returning > 1000 products may extend to 2000ms (p99). Search pagination (page N) shall respond within 700ms (p95).
sla_definitions.measurement: Application Performance Monitoring (APM) - track search endpoint latency distribution
sla_definitions.alerting: If search p95 > 1200ms, alert within 2 minutes. Investigate slow queries (> 2000ms).
sla_definitions.exceptions: Complex queries (multiple filters, very large result sets > 10,000 products) may take longer

sla_definitions.sla_statement: 99.5% of login attempts shall succeed within 1200ms (p95). System-induced latency failures < 0.5%. Registration shall complete within 1200ms (p95); welcome email shall be sent asynchronously within 5 minutes.
sla_definitions.measurement: APM - monitor authentication endpoint latency and error rates
sla_definitions.alerting: If login latency p95 > 1200ms or error rate > 1%, page alert
sla_definitions.security_note: Password hashing uses bcrypt (150-200ms intentional delay for security); this is included in budget

sla_definitions.transaction: Add product to cart, view cart, update quantities
sla_definitions.sla_statement: 99% of add-to-cart operations shall complete within 800ms (p95). Cart page render shall complete within 1000ms (p95). Update quantity shall complete within 500ms (p95). Cart operations may use optimistic UI updates (update client immediately, sync with server asynchronously).
sla_definitions.measurement: RUM + APM - track button clicks to DOM updates
sla_definitions.alerting: If cart operations > 800ms p95, investigate and alert
sla_definitions.optimization_note: Implement Redis-backed cart with eventual consistency to database

sla_definitions.transaction: Add to wishlist, view wishlist, remove from wishlist
sla_definitions.sla_statement: 95% of wishlist operations shall complete within 800ms (p95). Wishlist is secondary feature; latency SLA is relaxed compared to cart.

98% of address validation shall complete within 2000ms (p95). Address validation may invoke external APIs (USPS, UPS); if API timeout > 2000ms, system shall accept address and mark for manual verification.
sla_definitions.measurement: APM - track address validation endpoint and upstream API latency
sla_definitions.alerting: If address validation > 2500ms, check external API status
sla_definitions.external_dependency: USPS/UPS address validation API; typical latency 800-1500ms, may spike during high load

sla_definitions.transaction: Select shipping method and calculate shipping cost
sla_definitions.sla_statement: 95% of shipping method selection and rate calculation shall complete within 2500ms (p95). Large shipping rate calculations (10+ methods) may extend to 3500ms (p99). If 3PL APIs slow, show estimated rates immediately and update asynchronously.
sla_definitions.measurement: APM - track shipping calculation API latency
sla_definitions.alerting: If shipping calculation > 3000ms, check 3PL API status
sla_definitions.external_dependency: FedEx, UPS, USPS APIs for real-time rate quotes; typical latency 1000-2000ms

sla_definitions.transaction: Submit payment information and receive authorization response
sla_definitions.sla_statement: 90% of payment authorizations shall complete within 3500ms (p95). 95% shall complete within 5000ms (p99). Payment gateways are inherently variable; timeout at 5000ms with fallback to 'Authorization Pending' status. If authorization not received within 5 seconds, customer shown 'Payment Verification in Progress' and system polls gateway every 10 seconds for 5 minutes.
sla_definitions.measurement: APM - track payment gateway API latency; monitor authorization success rate
sla_definitions.alerting: If payment gateway latency p99 > 5000ms or authorization rate < 95%, alert immediately
sla_definitions.external_dependency: Stripe, PayPal, Square, or similar; typical latency 1000-3000ms, can reach 5000ms during peak load or fraud scoring
sla_definitions.idempotency_note: Payment requests MUST include idempotency key to prevent double-charging on retry

sla_definitions.transaction: View order confirmation page after payment authorization
sla_definitions.sla_statement: 99% of confirmation pages shall render within 500ms (p95) from payment authorization completion. Confirmation email shall be sent asynchronously within 5 minutes (separate SLA).
sla_definitions.measurement: RUM - measure time from payment submission to confirmation page visible

sla_definitions.transaction: Complete entire checkout flow without registration
sla_definitions.sla_statement: 90% of guest checkouts shall complete within 9000ms (p95) total time from cart to confirmation. This is aggregate of 5 steps (Address 2000ms, Shipping 2500ms, Payment Method 500ms, Payment Authorization 3500ms, Confirmation 500ms). Breakdown per-step must be maintained to meet overall target.
sla_definitions.measurement: RUM - measure time from 'Checkout' button click to order confirmation visible
sla_definitions.alerting: If end-to-end latency > 10000ms, alert for investigation

sla_definitions.transaction: View product reviews and rating summary
sla_definitions.sla_statement: 98% of review page loads shall complete within 1500ms (p95). Review moderation is asynchronous (not in response path). Helpful votes (upvote/downvote) shall complete within 500ms (p95).
sla_definitions.measurement: RUM - track review section load time
sla_definitions.alerting: If review load time degrades, investigate database query performance

sla_definitions.transaction: Load homepage with featured products, promotions, news
sla_definitions.sla_statement: 98% of homepage loads shall complete within 2000ms (p95). Homepage should be fully cached for 1 hour, with cache invalidation on content changes.

98% of database queries shall execute within 150ms (p95) including network latency and result serialization. Queries exceeding 500ms (p99) shall be logged as slow queries and investigated. Connection pool size: min 10, max 50 connections; timeout 30 seconds.
sla_definitions.measurement: APM - database query metrics (query time, count, slow query log)
sla_definitions.alerting: If p95 query time > 200ms or slow query rate > 1%, investigate and optimize

sla_definitions.transaction: Calls to external APIs (payment, shipping, tax, address validation)
sla_definitions.sla_statement: External API calls shall have per-API timeouts: Address validation 2000ms, Tax calculation 1500ms, Shipping rates 2000ms, Payment gateway 5000ms. If API timeout, system SHALL fallback to cached values (address/shipping/tax) or 'Manual Review' (payment). Circuit breaker: if API fails > 5 times in 10 minutes, suspend API calls for 5 minutes and use fallback automatically.
sla_definitions.measurement: APM - track external API latency, timeouts, error rates
sla_definitions.alerting: If external API latency > timeout, check API health. If error rate > 10%, activate circuit breaker

sla_definitions.sla_statement: System SHALL support minimum 5000 concurrent users (peak load) with p95 latency not exceeding 3000ms. Average capacity: 1000 concurrent users with p95 latency <= 2200ms. Sustained load test: system SHALL maintain SLA for 4 hours continuous 1000-concurrent-user load. Monthly load testing required.
sla_definitions.measurement: Load test monitoring - measure latency and error rates under load
sla_definitions.alerting: If concurrent users approaching capacity, auto-scale infrastructure. If latency degradation during load test, investigate bottleneck

phase_1_immediate: NFR-LAT-001: Homepage and product page load time (2.2s p95)
phase_2_high: NFR-LAT-009: External API integration and caching
NFR-LAT-012: Caching strategy (CDN + Redis + browser cache)
phase_3_medium: NFR-LAT-013: Asynchronous processing for long-running operations
NFR-LAT-015: Capacity planning and load testing
phase_4_ongoing: NFR-LAT-014: Real User Monitoring (RUM) and alerting

performance_budgets.analyst_notes: This comprehensive latency analysis addresses the critical DeFOSPAM finding: 153+ requirements with ZERO performance specifications. Every feature now has response time budgets with component-level allocations, realistic SLA definitions, and actionable recommendations. Budgets account for external dependencies (payment gateways, shipping APIs, address validation) with timeout strategies and fallbacks. Mobile optimization and global reach (multi-currency, multi-language) reflected in separate budget tiers for 4G/3G networks. Database and caching strategies are foundational to meeting all latency targets.

metadata.focus: Uptime targets, failover strategies, RTO/RPO, disaster recovery

enterprise_class_services.description: Services directly impacting customer transactions and revenue

services.reason: Checkout is the direct revenue path. Lost checkout availability = lost sales. e-commerce industry standard is 99.99%. Every minute of downtime directly impacts revenue.

Payment processing must be near-perfect. PCI-DSS compliance requires robust error handling and uptime. DeFOSPAM flagged: Payment Gateway Decline/Failure Recovery, Invalid Payment Gateway Response, Refund Processing - all critical availability gaps.

Order confirmation is the commitment point. Customer must receive order confirmation email or UI message. Race condition at order confirmation can cause customer uncertainty and support escalations. DeFOSPAM flagged race condition in stock validation.
Product catalog is essential for browsing and discovery but less time-critical than checkout. Downtime prevents customer discovery but does not immediately lose completed transactions.
Authentication outage prevents returning customers from accessing their accounts but guests can still browse and checkout. Guest checkout (REQ086) provides fallback.
Shopping cart persistence is important for user experience but guests can still checkout (REQ086). Cart loss for authenticated users degrades experience but alternative is manual re-selection.

secondary_services.description: Services important for customer experience but not directly impacting revenue

services.reason: Wishlist is convenience feature for discovery and future purchases. Downtime does not prevent checkout or immediate purchase.
Reviews influence purchase decisions but catalog and product info remain available if review service fails. Graceful degradation possible.
Informational content (news, blog, terms of service) is important but not transactional. Downtime does not prevent commerce.
Support communication is important for customer satisfaction but not blocking commerce. Issues can be queued and resolved post-recovery.

rto_definitions.description: Recovery Time Objective: How quickly service must be restored after failure

by_service_tier.justification: Payment processing and checkout cannot remain down. 5 minutes = ~1-2 customer transactions impacted. Requires automated failover.
Catalog and authentication downtime degrades experience but doesn't immediately lose transactions. 15 minutes = ~10-20 customers impacted. Can tolerate brief manual intervention.
Non-critical services. Downtime is noticeable but not catastrophic. 1 hour RTO allows planned maintenance and controlled failover.

rpo_definitions.description: Recovery Point Objective: How much data loss is acceptable after failure

by_data_type.justification: Customer is actively in checkout. Lost session = lost sale. Requires real-time replication or synchronous writes to secondary database. DeFOSPAM flagged race conditions at order confirmation - must ensure atomic writes.
by_data_type.technical_approach: Synchronous database replication, distributed transactions, or event-driven consistency

by_data_type.justification: Order records are financial transactions. Loss = audit failure, compliance violation, and customer disputes. Must be immediately consistent across replicas.
by_data_type.technical_approach: Multi-region replication, write-ahead logging, transaction journals

by_data_type.max_data_loss: Up to 1 minute of inventory changes
by_data_type.justification: DeFOSPAM flagged Out-of-Stock Detection During Checkout and race conditions. RPO of 1 minute acceptable IF in-flight checkout validates against real-time inventory. Stock data must sync frequently.
by_data_type.technical_approach: Event-driven inventory updates, cache invalidation on stock changes, RPO=1min allows async batch syncs

by_data_type.max_data_loss: Up to 5 minutes of new registrations/profile changes
by_data_type.justification: Customer data is important but not immediately transactional. New registrations can be re-entered by customer. Profile updates can be replayed.
by_data_type.technical_approach: Async replication with 5-minute SLA, periodic consistency checks

by_data_type.max_data_loss: Up to 5 minutes of browsing/cart changes
by_data_type.justification: Session data is ephemeral. Loss causes inconvenience (customer must re-add items) but no data loss for completed orders. Acceptable for non-critical service.
by_data_type.technical_approach: In-memory cache with periodic persistence, async writes acceptable

by_data_type.max_data_loss: Up to 15 minutes of new reviews/wishlist additions
by_data_type.justification: Non-critical data. Loss is minor inconvenience (user re-submits review or re-adds to wishlist).

by_data_type.max_data_loss: Up to 1 hour of content edits
by_data_type.justification: Informational content. Minor edits can be replayed post-recovery.
by_data_type.technical_approach: Async replication, version control, rollback capability

failover_strategies.overall_strategy: Hybrid multi-tier approach: Revenue-critical services use active-active with automated failover; secondary services use active-passive with manual intervention possible

strategies_by_tier.description: Both regions process traffic simultaneously. No single point of failure. Both data centers replicate writes synchronously.
mechanism: DNS-based geographic routing (GeoDNS) or intelligent load balancer detects regional failures
Customers route to healthy region automatically
Database replication ensures both regions have identical order data
Payment gateway integration with backup provider (primary + fallback)
challenges: Requires 2+ data centers in different geographic regions
Synchronous replication adds latency (must be minimized via WAN optimization)
strategies_by_tier.recovery_approach: Automatic. Traffic routed to healthy region within 30-60 seconds of health check failure

strategies_by_tier.description: Primary data center handles all traffic. Secondary data center is standby replica. On primary failure, traffic automatically shifts to secondary.
strategies_by_tier.rto: 15 minutes (automated detection + traffic shift, secondary may need brief warm-up)
mechanism: Load balancer or DNS automatically routes traffic to secondary
Secondary database is real-time replica of primary
Session state persisted to shared storage or database (not in-memory only)
challenges: Requires automated health monitoring and failover orchestration
Secondary data center must be fully provisioned (standing cost)
Brief moment of service degradation during failover (15-30 seconds)
strategies_by_tier.recovery_approach: Automated with ~15 minute RTO. Secondary promoted to primary. Investigate primary failure asynchronously. Failback once primary is healthy.

strategies_by_tier.description: Two identical production environments (Blue=active, Green=standby). New versions deployed to Green, then traffic switched with zero downtime. Used for planned maintenance and updates.
strategies_by_tier.rto: 60 minutes (scheduled maintenance window or manual failover)
mechanism: Deploy updates to inactive environment (Green) while Blue serves traffic
Switch router/load balancer to Green (traffic reroutes instantly)
If issues detected, switch back to Blue
challenges: Requires database migration planning (schema changes must be backwards-compatible)
Not suitable for unplanned failures (designed for planned updates)
strategies_by_tier.recovery_approach: For planned maintenance: deploy to Green, test, switch, monitor. If issues: rollback to Blue. For unplanned failures: use active-passive backup to secondary data center.

canary_deployment.description: Route 5-10% of traffic to new version while 90-95% uses stable version. Monitor error rate and latency. If healthy, gradually increase traffic.
not_applicable_to: Security patches (must be immediate and full)
canary_deployment.rto: 5 minutes (can revert canary traffic immediately if issues detected)

single_points_of_failure.description: Architecture components that, if they fail, cause service outage. Must be eliminated or mitigated with redundancy.

identified_spofs.impact: All services depend on database. Database failure = total system failure.
identified_spofs.current_state: Not specified in requirements. Likely single instance.
identified_spofs.mitigation: Implement database replication (primary-secondary or multi-master). Use managed database services (AWS RDS, Azure SQL) with automatic failover. Deploy to 2+ availability zones minimum.

identified_spofs.impact: DeFOSPAM flagged: Missing Payment Gateway Decline/Failure Recovery. If single gateway fails, no payments can be processed.
identified_spofs.current_state: Requirements mention payment processing (REQ090-091) but no fallback or backup gateway specified.
identified_spofs.mitigation: Integrate 2+ payment gateway providers. Detect gateway failures and automatically route to backup. Implement retry logic with exponential backoff. Queue payments during gateway outages for later processing.

identified_spofs.impact: DeFOSPAM flagged missing outcomes: Payment Failure Recovery, Customer Registration Confirmation, Password Reset Email. If email service fails, customers don't receive confirmations.
identified_spofs.current_state: Requirements require email notifications but no backup or queuing specified.
identified_spofs.mitigation: Implement email queuing (async job queue). If primary email service fails, queue emails and retry with backup provider. Monitor email delivery rates. Provide in-app notifications as backup.
identified_spofs.required_rto: Email queue processes within 1 hour; customer can view order in account
identified_spofs.implementation: Email queue + async workers + backup email provider

identified_spofs.impact: If session data stored only in single server memory, server failure = all customer sessions lost. Customers forced to re-login.
identified_spofs.current_state: Not specified. Likely single-server in-memory sessions.
identified_spofs.mitigation: Persist session data to distributed cache (Redis cluster) or database. Use sticky sessions with health-aware routing, but ensure graceful degradation if session lost.

identified_spofs.impact: DeFOSPAM flagged: Out-of-Stock Detection During Checkout and Race Condition in Stock Validation. If inventory data is unreliable or unavailable, customers can order out-of-stock items (overselling) or checkout fails.
identified_spofs.current_state: Race condition in stock validation at order confirmation suggests no distributed lock mechanism.
identified_spofs.mitigation: Implement optimistic locking or distributed locks (using database row locks or Redis locks) to prevent race conditions. Real-time inventory sync. Asynchronous inventory deduction with compensation (retry on failure).
identified_spofs.implementation: Distributed locking mechanism + real-time inventory sync

identified_spofs.impact: DeFOSPAM flagged: Missing Refund Processing Workflow. No specification means no disaster recovery for refund processing. If refund system fails, customer refunds are lost or delayed.
identified_spofs.current_state: No refund processing workflow specified in requirements.
identified_spofs.mitigation: Implement refund processing as asynchronous workflow with persistent state machine. Implement compensating transactions: if refund fails, retry logic must exist. Maintain refund audit log. Design for idempotency (retrying refund doesn't double-refund).
identified_spofs.required_rto: 24 hours (refund is non-blocking but must be reliable)
identified_spofs.implementation: Durable refund processing service with saga pattern

identified_spofs.impact: DeFOSPAM flagged: Unpredictable Tax Calculation Timing and Order Total Accuracy. If tax calculation fails or is slow, order total is incorrect and checkout fails.
identified_spofs.current_state: No error handling or timeout specified for tax calculations.
identified_spofs.mitigation: Cache tax rates locally with periodic refresh. Implement timeout (max wait 5 seconds). Fallback to last-known rate if service unavailable. Implement circuit breaker to detect failures.
identified_spofs.implementation: Tax service with circuit breaker + local cache + fallback

identified_spofs.impact: DeFOSPAM flagged: Missing Error Handling for Invalid Payment Gateway Response. If gateway returns malformed response, system cannot determine payment success/failure.
identified_spofs.current_state: No error handling specified for invalid gateway responses.
identified_spofs.mitigation: Implement robust error handling: validate all gateway responses against schema. Timeout after 30 seconds (as noted in DeFOSPAM). If response invalid or timeout, query gateway for order status before retrying. Implement retry logic with exponential backoff.
identified_spofs.implementation: Response validation + status query service + retry orchestration

identified_spofs.impact: Single load balancer failure = traffic cannot reach application tier.
identified_spofs.current_state: Not specified. Likely single load balancer.
identified_spofs.mitigation: Deploy load balancer in high-availability pair (active-active or active-passive). Use health checks to detect failures. Implement DNS failover as backup.
identified_spofs.implementation: HA load balancer pair (e.g., AWS ALB with multi-AZ, F5, HAProxy)

identified_spofs.impact: DNS failure = customers cannot reach site.
identified_spofs.current_state: Not specified. Likely uses single DNS provider.
identified_spofs.mitigation: Use managed DNS service with automatic failover. Implement GeoDNS for region-aware routing. Use multiple DNS providers (CNAME fallback). Set appropriate TTLs (60-300 seconds for critical changes).
identified_spofs.implementation: Managed DNS (Route 53, Cloudflare, NS1) with failover

external_dependencies.description: Third-party services and their availability impact on nopCommerce

dependencies.failure_mode: Payment processing fails. DeFOSPAM flagged: Missing Payment Gateway Decline/Failure Recovery.
dependencies.mitigation: Multi-provider strategy. Implement fallback to 2nd provider if 1st fails. Queue payments for retry.

dependencies.failure_mode: Order confirmation, registration confirmation, password reset emails fail. DeFOSPAM flagged missing outcomes.
dependencies.mitigation: Async email queue with retry. Backup email provider. In-app notifications as fallback.

dependencies.failure_mode: Cannot calculate shipping rates or validate addresses during checkout. Checkout may fail or timeout.
dependencies.mitigation: Cache shipping rates locally with refresh schedule. Implement timeout (max 5 seconds). Fallback to standard rates if provider unavailable.

dependencies.failure_mode: Cannot calculate tax, order total incorrect. DeFOSPAM flagged: Unpredictable Tax Calculation Timing.
dependencies.mitigation: Cache tax rates locally. Implement timeout (5 seconds). Fallback to last-known rates. Circuit breaker pattern.

dependencies.failure_mode: Cannot validate shipping address. Checkout may warn user but allow proceed.
dependencies.mitigation: Make validation optional or async. Cache common addresses. Implement timeout (5 seconds).

dependencies.failure_mode: Cannot detect fraud. Either block all transactions (fail-safe) or allow all (risky). DeFOSPAM flagged security gaps.
dependencies.mitigation: Implement circuit breaker. Failover behavior: if fraud service unavailable, use conservative rules or allow with additional verification.

dependencies.failure_mode: Product images load slowly. Site remains functional but UX degraded.
dependencies.mitigation: Fallback to origin server for images if CDN fails. Implement browser caching.

dependencies.failure_mode: Cannot track customer behavior or system metrics. Does not block transactions.
dependencies.mitigation: Non-blocking calls. Queue analytics events asynchronously. Graceful degradation if service unavailable.

frequency.order_database: Continuous (real-time replication) + Daily full backup + Hourly incremental backup
frequency.product_catalog: Daily full backup + Real-time replication to secondary region
frequency.session_data: No backup needed (ephemeral, rebuilt on login)

scenario_1.title: Single Server Failure
scenario_1.trigger: One application server fails or becomes unresponsive
recovery_procedure: Load balancer detects server health check failure
Load balancer removes server from rotation (automatic)
In-flight requests to failed server may timeout (retry from client)
No data loss (databases remain healthy)
Service continues with reduced capacity
scenario_1.action_needed: Manual: restart failed server or replace instance

scenario_2.title: Database Primary Failure
recovery_procedure: Automated failover mechanism detects primary failure
Secondary database promoted to primary (automatic)
Connection pool redirects to new primary
Applications reconnect automatically
Minimal data loss (RPO depends on replication lag)
scenario_2.action_needed: Manual: investigate root cause, plan failback to restored primary

scenario_3.title: Payment Gateway Failure
scenario_3.trigger: Primary payment gateway (Stripe, PayPal, etc.) becomes unavailable
recovery_procedure: Payment service detects gateway errors
Automatically routes to backup payment gateway (active-passive)
Customer checkout continues with backup gateway
If backup also unavailable, queue payment for later processing
Customer receives notification: 'payment queued, will process within 1 hour'
scenario_3.action_needed: Monitoring: track queued payments, retry once gateway recovers

scenario_4.title: Data Center Failure (Region Outage)
scenario_4.trigger: Entire primary data center becomes unavailable (power, network, disaster)
recovery_procedure: Traffic routing system detects all servers in region failing
DNS/GeoDNS automatically routes traffic to secondary region (active-active)
Secondary region takes over all traffic (if active-active configured)
OR: Load balancer fails over to secondary region (if active-passive)
Databases in secondary region become live for reads and writes
Minimal data loss if synchronous replication (RTO 5 min, RPO <1 sec)
scenario_4.action_needed: Declare disaster, activate secondary region as primary, notify stakeholders, plan restoration of primary region

scenario_5.title: Database Data Corruption
scenario_5.trigger: Corrupted data detected (bug introduces bad data, malware modifies data)
recovery_procedure: STOP replication immediately (prevent corruption spreading to secondary)
Restore from last known good backup
Replay transaction logs from backup point to current time
Verify data integrity before resuming service
Manual DBA intervention required
scenario_5.rto: 1-4 hours (depends on backup size and replay speed)
scenario_5.rpo: Time from last backup (hourly backup = max 1 hour loss)
scenario_5.action_needed: Manual: DBA leads recovery, validates data integrity, communicates with stakeholders

scenario_6.title: Network Partition (Split-Brain)
scenario_6.trigger: Network connection between primary and secondary regions fails (still healthy locally)
recovery_procedure: Both regions detect loss of replication connection
PRIMARY region (larger data center) continues accepting writes
SECONDARY region enters read-only mode (prevents split-brain)
When network heals, secondary catches up from primary
Automatic re-sync of replicated data
scenario_6.action_needed: Monitoring: detect network partition, verify both regions reachable from outside, plan WAN repair

scheduled_maintenance.policy: Maintenance windows must be scheduled outside peak hours and announced in advance
allowed_services_down: Never take down: Checkout, Payment Processing, Order Management
scheduled_maintenance.communication: Announce 7 days before. Show banner on site 24 hours before. Post-maintenance verification required before marking complete.

critical_services_maintenance.reason: 99.9% target allows 8.76 hours unplanned downtime. Must reserve some for planned maintenance.
critical_services_maintenance.strategy: Use blue-green deployments for zero-downtime updates. Only critical patches during maintenance window.

high_value_services_maintenance.reason: 99.95% target allows 4.38 hours unplanned. Can use more planned maintenance with blue-green.
high_value_services_maintenance.strategy: Blue-green deployments for updates. Database migrations during scheduled windows.

secondary_services_maintenance.reason: 99.9% target allows 8.76 hours unplanned. Can conduct more invasive maintenance.
secondary_services_maintenance.strategy: Take services offline for maintenance if needed. Announce in advance.

executive_summary.current_infrastructure: 4 web, 6 app, 2 DB servers (112 vCPU, 272 GB RAM, 2.5 TB storage)
critical_gaps: No horizontal scaling strategy for database tier
Session management under scale not defined
Race conditions in stock validation at peak load
executive_summary.key_finding: With ZERO scalability specifications in 153 functional requirements, nopCommerce platform faces undefined breaking points. Peak load (2,500 users, 8,000 tx/hr) approaches current capacity; 5,000 user target requires fundamental scaling redesign.

scaling_requirements.title: Horizontal Scaling Strategy for Web Tier

scalability.auto_scale_trigger: CPU > 70%, Request latency > 500ms, Request queue depth > 50
scalability.bottleneck_component: Web server reverse proxy, SSL/TLS termination

scaling_requirements.reasoning: Web tier is stateless (no session affinity required). Current 4 servers handle 2,500 peak users; scaling to 5,000 users requires 8+ web servers. Each server handles ~300-400 concurrent connections. Standard HTTP load balancing (round-robin, least-conn) sufficient.
scaling_requirements.recommendation: Implement auto-scaling group: baseline 4 servers, scale to 8 servers at 70% CPU, scale down at <20% CPU with 5-min stability check. Use health checks (HTTP GET / response <200ms). Consider Kubernetes StatefulSet for container-based deployment with HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler). Implement sticky sessions (session affinity) only if required by application state; prefer stateless design.

scaling_requirements.title: Horizontal Scaling Strategy for App Tier

scalability.auto_scale_trigger: CPU > 75%, Memory > 80%, Database connection pool utilization > 85%
scalability.bottleneck_component: Database connection pool, thread pool exhaustion

scaling_requirements.reasoning: App tier (6 servers baseline) handles business logic: cart management, order processing, payment gateway integration, inventory validation. Each server pools 20 DB connections (120 total). At peak (8,000 tx/hr = 2.2 tx/sec per app server), thread pool (600 total) approaches saturation. Scaling to 5,000 users requires 10-12 app servers.
scaling_requirements.recommendation: Auto-scale app tier: baseline 6 servers, scale to 12 servers at 75% CPU or 85% connection pool. Monitor thread pool queue length; alert if queue > 100 requests. Implement distributed request queuing (Redis pub/sub or RabbitMQ) to decouple order submission from processing. Use circuit breaker pattern for payment gateway calls to prevent cascading failures.

load_model.2x_users_1000_concurrent: 6 app servers sufficient; tx/sec ~1.1, pool util ~45%

scaling_requirements.critical_note: Database connection pool is the hard bottleneck. With 200-connection pool cap and 120 allocated to app tier, only 50-80 connections remain for spikes. Must implement connection pooling middleware (PgBouncer, Proxysql) to allow dynamic pool scaling.

scaling_requirements.title: Database Scaling Strategy: Read Replicas and Sharding

scalability.auto_scale_trigger: Primary disk I/O > 80%, Query latency p95 > 2000ms, Replication lag > 5 seconds
scalability.bottleneck_component: Primary database write throughput, storage growth rate

scaling_requirements.reasoning: Current: 2-server setup (primary + read replica). Peak load generates ~8,000 tx/hr = 2.2 writes/sec. With 1,200 avg tx/hr = 0.33 writes/sec, primary has capacity headroom. However, growth projection (120 GB at 12mo, 350 GB at 24mo) shows 3x storage growth annually. Read-heavy queries (product search, order history) can scale horizontally; writes must go to primary.
scaling_requirements.recommendation: Multi-tiered DB strategy: (1) PRIMARY: Scale vertically to 24-32 vCPU, 256+ GB RAM, NVMe storage, 10 Gbps network. (2) READ REPLICAS: Deploy 2-3 async replicas for read-heavy features (product catalog, order history, reviews). (3) SHARDING (future): If writes exceed primary capacity (>10 tx/sec), implement order sharding by customer_id or date range to partition write load. (4) MONITORING: Track primary disk usage, replication lag, query performance. Set alerts: replication lag > 5s, primary disk > 80%, slow queries > 2s.

read_write_split.writes: Primary only (orders, inventory updates, customer changes)
read_write_split.transaction_consistency: Use read-after-write consistency for critical paths (checkout, payment)

storage_capacity_planning.projected_12mo_120gb: 3.3x growth; upgrade primary to 1 TB NVMe + 500 GB replica
storage_capacity_planning.projected_24mo_350gb: If sharding not implemented, primary needs 500 GB+ (expensive). Shard orders by date/customer by month 18 to cap primary size.

scaling_requirements.title: Cache Layer Scaling and Elasticity
scaling_requirements.feature: Session Management, Product Search, Shopping Cart

scalability.auto_scale_trigger: Cache hit rate < 80%, Eviction rate > 5%, Memory utilization > 85%

scaling_requirements.reasoning: Baseline: 2 Redis servers, 64 GB total cache. At 2,500 peak concurrent users with 30-min session timeout, ~2,500 active sessions (64 MB worst-case) + shopping carts (8 MB per 200 carts) + product catalog cache (8-10 GB) + rate limits/locks = ~12-15 GB required. At 5,000 users, cache demand approaches 30+ GB, requiring 48+ GB infrastructure.
scaling_requirements.recommendation: Redis scaling: (1) Baseline: 2 servers, 64 GB total, master-slave replication. (2) Cluster mode: Deploy Redis Cluster (3 masters + 3 slaves, 64 GB per shard = 192 GB total) to horizontally shard cache by key prefix. (3) Auto-eviction: LRU with TTL; session TTL 30 min, product cache TTL 12 hrs, cart TTL 7 days. (4) Monitoring: Hit rate dashboard, eviction alerts, memory pressure alerts. (5) Failover: Use Redis Sentinel for automatic failover if replica detects primary failure.
scaling_requirements.cache_warming_strategy: Pre-load product catalog on startup; warm top-N products on cache miss with 2-tier fetch (cache, then DB with replication)

scaling_requirements.title: Session Management Under Scale: Distributed Session Store

scalability.auto_scale_trigger: Session lookup latency > 100ms, Session eviction rate > 2%

scaling_requirements.reasoning: Current baseline: Sessions stored in Redis (2 servers). Peak concurrent sessions: 2,500 (avg), 5,000 (target). Session size: ~1-2 KB (user ID, cart contents, auth token). At 5,000 sessions * 1.5 KB * 30-min timeout = 7.5 GB minimum. Web tier currently 4 servers; if sessions stored on local memory (not recommended), server failure = session loss. Redis is correct choice.
scaling_requirements.recommendation: Session architecture: (1) All web/app tier servers delegate sessions to Redis (no local session storage). (2) Session format: JSON serialized, not binary (easier debugging). (3) Session TTL: 30 min idle (extend on each request), absolute max 8 hrs. (4) Session replication: Redis replication set (master-slave) with automatic failover (Sentinel). (5) Session stickiness: Disable cookie-based sticky sessions; instead, store session ID in cookie, lookup in Redis. This allows any web server to serve any request. (6) Monitoring: Session count, lookup latency (target < 50ms p95), eviction rate.
scaling_requirements.session_resilience: If Redis fails: Session loss is acceptable for short outage (<5 min); user must re-login. Implement graceful degradation: deny new sessions during Redis outage, allow in-flight requests to complete.

scaling_requirements.title: Load Balancing Strategy per Tier

scalability.auto_scale_trigger: LB CPU > 80%, LB connection count > 100k concurrent

scaling_requirements.reasoning: Current: 1 load balancer (bottleneck at 2,500+ concurrent users). Single LB can handle ~200k concurrent connections, sufficient for 5,000 users (~25k connections). However, single LB is SPOF (Single Point of Failure). At peak, LB processes 8,000 tx/hr = 2.2 req/sec (low for LB), but SSL/TLS termination adds CPU load.
scaling_requirements.recommendation: LB architecture: (1) Primary + Secondary LB in active-passive HA mode (IP failover via heartbeat protocol). (2) Web tier: Layer 7 LB (application-aware) using round-robin or least-conn algorithm. Health checks: HTTP GET / every 10s, mark dead after 3 failures. (3) App tier: Layer 4 LB (TCP round-robin) with connection affinity for long-lived connections. (4) Database tier: No LB needed; app servers connect directly to primary (reads) and replicas (writes). (5) SSL/TLS termination: LB terminates SSL (offload CPU from web servers); reuses connection to backend pool.
scaling_requirements.geographic_distribution_note: For true HA across data centers, implement DNS failover (weighted round-robin by DC) or active-active load balancing across DCs with database replication.

scaling_requirements.title: Bottleneck Analysis: Database Connection Pool Exhaustion
scaling_requirements.feature: Shopping Cart, Order Management, Inventory Validation

scalability.bottleneck_component: Database connection pool (200 total, 120 app tier, 50 web, 30 admin)

scaling_requirements.reasoning: Critical bottleneck: Current pool size 200 is insufficient for 5,000 concurrent users. At peak, 6 app servers share 120 connections = 20 conn/server. If app server has 600 threads, only 20 can execute DB queries concurrently; 580 threads queue waiting for a connection. Queue wait time = latency spike (500ms+). Peak load 8,000 tx/hr requires <10 ms query execution; if queries take 50 ms, max throughput = (120 connections / 0.050 sec) = 2,400 tx/hr (only 30% of peak demand).
scaling_requirements.recommendation: Connection pool redesign: (1) Implement PgBouncer or Proxysql middleware on each app server (local connection pooling). (2) App server -> PgBouncer: unlimited connections, reuse DB connections from pool. PgBouncer handles session/transaction mode multiplexing. (3) PgBouncer -> Primary DB: use 40-50 total persistent DB connections across all PgBouncers. (4) Configuration: connection_max_age 600s (idle 10 min), wait_timeout 30s (abort if wait > 30s). (5) Monitoring: track connection pool utilization, queue depth, rejected connections. Alert if queue depth > 50 or wait time > 1 second.
scaling_requirements.immediate_action: Increase pool size from 200 to 300-400 (before scaling to 5,000 users). Monitor queue depth; if queue > 20 at current load, pool is undersized.

scaling_requirements.title: Bottleneck Analysis: Race Condition in Stock Validation
scaling_requirements.feature: Shopping Cart, Order Confirmation, Inventory Management

scalability.bottleneck_component: Inventory table row-level locking, concurrent update serialization

scaling_requirements.reasoning: DeFOSPAM flagged race condition in stock validation: 'Multiple customers simultaneously add the same limited-stock product to cart and reach order confirmation. Between cart add validation and order confirmation, stock depletes (stock can go negative).' At peak, 2,500+ concurrent users all querying/updating same product rows. Without row locking, race condition causes: (1) Overselling (sell more units than in stock), (2) Negative inventory, (3) Unfulfilled orders.
scaling_requirements.recommendation: Stock validation architecture: (1) Pessimistic locking: SELECT * FROM products WHERE id=X FOR UPDATE (lock row during read). Holds lock for minimal duration (query execution only). (2) Stock reservation: When item added to cart, decrement inventory immediately + log reservation (not at confirmation). Unlock if customer abandons cart (after 30 min). (3) Validation at confirmation: Check if reserved qty still valid; if stock depletion occurred (other customer completed), offer backorder or cancel. (4) Monitoring: Track inventory lock contention, failed validations, backorder rate. Alert if lock waits > 100 ms or failed validations > 1% of checkouts.
scaling_requirements.breaking_point: Without row locking, system oversells when concurrent checkouts exceed (inventory qty / average checkout duration). If product has 10 units and avg checkout takes 5 minutes, system can safely handle 2 concurrent checkouts; 3+ concurrent = risk of overselling.

scaling_requirements.title: Bottleneck Analysis: Payment Gateway Failure Cascade

scalability.bottleneck_component: Payment gateway API latency, timeout handling, thread pool queue

scaling_requirements.reasoning: DeFOSPAM flagged: 'Payment gateway failures' without mitigation. Current requirements silent on payment failure handling. At peak, 2.2 orders/sec = 2.2 gateway API calls/sec. If gateway latency increases from 100 ms to 5 sec (degradation), checkout threads block for 5 sec. App tier has 600 threads; if 2.2 orders/sec each take 5 sec, max concurrent = 11 threads occupied, leaving 589 free (no issue). BUT if gateway is FULLY DOWN, all payment requests fail -> order queue backs up -> checkout latency spikes -> customers abandon carts -> lost revenue.
scaling_requirements.recommendation: Payment resilience strategy: (1) Timeout: Set payment gateway timeout to 30 sec (fail fast). (2) Circuit breaker: If >5% of payment requests fail in 1 min window, trip circuit breaker, return error to customer with 'Try again later' (do NOT retry automatically). Breaker resets after 5 min. (3) Async processing: Enqueue payment request to Redis queue; worker thread processes async; return 'Payment pending' to customer. (4) Retry logic: Implement idempotency key in payment API call (same key + amount = deduplicate retries). (5) Fallback: If queue depth > 1000 pending payments, queue new requests with delay notification: 'Our payment system is busy; we'll process your payment in ~5 minutes.'
scaling_requirements.breaking_point: Gateway failure = system cascades if no circuit breaker. 100 concurrent customers waiting for payment response = 100 threads blocked = checkout flow stalled for all users until gateway recovers.

scaling_requirements.title: Bottleneck Analysis: Thread Pool Exhaustion Under Peak Load

scalability.bottleneck_component: App server thread pools (600 per server, 3,600 total across 6 servers)

scaling_requirements.reasoning: App servers configured with 600 worker threads per server (3,600 total). At peak load 2,500 concurrent users, avg request takes 200 ms (time in server), max concurrent requests = 2,500 * (200 ms / 1000 ms) = 500. Thread pool capacity is 3,600, so utilization = 500/3,600 = 14% (headroom). However, if: (1) requests slow to 500 ms (due to DB lock contention), max concurrent = 2,500 * 0.5 = 1,250 threads, utilization = 35%. (2) database connection pool exhausted, requests queue waiting for connection, thread holds for full request duration. If 50 threads are all blocked on connection pool (waiting > 1 sec), they prevent other requests from executing.
scaling_requirements.recommendation: Thread pool monitoring: (1) Track active thread count, queue depth, rejection rate. Alert if active threads > 80% of pool size. (2) Configure rejection policy: ThreadPoolExecutor.AbortPolicy (fail fast) vs. CallerRunsPolicy (caller blocks). Prefer AbortPolicy (fail fast) to prevent thread saturation. (3) Separate thread pools by workload: (a) Request I/O threads (600) for HTTP requests, (b) Background processing threads (50) for async order processing, (c) Scheduled task threads (20) for cache refresh, inventory sync. (4) Increase pool size to 800 per server when scaling to 5,000 users (allowing 1,000+ concurrent requests).

scaling_requirements.title: Breaking Point Analysis: Theoretical Max Throughput per Component
scaling_requirements.reasoning: Define max theoretical throughput before system fails completely:

breaking_points_by_component.theoretical_max: 10 tx/sec (database can sustain ~10 writes/sec on current hardware before index contention degrades performance to unacceptable levels)
breaking_points_by_component.scaling_at_max: If 10 tx/sec < required, shard by order_id or date range to partition write load across multiple databases

breaking_points_by_component.current_capacity: 2.2 queries/sec * avg query time 50ms = 110 max concurrent queries; pool size 120 is barely sufficient
breaking_points_by_component.theoretical_max: 240 queries/sec (if queries execute in 20ms avg, 120 connections * 1000ms/20ms = 6,000 queries/sec; but realistic is 240 with 50ms queries)
breaking_points_by_component.scaling_at_max: Deploy PgBouncer to increase multiplexing; increase pool size to 300

breaking_points_by_component.current_capacity: 4 web servers * 25 req/sec per server = 100 req/sec
breaking_points_by_component.theoretical_max: 200 req/sec (web servers mainly reverse proxy; not CPU-bound until ~25 req/sec per server for HTTPS); can sustain 8 web servers * 25 = 200 req/sec
breaking_points_by_component.scaling_at_max: Add web servers; scale to 8-10 at peak

breaking_points_by_component.current_capacity: 6 app servers * 0.37 tx/sec per server = 2.2 tx/sec
breaking_points_by_component.theoretical_max: 4.4 tx/sec per tier (if orders take 1.5 sec each on app server, 600 threads / 1.5 = 400 max concurrent, with 6 servers = 2,400 concurrent, = 2.2 tx/sec at 2,500 users already near max)
breaking_points_by_component.scaling_at_max: App tier is BOTTLENECK. Each transaction spends 1.5 sec on app tier (payment gateway, inventory check, order creation). Need 12+ app servers at peak

breaking_points_by_component.current_capacity: Unknown (vendor-dependent); assume 10 tx/sec per merchant account
breaking_points_by_component.theoretical_max: 10 tx/sec sustained (payment processors have rate limits per merchant, typically 5-20 tx/sec)
breaking_points_by_component.scaling_at_max: Contact payment processor for higher rate limits; implement circuit breaker + retry queue to handle gateway degradation gracefully
Deploy Redis Cluster (3 shards); monitor eviction rate; ensure cache hit ratio > 95% for cart/session

scaling_requirements.overall_system_breaking_point: At 5,000 concurrent users, system breaking point is APP TIER + DATABASE CONNECTION POOL. Currently at 2,500 users, app tier is at 65-75% utilization; 5,000 users = 150% utilization (overloaded). Must scale app tier from 6 to 12 servers BEFORE targeting 5,000 concurrent users.

scaling_requirements.title: Elasticity Gap: Auto-Scaling Triggers and Policies Not Specified
scaling_requirements.reasoning: No scalability requirements specify when/how to scale. Without auto-scaling policies, peak load management requires manual intervention (deploy new servers by hand). At 2,500 -> 5,000 users, infrastructure cannot scale automatically.
scaling_requirements.recommendation: Define auto-scaling policies for EACH tier: (1) WEB TIER: Scale up trigger = CPU > 70% for 2 min OR req latency p95 > 500ms. Scale down = CPU < 30% for 5 min. Min 4 servers, max 10. (2) APP TIER: Scale up trigger = CPU > 75% for 2 min OR DB connection pool utilization > 85% OR queue depth > 50. Scale down = CPU < 30% for 5 min. Min 6 servers, max 16. (3) CACHE TIER: Scale up trigger = Memory utilization > 85% OR eviction rate > 3%. Min 2 shards, max 8 shards. (4) DATABASE: Scale up trigger = Primary disk > 80% OR replication lag > 5 sec OR slow query rate > 5%. MANUAL: Requires maintenance window to add replicas. (5) COOLDOWN PERIOD: 60 sec between scale-up events, 300 sec between scale-down events (prevent flapping).

scaling_implementation.policy: target CPU 70%, scale up at 70%, scale down at 30%, cooldown 60/300 sec

scaling_implementation.tool: Custom metrics (app-specific thread pool, queue depth)
scaling_implementation.policy: target queue depth < 20, scale up at queue > 50, scale down at queue < 5

scaling_implementation.tool: Manual scaling (or DBaaS auto-scaling if available)
scaling_implementation.policy: monitor replication lag; add replica if lag > 5 sec or disk > 80%

scaling_requirements.title: Geographic Distribution Planning: Multi-Region Deployment
scaling_requirements.reasoning: Single data center deployment creates: (1) Single region latency (users in Asia/Europe experience 150-300 ms latency from US DC), (2) Disaster risk (DC failure = total outage), (3) Data residency compliance (GDPR requires EU data in EU, not US). No requirements address geographic distribution, yet it's critical for: global e-commerce, legal compliance, user experience.
scaling_requirements.recommendation: Multi-region strategy (24-36 month roadmap): (1) PHASE 1 (now): Single region + CDN. Deploy CloudFront (or equivalent) for static assets (images, CSS, JS). Serves 60% of content from edge. Reduces origin load by 60%, improves user-perceived latency by 100-200 ms. (2) PHASE 2 (month 12): Add secondary region for HA. Primary US-East, secondary US-West (or EU-Central). Database replication: Primary in US-East, read replica in US-West. App tier active-active with DNS failover (latency-based routing to nearest region). (3) PHASE 3 (month 24): Multi-region active-active. 3+ regions (US, EU, APAC) with local databases. Implement conflict-free replication for non-critical data (product catalog, reviews) or accept eventual consistency.
scaling_requirements.caching_and_cdn: Implement CDN for: product images (12 GB data, 24 hour cache TTL), static HTML (homepage, category pages), API responses (category list, featured products, 1 hour cache). Reduces origin bandwidth by ~70%.

database_replication_strategy.phase_1: Primary in US-East, async replica in US-West. Disaster recovery RTO 15 min, RPO 1 min.
database_replication_strategy.phase_2: Primary in US-East, async replicas in US-West + EU. All regions read their local replica, writes go to primary (single-master).
database_replication_strategy.phase_3: Multi-master replication with conflict resolution (last-write-wins for product updates, vector clocks for order updates)

scaling_requirements.title: Load Model for 2x, 5x, 10x User Growth

infrastructure_changes: App tier: 7-8 servers (vs. 6), CPU ~75%, DB conn pool util ~75%
Database: 24 vCPU (vs. 16), 256 GB RAM, 200 GB storage
Cache: 80 GB Redis (vs. 64 GB), hit rate 90%
App tier: 14-16 servers, CPU ~75%, DB conn pool util 90%
Database SHARDING REQUIRED: Primary overloaded at 5+ tx/sec. Shard orders by customer_id hash; maintain inventory on primary only
Cache: 128 GB Redis Cluster (4 shards), hit rate > 95%
load_models.critical_note: 5x growth without sharding = system failure. Must implement sharding by month 12 to prepare.

infrastructure_changes: Database: MANDATORY sharding. 4 order shards (by customer_id), 1 inventory primary, geo-replicated catalog
Cache: 256 GB Redis Cluster (8 shards + 8 replicas)
Network: 750 Mbps egress, CDN must serve 80% of content
load_models.recommendation: 10x growth is outside scope of current platform. Recommend architectural redesign by month 18 to support 10x.

scaling_requirements.scaling_rule_of_thumb: Every 2x user growth requires: (1) Web tier +50%, (2) App tier +50-75%, (3) Database resources +30-50% (until sharding needed), (4) Cache +100%, (5) CDN serving 80% content.

cannot_scale_horizontally.reason: Single write authority required for transactional consistency; cannot replicate writes across multiple primaries without consensus overhead
cannot_scale_horizontally.scaling_strategy: Scale vertically (larger instance, faster storage) until write load exceeds ~10 tx/sec, then implement sharding by entity (orders shard by customer_id, inventory shard by product_id)

cannot_scale_horizontally.reason: If sessions stored in local memory on web servers, user affinity required (sticky sessions); request routing becomes complex
cannot_scale_horizontally.scaling_strategy: Move sessions to distributed Redis (no affinity needed); allows any web server to serve any user

cannot_scale_horizontally.reason: Single queue with strict ordering must enforce order; multiple queues add complexity (reordering, deduplication)
cannot_scale_horizontally.scaling_strategy: Use sharded queue (Redis streams, RabbitMQ partitions) to scale write throughput while maintaining per-shard ordering

security_performance.document_type: Security Performance Analysis and DoS Resilience Assessment

findings.title: TLS Termination Latency Overhead on Payment Authorization
findings.reasoning: Payment authorization in Noyce's budget is 3500ms p95. TLS handshake (session reuse) adds ~45ms for HTTPS termination at load balancer level, plus ~25ms for certificate validation (OCSP stapling). Total TLS overhead: ~70ms. At peak load (100+ concurrent payment requests), TLS becomes a contention point if not properly tuned. Session resumption reduces this but adds certificate validation latency per unique client.
findings.recommendation: Implement: (1) TLS session resumption (tickets or cache) to reduce handshake overhead to ~5ms for returning clients, (2) OCSP stapling on load balancer to avoid online certificate validation, (3) Hardware acceleration for AES-NI if on dedicated hardware, (4) Separate payment gateway connection with dedicated TLS connection pool (keep-alive), (5) Monitor TLS handshake latency via APM; alert if >100ms.

sub_findings.issue: Certificate validation adds 25ms per new client TLS connection
sub_findings.impact: First-time payer experience at p95 extends by 25ms
sub_findings.mitigation: OCSP stapling + session resumption

sub_findings.issue: Payment gateway TLS connection is separate from web server TLS
sub_findings.impact: Double TLS overhead: client-to-nopCommerce (~45ms) + nopCommerce-to-gateway (~50ms)
sub_findings.mitigation: Use gateway API connection pooling and HTTP/2 multiplexing

findings.title: Password Hash Verification Intentional Delay as Security Control
findings.reasoning: Noyce's login budget is 1200ms p95. bcrypt at cost factor 12 (recommended) intentionally delays hash verification by ~200ms to prevent brute-force attacks. This is a security-by-design choice that directly reduces throughput: at 100 concurrent logins, this means only ~500 logins/second max vs. ~2500 without hashing delay. This is acceptable for security but EXCEEDS current budget allocation.
findings.recommendation: Revise budget upward: (1) Increase login p95 target from 1200ms to 1500ms to accommodate 200ms bcrypt overhead, (2) Document that this 200ms delay is intentional and non-negotiable for security, (3) Implement parallel login request limiting to prevent hash-verification DoS: max 50 concurrent authentication attempts from single IP, (4) Use adaptive hashing: monitor login latency; if >1500ms, reduce concurrent auth processing, (5) Consider GPU-accelerated bcrypt for payment gateway SSO scenarios (if used).

sub_findings.issue: Current budget assumes faster hashing; bcrypt is slower by design
sub_findings.impact: p95 budget exceeded at peak load; SLA violations possible
sub_findings.mitigation: Accept 200ms overhead; increase budget to 1500ms

sub_findings.issue: Argon2 is slower than bcrypt (250-350ms) but more resistant to GPU attacks
sub_findings.impact: If migrating to Argon2, login latency extends further
sub_findings.mitigation: Use Argon2id with parameters tuned for 150ms max delay

findings.title: JWT Token Signature Validation Bottleneck at Scale
findings.reasoning: Every authenticated request requires JWT signature validation using RS256 (RSA 2048-bit). Each validation costs ~8ms on CPU. At peak load (1000 concurrent users × 2 requests/sec = 2000 req/sec), JWT validation consumes ~16 CPU cores (at 125 reqs/core/sec). If system has only 8 cores, JWT validation becomes the bottleneck. Session lookup (Redis 30ms) is faster but requires server-side session storage. Stateless JWT wins for scalability but costs CPU.
findings.recommendation: Implement: (1) JWT validation caching: for high-frequency API calls (search, cart), validate token only on first request; cache decision for 60 seconds, (2) Switch to HS256 (HMAC) for internal services where key sharing is feasible; ~0.5ms per validation vs. 8ms, (3) Use public key caching (Redis) to avoid re-fetching signing key per request, (4) Implement token validation with short expiry (15 min) to reduce validation frequency, (5) Monitor JWT validation latency; alert if >15ms, (6) For payment/admin APIs, keep RS256 but accept longer validation times; prioritize security over latency.

sub_findings.issue: RS256 validation is CPU-bound; scales linearly with concurrent users
sub_findings.impact: At 5000 concurrent users, JWT validation could consume 50% CPU
sub_findings.mitigation: Cache JWT decisions; use HS256 for internal APIs

sub_findings.issue: Public key rotation requires re-fetching key from key server
sub_findings.impact: Key rotation causes latency spikes if no cache
sub_findings.mitigation: Cache public key with TTL; fetch new key only on validation failure

findings.title: Missing Rate Limiting Creates Login Brute-Force DoS Vector
findings.reasoning: DeFOSPAM found: 'Missing Rate Limiting and Abuse Prevention' for login (REQ052 mentions lockout but not rate limiting). nopCommerce has account lockout on N failed attempts, but no rate limiting at HTTP request level. An attacker can send 1000 login attempts/second from different IPs, exhausting bcrypt CPU and creating denial of service. Lockout applies per email address; attacker can brute-force multiple email addresses in parallel. At 200ms per bcrypt verification, 5 concurrent logins exhaust 1 CPU core.
findings.recommendation: Implement rate limiting: (1) IP-based: max 5 login attempts per IP per 30 minutes (sliding window), (2) Email-based: max 10 attempts per email per hour (after 3 failures, notify user), (3) Distributed rate limiting via Redis to sync across servers, (4) Combine with WAF/DDoS mitigation: block IPs with >100 failed attempts in 5 minutes, (5) Implement exponential backoff: 1st failure = wait 1s, 2nd = 2s, 3rd = 4s (client-side + server-side enforcement), (6) Log all rate limit violations for anomaly detection, (7) SLA: login rate limiting shall not add >10ms latency via Redis lookup.

sub_findings.issue: Account lockout is per-email; attacker can enumerate valid emails and lock all
sub_findings.impact: Denial of service to customer accounts; mass account lockout
sub_findings.mitigation: Implement CAPTCHA after 3 failed attempts per IP

sub_findings.issue: No protection against credential stuffing attacks (valid email + common passwords)
sub_findings.impact: Compromised credentials from other breaches can be tested at scale
sub_findings.mitigation: Check credentials against HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) database; block known-bad combinations

findings.title: Missing Rate Limiting on Password Reset Creates Email Flooding Vector
findings.reasoning: DeFOSPAM identified: 'Missing Rate Limiting for password reset emails'. An attacker can submit unlimited password reset requests for any email address, causing: (1) Email bombing to victim inbox, (2) Email service quota exhaustion (if using SMTP rate limits), (3) Potential email delivery blacklisting if from IP sends too many emails, (4) Token validation lookups in database create query overhead.
findings.recommendation: Implement rate limiting on password reset: (1) Max 3 reset requests per email per hour (sliding window), (2) Max 5 reset requests per IP per 30 minutes, (3) Implement exponential backoff in UI: disable reset button for 60 seconds after request, (4) Store rate limit state in Redis for fast lookup; add <5ms latency, (5) Log all reset attempts; alert if >10 attempts per email in 1 hour, (6) Consider CAPTCHA after 2 reset attempts per IP, (7) Send 'suspicious activity' email if reset requested multiple times; user can click 'block' to cancel all pending resets.

sub_findings.issue: No rate limit on password reset endpoint; unbounded email generation
sub_findings.impact: Email flooding; service quota exhaustion; IP blacklisting
sub_findings.mitigation: 3 resets/email/hour; 5 resets/IP/30min

sub_findings.issue: Reset tokens are typically long-lived (24-48 hours); unused tokens accumulate
sub_findings.impact: Database grows with stale tokens; token validation queries slow
sub_findings.mitigation: Implement token cleanup job; delete tokens >48 hours old daily

findings.title: Missing Rate Limiting on Search API Enables Index Exhaustion DoS
findings.reasoning: Noyce's search budget is 1200ms p95. An attacker can submit 100 complex search queries/second (e.g., wildcard searches, full-text searches on large text fields), consuming search index resources and causing p95 to extend to 5000ms+. At 10 search servers, this is 100 req/sec = 10 req/sec per server, which can trigger auto-scaling. Attacker goal: exhaust search infrastructure and force unnecessary scaling costs.
findings.recommendation: Implement search rate limiting: (1) Per-IP: max 10 search queries per minute (sliding window), (2) Per-session (authenticated user): max 20 searches per minute, (3) Per-IP: max 5 concurrent search requests, (4) Implement search query timeout: 5 seconds max execution; return 'too complex' error, (5) Add request size limit: max 2000 characters per search query, (6) Implement search result pagination enforcement: max 50 results per page (prevent offset >50 to avoid deep pagination abuse), (7) Monitor search latency; alert if p95 exceeds 2000ms, (8) Rate limit breach latency: <3ms Redis lookup.

sub_findings.issue: Pagination offset can be exploited: requesting offset=1000000 forces index scan
sub_findings.impact: Database CPU exhaustion; slow queries affect all users
sub_findings.mitigation: Limit max offset to 50 * max_page_num; implement keyset pagination

sub_findings.issue: Wildcard searches (e.g., *) with leading wildcard force full index scan
sub_findings.impact: Single query can use 100% CPU on search server
sub_findings.mitigation: Disallow leading wildcards; use MIN_NGRAM=2 in search index

findings.title: Encryption at Rest (Database) Has Minimal Performance Impact if Properly Tuned

security_performance.control: Database encryption at rest (TDE or file-level)

findings.reasoning: DeFOSPAM identified PCI-DSS compliance gap: 'Missing encryption of payment data at rest'. Modern database encryption (SQL Server TDE, PostgreSQL pgcrypto, MySQL InnoDB encrypted tables) uses hardware acceleration (AES-NI) and operates at block storage level. Overhead is typically 2-3% on disk I/O due to encryption/decryption. At 1000 req/sec with 100ms database latency, 2-3% overhead = 2-3ms added latency per request. This is acceptable and non-negotiable for PCI compliance.
findings.recommendation: Implement database encryption at rest: (1) Enable Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) on SQL Server or equivalent on PostgreSQL/MySQL, (2) Use hardware AES acceleration (available on all modern CPUs), (3) Ensure key management: store encryption key in Azure Key Vault / AWS KMS (not on same database server), (4) Monitor encryption/decryption latency: set up CloudWatch metrics to track encrypted disk I/O, (5) Accept 2-3% throughput overhead as non-negotiable security cost, (6) Test failover with encrypted database; encryption key must be accessible on standby, (7) Document: 'All payment data at rest is encrypted per PCI-DSS 3.4'.

sub_findings.issue: Encryption key management is critical; key loss = data loss
sub_findings.impact: If encryption key is lost, database is unrecoverable
sub_findings.mitigation: Use managed key service (Azure KMS); enable key rotation every 90 days

sub_findings.issue: Column-level encryption (for highly sensitive fields) has 5-10% overhead
sub_findings.impact: If encrypting payment card fields individually, overhead increases
sub_findings.mitigation: Use column encryption only for card numbers/CVV; rest use TDE

findings.title: Connection Pooling and Session Limit DoS Prevention
findings.reasoning: Without connection limits, an attacker can open 10,000 idle TCP connections to web servers, exhausting file descriptor limits (typically 65,536 per process, 1,024 per user on Linux). This causes new connections to fail immediately ('connection reset'). nopCommerce runs behind load balancer; without per-client connection limits, attacker can still exhaust connection pools. Solution: implement max 50 concurrent connections per IP, with graceful degradation (queue requests).
findings.recommendation: Implement connection and session limits: (1) Max 50 concurrent TCP connections per client IP (enforced at LB), (2) Max 5000 active sessions per application server instance, (3) Idle connection timeout: 600 seconds (10 min); close inactive sessions, (4) Database connection pool: min 10, max 50 per app server (total: max 400 connections on 8-server farm), (5) Connection limit breach response: close oldest idle connection; if no idle connections, queue request for 30 seconds, (6) Implement connection pooling latency monitoring; queue wait should be <5ms, (7) Log connection limit breaches for traffic analysis.

sub_findings.issue: Slow-read attacks: attacker opens connection, reads 1 byte/second from response
sub_findings.impact: Exhausts server threads; each connection waits for full response read
sub_findings.mitigation: Implement read timeout: 30 seconds max per response chunk

sub_findings.issue: Session table can grow unbounded if cleanup job fails
sub_findings.impact: Database bloat; session lookups slow; memory exhaustion
sub_findings.mitigation: Implement automatic session cleanup every 30 min; delete sessions >24 hours old

findings.title: DDoS Mitigation Strategy and WAF Performance Requirements
findings.reasoning: nopCommerce is public e-commerce; subject to volumetric DDoS (UDP floods), application-layer DDoS (HTTP floods), and state-exhaustion attacks (SYN floods). Without DDoS mitigation, a single 1 Gbps attack can take down unprotected infrastructure. WAF latency adds ~15ms per request (rule evaluation). CDN edge filtering (Cloudflare, AWS Shield) adds <5ms and filters attacks before reaching origin.
findings.recommendation: Implement layered DDoS mitigation: (1) LAYER 1 (Infrastructure): AWS Shield Standard (free, automatic) or equivalent DDoS mitigation at cloud provider level; blocks volumetric attacks, (2) LAYER 2 (Edge): Use CDN with DDoS protection (Cloudflare, Akamai, AWS CloudFront); inspect requests at edge, filter malicious traffic, (3) LAYER 3 (Application): Deploy WAF (AWS WAF, ModSecurity, Imperva) at load balancer; rules: (a) Block IPs with >100 req/min to any endpoint, (b) Block IPs with >50 failed logins in 5 min, (c) Block requests with malformed headers, (d) Block requests with SQL injection or XSS payloads, (4) Rate limiting: as specified in NFR-SEC-004, NFR-SEC-005, NFR-SEC-006, (5) Graceful degradation: under attack, shed non-critical features (recommendations, wishlists) and prioritize checkout, (6) WAF latency SLA: <20ms per rule evaluation.

sub_findings.issue: HTTP/2 or HTTP/3 connection multiplexing can be exploited: attacker opens 1000 streams per connection
sub_findings.impact: Server allocates memory per stream; DoS without proper stream limits
sub_findings.mitigation: Limit max streams per connection to 100; prioritize payment/auth streams

sub_findings.issue: Slow-POST attacks: attacker sends HTTP body 1 byte/second
sub_findings.impact: Server holds connection open; threads exhausted
sub_findings.mitigation: Implement read timeout: 30 seconds for full request body

sub_findings.issue: WAF rule evaluation is CPU-intensive; rules can create CPU spike
sub_findings.impact: WAF becomes bottleneck; legitimate traffic blocked
sub_findings.mitigation: Benchmark WAF rules; ensure <20ms latency per request

findings.title: OAuth 2.0 / SSO Token Validation Performance Under Peak Load
findings.reasoning: If nopCommerce integrates OAuth 2.0 SSO (e.g., Microsoft Entra, Google, Okta), each authenticated request requires token validation via remote endpoint (e.g., /oauth/introspect). This is an out-of-process, network-dependent call: 150ms baseline + network latency. At peak (2000 req/sec), this is 300 HTTP calls/sec to OAuth provider. If provider returns 500ms due to outage, all traffic to nopCommerce is blocked (cascading failure).
findings.recommendation: Implement OAuth token validation caching: (1) Cache token validity in Redis with TTL = token expiry (typically 1 hour), (2) On cache miss, validate token with OAuth provider; add <200ms, (3) If OAuth provider unavailable, accept cached token decision for 5 minutes (graceful degradation), (4) Implement token validation batching: bundle 10 token lookups per request to OAuth provider (reduces round trips), (5) Use OAuth provider's token introspection endpoint with optimized parameters; request only {active, scope, exp}, (6) Monitor token validation latency; alert if >250ms, (7) Separate OAuth cache from session cache (different Redis instance/cluster) to prevent contention, (8) For SSO, accept 200ms additional latency at login; prioritize cached validation for subsequent API calls.

sub_findings.issue: OAuth provider outage blocks all authenticated requests (cascading failure)
sub_findings.impact: Single point of failure for entire authentication system
sub_findings.mitigation: Implement fallback: use cached token validity for 5 minutes if provider unreachable

sub_findings.issue: Token introspection endpoint has rate limits; provider may throttle nopCommerce
sub_findings.impact: High-concurrency traffic hits OAuth provider rate limits
sub_findings.mitigation: Request higher rate limit quota from OAuth provider; implement token caching

findings.title: Session Management Scalability: Redis vs. Database Sessions
findings.reasoning: Noyce's budget allocates 30ms for 'Session Lookup (Redis)'. At 10,000 concurrent users (peak nopCommerce load), this is 10,000 Redis keys in memory. Redis lookup is O(1), taking ~0.1ms. However, if sessions stored in database instead, lookup takes 50-100ms (network + query). Scaling: Redis cluster with 3 nodes can handle 100,000 sessions; database table scales to millions but with linear latency increase. Session size is ~2KB (user_id, roles, cart_id, timestamp); 10,000 sessions = 20MB in Redis.
