findings.recommendation: Implement Redis-backed sessions: (1) Store sessions in Redis cluster (3 nodes minimum for HA; avoid single-node SPOF), (2) Session key format: session::{session_id} (TTL = configured session timeout, default 30 min), (3) Session data: {user_id, roles, created_at, last_accessed, ip_address, user_agent}, (4) Implement session replication: Redis Sentinel or Redis Cluster for failover, (5) Monitor session count; alert if >100,000 sessions (indicates bot traffic or session leak), (6) Implement session timeout: if session idle >30 min, delete from Redis and force re-login, (7) Log session creation/deletion for audit, (8) Latency target: session lookup <5ms; alert if >10ms, (9) For stateless architecture (JWT), skip Redis sessions entirely; accept 8ms JWT validation cost instead.

sub_findings.issue: Session fixation attack: attacker sets session cookie to known value
sub_findings.impact: Attacker can hijack user session without password
sub_findings.mitigation: Generate cryptographically random session ID; invalidate on login

sub_findings.issue: Session reuse across browsers/devices can be exploited
sub_findings.impact: If session token exposed, any device can use it
sub_findings.mitigation: Include user_agent + ip_address in session validation; re-auth if mismatch

findings.title: PCI-DSS Compliance Performance Implications: Tokenization vs. Direct Processing
findings.reasoning: DeFOSPAM critical finding: 'Missing Payment Card Data PCI Compliance'. nopCommerce REQ090-091 assume direct card entry without tokenization. PCI-DSS REQUIRES one of three options: (1) Tokenization (nopCommerce receives token only, gateway stores card), (2) Hosted payment page (gateway hosts form, nopCommerce never sees card), (3) 3D Secure redirect (card issuer authenticates user). Option 1 adds ~50ms for gateway token call; Option 2 adds 200ms redirect + user interaction; Option 3 adds 500ms-5s (user entry required). Current budget (3500ms) is feasible with tokenization but NOT with SAQ D (no tokenization) which is non-compliant.
findings.recommendation: Implement PCI-DSS compliant payment processing: (1) REQUIRED: Use tokenization (Option 1). Stripe/Braintree/Square APIs: merchant sends payment_method_token to gateway; gateway returns authorization token. Adds ~50ms. (2) Rewrite REQ090-091: 'System SHALL accept only payment tokens (not raw card data). Tokens generated by gateway API or hosted payment form. System SHALL never store, transmit, or log raw PAN (Primary Account Number), CVV, or expiration date.' (3) Verify PCI compliance: (a) Deploy nopCommerce in 'SAQ A' or 'SAQ A-EP' scope (minimal card handling), (b) Annual PCI-DSS assessment by Qualified Security Assessor (QSA), (c) Provide proof of PCI compliance to customers on request, (4) Adjust payment authorization latency: original 1500ms (gateway) + 50ms (tokenization) = 1550ms is acceptable within 3500ms budget, (5) Implement PCI compliance monitoring: audit logs for all card-related operations, encryption validation, access control checks.

sub_findings.issue: If database is breached, tokenization prevents card exposure (attacker gets tokens, not cards)
sub_findings.impact: Tokens are single-use or time-limited; useless to attacker without gateway access
sub_findings.mitigation: Tokenization is mandatory; no direct card storage allowed

sub_findings.issue: 3D Secure (3DS) adds 500ms-5s due to user authentication step
sub_findings.impact: Payment p95 could extend to 5000-10000ms with 3DS enabled
sub_findings.mitigation: Use frictionless 3DS (issuer authenticates without challenge); fallback to challenge if needed

sub_findings.issue: Scope: nopCommerce itself must be in SAQ A (minimal card data handling)
sub_findings.impact: Reduces PCI audit scope; lowers compliance cost
sub_findings.mitigation: Ensure zero raw card data in nopCommerce; all cards tokenized by gateway

critical_bottlenecks: JWT token signature validation (8ms per request)
dos_vectors: Password reset email flooding (no rate limit)

recommendations_by_priority.action: Implement rate limiting across all endpoints (NFR-SEC-004, NFR-SEC-005, NFR-SEC-006, NFR-SEC-008)
recommendations_by_priority.impact: Eliminates 5 major DoS vectors; enables scalability
Eliminates critical compliance violation; protects customer data
recommendations_by_priority.effort: High (4-6 weeks; requires payment gateway integration)

recommendations_by_priority.action: Adjust performance budgets for security controls (NFR-SEC-001, NFR-SEC-002, NFR-SEC-003, NFR-SEC-010)
recommendations_by_priority.impact: Realistic SLAs that account for security overhead
recommendations_by_priority.effort: Low (1 week for review and documentation)

recommendations_by_priority.impact: Protects against volumetric and application-layer attacks
recommendations_by_priority.effort: Medium (2-3 weeks; includes WAF rules tuning)

recommendations_by_priority.impact: PCI requirement; protects customer data if database stolen
recommendations_by_priority.effort: Medium (2-3 weeks; requires key management setup)

user_login_adjusted.rationale: bcrypt verification cost is non-negotiable for security

search_adjusted.rationale: Rate limiting Redis lookup minimal impact if cached

usability_metrics.document_type: Usability Performance Metrics and User Experience Targets

critical_gaps: Accessibility (WCAG 2.1 Level AA) not integrated with usability metrics
i18n has no usability performance targets
Mobile 3G targets missing from Noyce budgets

usability_metrics.description: Time from login form submission to successful account access or error message display
usability_metrics.rationale: Authentication is a critical entry point. Long waits cause user frustration and cart abandonment. Target aligns with Noyce budget (p95: 1.2s). 3G target is 2x slower due to network latency.

usability_metrics.description: Time from registration form start to account creation confirmation and auto-login
usability_metrics.rationale: New user acquisition requires friction-free registration. Email validation should not block response. Async verification email transmission keeps response time short.

usability_metrics.description: Time from search query submission to first results displayed with filters available
usability_metrics.rationale: Search is key discovery mechanism. Slow results cause users to abandon and use competitors. Cache frequently searched terms. Implement autocomplete to reduce latency perception.

usability_metrics.description: Time from category selection to product list with filters displayed
usability_metrics.rationale: Slightly higher budget (2.2s vs 1.2s) allows for category data aggregation and filter rendering. Still under 3-second frustration threshold.

usability_metrics.metric_name: Add to Cart Task Completion Time
usability_metrics.description: Time from add-to-cart button click to visual confirmation (no page reload required)
usability_metrics.rationale: Cart interactions must be perceived as instant (< 1s). Use AJAX to avoid page reload. Cache inventory availability. This is a high-frequency interaction that directly impacts perceived performance.

usability_metrics.metric_name: Checkout Process - End-to-End Task Completion Time
usability_metrics.description: Time from checkout initiation through all steps (address, shipping, payment, confirmation) to final order confirmation
usability_metrics.rationale: Multi-step checkout allows higher total latency. Each step (address, shipping, payment, review) should be < 2s. Payment processing externally may add 3-5s. Monitor for abandonment after step 3 (payment).

usability_metrics.metric_name: Time to First Meaningful Paint (Product Pages)
usability_metrics.description: Time to render product image, name, price, and add-to-cart button (perceived load completion)
usability_metrics.rationale: Users perceive site as loaded when product image and price are visible. Defer loading reviews, related products, specs. Use progressive image loading.

usability_metrics.description: Time until user can interact with page (add to cart, select options, navigate)
usability_metrics.rationale: Users may leave if page feels unresponsive. Defer non-critical JS. Load critical path first (add to cart, option selection). Test with Lighthouse TTI metric.

usability_metrics.description: Time from form submission error message display to user able to correct and resubmit
usability_metrics.rationale: Error messages must be displayed immediately. Client-side validation provides instant feedback. Server validation errors should return within 500ms to avoid user frustration.

usability_metrics.metric_name: Page Load Time - Accessibility Compliance Check
usability_metrics.description: Time to load page with full semantic HTML, ARIA landmarks, and alt text for assistive technology users
usability_metrics.rationale: Accessibility features (alt text, ARIA labels, semantic HTML) must not degrade performance. Lazy-load offscreen images. Use responsive images with srcset. Keyboard navigation should not impact perceived load time.

usability_metrics.description: Time to detect user locale and serve appropriate language/currency version without visible delay
usability_metrics.rationale: Locale detection must happen pre-render. Use Accept-Language header + IP geolocation + user preference. Cache translations for all supported languages. Avoid visible redirects or content flashing.

usability_metrics.metric_name: Mobile App Launch and Initial Render Time
usability_metrics.description: Time from app launch to showing home screen with navigation available (native mobile app or progressive web app)
usability_metrics.rationale: Cold app launch time is critical for retention. Pre-cache critical assets (home, category list). Lazy-load product images. For PWA: implement service worker for instant repeat visits. Target 150MB memory footprint on mid-range devices.

satisfaction_framework.description: User satisfaction thresholds define the perceived user experience based on response time

delighted.description: User perceives instant feedback. Feels snappy and responsive. Increases satisfaction and willingness to continue shopping.

acceptable.description: User perceives normal performance. Acceptable for most transactions. User will wait and complete task.

frustrated.description: User begins to notice wait. Frustration increases. May bounce to competitor if alternative exists. Common abandonment trigger for slow pages.

abandoned.description: User will almost certainly abandon. Studies show abandonment rate > 95% at 10+ second load times.

wifi.description: Office/home WiFi. Best-case scenario. Used as baseline.

4g.description: Cellular 4G/LTE. Typical mobile user. Mid-range target.

3g.description: Cellular 3G. Emerging markets, rural areas, poor signal. Hardest target.

transaction_types.rationale: Only 0.5% of logins fail due to system latency (invalid credentials not counted). High reliability critical for user trust.
Email validation may fail in async queue. Max 1% error rate acceptable for new user acquisition.
Search should rarely error. Must-have feature; low tolerance for failure.
Critical transaction. High inventory cache hit rate (10s TTL) minimizes errors.
Payment gateway may timeout. Max 1% error rate; all failures must be visible to user with recovery path.
Payment gateway reliability. Match gateway SLA (typically 99.5%). User must be informed of failure; allow retry.
Email may be delayed/lost. Must show order confirmation on screen regardless. Async sends may fail; max 5% acceptable.

battery_impact.optimization: Batch network requests. Reduce refresh rate when inactive. Defer non-critical syncs.

offline_capability.description: Critical features should work offline via service worker cache

abandonment_rate_targets.target_description: Percentage of users who abandon the task before completion

by_transaction.rationale: Users will retry login if < 10s wait. Beyond 10s, many will abandon and use password reset instead.
New users less committed. Higher patience threshold than login. But > 10s causes significant abandonment.
Users have many options (competitors). Slow search = immediate bounce. Target < 5% abandonment.
High-intent user. Will persist through short waits. > 5s wait may cause double-adds.
Users are committed but price-sensitive. Slow form entry frustrates. 8% abandonment acceptable.
Payment delay is primary abandonment trigger. Must complete within 10s. Monitor payment gateway latency.
Final step. User highly committed. But > 15s without feedback = abandoned (user thinks order failed).

nps_and_uss_integration.description: NPS (Net Promoter Score) and USS (Usable Systems Score) measurement points integrate performance metrics with user satisfaction

measurement_points.nps_question: How likely are you to recommend our login process to a friend? (0-10)
measurement_points.uss_criterion: Could you log in without difficulty?
measurement_points.threshold: NPS >= 50 when login time <= 1.2s; NPS drops to 0-10 when >= 10s

measurement_points.nps_question: How helpful was our search in finding what you wanted? (0-10)
measurement_points.uss_criterion: Did you find products quickly without frustration?
measurement_points.threshold: NPS >= 70 when search <= 1.2s; each 1s additional latency reduces NPS by ~5 points

measurement_points.uss_criterion: Did you complete checkout without confusing steps?
measurement_points.threshold: NPS >= 60 when checkout <= 9s. > 15s checkout time reduces NPS to < 30.

measurement_points.metric: Time to meaningful paint (p95: 1.5s) + image load
measurement_points.nps_question: Was the product page easy to understand? (0-10)
measurement_points.uss_criterion: Could you see product details without waiting?
measurement_points.threshold: NPS >= 75 when FMP <= 1.5s; slow images reduce NPS by 20+ points

correlation_analysis.description: Empirical correlation between performance metrics and NPS

executive_summary.title: Complete Monitoring and Observability Strategy for nopCommerce
executive_summary.key_findings: nopCommerce has ZERO production monitoring, observability, or alerting infrastructure defined. Platform running at 500 avg / 2,500 peak concurrent users with 6 critical SLA tiers requires comprehensive synthetic, RUM, APM, and infrastructure monitoring. Security overhead (513ms) and scalability bottlenecks demand real-time alerting with automated mitigation.
executive_summary.deployment_phases: Phase 1 (2 weeks): Health checks + synthetic monitoring. Phase 2 (3 weeks): RUM + infrastructure APM. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Mobile, transaction tracing, regression detection.

layers.purpose: Proactive detection of performance regressions before users are affected
layers.tools: Datadog Synthetics, AWS CloudWatch Synthetics, New Relic

layers.purpose: Capture actual user experience metrics from browsers and mobile apps
layers.tools: New Relic Browser, Datadog RUM, Segment, mParticle
metrics: Page load time (DNSLookup, TCP, TTFB, ContentDownload, Rendering)
layers.sampling: 100% for errors, 10% for successful transactions (configurable per feature)
layers.geo_filtering: Capture by country, region, ISP, device type, browser

layers.purpose: Distributed tracing and component-level latency isolation
layers.tools: Datadog APM, New Relic APM, Jaeger, Zipkin
coverage: Database queries (duration, query type, connection pool utilization)
External API calls (payment gateway, shipping, recommendations)
Message queue processing (if async patterns used)
layers.trace_sampling: 100% for errors/slow requests (>2x baseline), 5% for normal requests

layers.purpose: Understand resource utilization and capacity headroom
layers.tools: Prometheus, Grafana, Datadog Infrastructure, New Relic Infrastructure
layers.collection_interval: 10-second intervals for critical metrics, 60-second for others

layers.purpose: Troubleshooting, compliance audit, and anomaly detection
layers.tools: ELK Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana), Datadog Logs, Splunk
log_sources: Web server access logs (Nginx, IIS) - timestamp, method, URL, status, latency
Application logs (error, warning, info) - stack traces for exceptions
Database logs - slow query log (>100ms), connection pool events
Security logs - authentication failures, rate limit violations, WAF blocks
Payment gateway logs - transaction status, auth codes
Audit logs - user actions, admin operations, data changes
layers.sampling: 100% for errors and security events; 10% for normal operations

layers.purpose: Native app-specific metrics and crash detection
layers.tools: Firebase Crashlytics, Datadog RUM Mobile, New Relic Mobile, Sentry
metrics: API error rates specific to mobile
layers.crash_reporting: Real-time alerts on new crash signatures

layers.purpose: Root cause analysis for performance incidents
layers.tools: Continuous Profiler (CPU, memory), Thread Dump analysis, GC logs
layers.when_triggered: Automatically when p95 latency > 2.5x baseline or error rate spike detected
diagnostics: Memory heap dump (identify leaks, large object allocations)

components: Cache hit ratio (Redis for product details)

infrastructure.cpu_per_search: Should be linear or sublinear with result set

infrastructure.cpu_alert: Bcrypt CPU spike is expected; alert if > 95%

alert_thresholds.secondary_critical: Error rate > 0.1% (possible brute-force attack or rate limiting issue)

synthetic_monitoring.endpoint: Full user journey: POST /checkout/address -> POST /checkout/shipping -> GET /checkout/review -> POST /checkout/payment -> GET /checkout/confirmation

components: Step 1: Address validation API call (external, target < 1500ms)
Step 2: Shipping rate calculation (external carrier API, target < 2000ms)
Step 4: Payment gateway connection (circuit breaker applied)

test_cards: Declined test card (to verify error handling)

alert_thresholds.secondary_critical: Authorization failure rate > 2% (possible fraud or gateway issue)

development.alerting: Email to dev team on critical errors

staging.alerting: Pagerduty to on-call engineer for critical SLA violations
staging.performance_gates: Automated: p95 must be within 5% of production baseline

production.apm_monitoring: 5% trace sampling normal, 100% errors and slow (>2x baseline)
production.infrastructure: Full monitoring with 10-second collection for critical metrics, 60-second for others
production.alerting: Pagerduty escalation with automated mitigation triggers

level_1_warning.condition: SLA p95 approaches threshold (75% of SLA)

level_2_critical.action: Page on-call engineer, Slack #incidents channel, start war room call

level_3_incident.condition: SLA p95 breached (exceeds threshold) for > 2 minutes
level_3_incident.action: Page engineering lead + on-call engineer, trigger automated mitigation

Shopping Cart.metric: p95 response time + persistence failure rate

Checkout Flow.metric: p95 response time + error rate + revenue impact

Payment Processing.metric: p95 response time + auth failure rate

alert_fatigue_prevention.strategy: Multi-layer alert correlation and intelligent deduplication

components.description: Group related alerts (e.g., all 'high CPU' alerts from different servers as single 'cluster CPU spike')
components.implementation: Configure alert routing rules in Pagerduty/Datadog to group by tag (app_tier, region, feature)

components.description: Only alert once per incident, not repeatedly every 5 minutes
components.implementation: Set alert duration windows: warn every 5 min, critical every 2 min, incident only at transition

components.description: If database is down, don't alert on downstream 'high app CPU' (expected due to queuing)
components.implementation: Configure alert dependencies: app latency alert only fires if database is healthy

components.description: Adjust alert thresholds based on time of day, day of week, promotional events
components.implementation: Increase thresholds during known promotional windows (Black Friday, cyber sales)

components.description: Alert on deviation from learned baseline, not absolute threshold
components.implementation: Use Datadog/New Relic anomaly detection: alert if response time increases 2x normal for that time of day

components.description: Treat alerts as error budget consumption; quiet mode during high error budget burn
components.implementation: If SLA p95 is already breached, don't alert on secondary metrics; focus on root cause

trigger_and_action_pairs.trigger: App tier CPU > 85% for > 2 minutes AND scaling capacity available
trigger_and_action_pairs.action: Auto-scale app tier: spin up 2 additional app servers

trigger_and_action_pairs.trigger: Database connection pool > 80% utilized for > 1 minute
trigger_and_action_pairs.action: Trigger graceful connection reset; drain idle connections from cache layer
trigger_and_action_pairs.root_cause: Possible upstream queue buildup; may need additional troubleshooting

trigger_and_action_pairs.trigger: Payment gateway response time > 5 seconds or error rate > 5%
trigger_and_action_pairs.action: Activate circuit breaker (fail-fast): return 'Authorization Pending' to user, queue for async retry
trigger_and_action_pairs.fallback_queue: Process up to 1000 retries/hour in background, alert payments team
trigger_and_action_pairs.manual_override: Engineer can disable circuit breaker if gateway restored

trigger_and_action_pairs.trigger: Search p95 latency > 1200ms AND Elasticsearch JVM GC > 1 second
trigger_and_action_pairs.action: Trigger cache flush of oldest 10% of search result cache; reduce facet aggregation depth
trigger_and_action_pairs.root_cause: Memory pressure on search cluster; may need additional nodes

trigger_and_action_pairs.trigger: Error rate on login endpoint > 0.5% (possible brute-force attack) AND rate limiting not already active
trigger_and_action_pairs.action: Activate aggressive rate limiting: 2 attempts per IP per 5 minutes (vs. normal 5 per 30 min)
trigger_and_action_pairs.duration: Until error rate drops < 0.1% for 10 minutes
trigger_and_action_pairs.log_event: Security incident: brute force attack detected

trigger_and_action_pairs.trigger: Redis cache eviction rate > 100 items/sec (memory pressure)
trigger_and_action_pairs.action: Set lazy-evict policy; alert engineer to add cache nodes
trigger_and_action_pairs.temporary_ttl_reduction: Reduce all cache TTLs by 50% for 30 minutes to shed memory
trigger_and_action_pairs.monitor_hit_ratio: If hit ratio drops > 5%, revert TTL reduction

trigger_and_action_pairs.trigger: Network saturation > 80% on load balancer for > 1 minute
trigger_and_action_pairs.action: Activate 'traffic shed' mode: prioritize checkout and auth traffic; defer product recommendations and wishlists
trigger_and_action_pairs.graceful_degradation_message: Show user message: 'High demand; some features temporarily unavailable'

trigger_and_action_pairs.trigger: Memory leak suspected: Java heap steadily grows > 100MB/hour despite GC
trigger_and_action_pairs.action: Alert engineer; trigger heap dump and send to on-call analyst; consider rolling restart if critical
trigger_and_action_pairs.rolling_restart: Drain traffic from 1 app server, restart, return to pool (repeat for others)

engineering_dashboard.purpose: On-call engineer needs instant visibility into system health

Memory used vs available by tier

App tier: count, avg CPU %, avg memory %, GC pause time

Payment success rate (% of payment attempts that succeed)

health_check_endpoints.design_principle: Health checks must be lightweight (< 50ms) and not consume expensive resources (e.g., no database queries)

endpoints.frequency: Every 10 seconds by orchestrator (Kubernetes, Docker Compose)
endpoints.action_if_failing: Drain traffic from server; restart if not recovered in 30 sec

checks_performed: Payment gateway: check circuit breaker status (no actual call)

recommended_toolset.apm_platform: Datadog APM or New Relic APM (distributed tracing)
recommended_toolset.log_aggregation: Datadog Logs or ELK Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana)

recommended_toolset.synthetic_monitoring: Datadog Synthetics or New Relic Synthetics
recommended_toolset.alerting_and_anomaly: Datadog Monitors or New Relic Alerts

integration_points.integration: Nginx exporter for Prometheus; Datadog agent with log integration

integration_points.integration: APM agent library (Datadog .NET agent or Node.js agent)
integration_points.custom_instrumentation: Engineer can add custom spans for business logic

integration_points.integration: APM agent captures query execution as part of traces
integration_points.additional_monitoring: Slow query log analysis, connection pool metrics via agent

integration_points.integration: Redis exporter for Prometheus; APM agent traces cache operations
integration_points.metrics: hit ratio, eviction rate, memory utilization, command latency
message rate, processing time, dead letter queue depth

performance_regression_detection.strategy: Build-over-build comparison to detect performance regressions before production deployment

implementation.name: Baseline Performance Test (Staging)
implementation.trigger: After all unit tests pass, before code review
process: Deploy build to isolated performance test environment
Run synthetic test suite (load at 1x expected peak: 2500 users, 8000 tx/hr)
Collect p50, p90, p95, p99 latencies for each critical transaction
Measure CPU, memory, GC time, database query times

implementation.name: Compare Build to Previous Baseline
process: Load previous known-good baseline from artifact repository
Compare new p95 latencies per feature to baseline
Flag regression if > 5% for any critical feature
implementation.action_if_failed: Block merge; require engineer to investigate and remediate

implementation.name: Drill Into Regression
diagnostics: Component-level latency breakdown: which component regressed? (DB, cache, API, network)
Code diff analysis: which method/endpoint changed? Highlight lines added/removed
Allocations: did new code allocate more memory? Trigger heap analysis
Database queries: new queries added? Changed indexes? Explain plans comparison
External API calls: new calls added? Changed timeout values?
implementation.report: Auto-generated HTML report with suggestions (merge if approved by engineer)

implementation.name: Production-Like Load Test
implementation.trigger: After code merge to main branch
process: Deploy to staging with production-like data volume
Run sustained load test at 1.5x peak: 3750 concurrent users, 12000 tx/hr for 1 hour
Measure p95 latency, error rate, resource utilization, GC behavior
Validate that system recovers quickly after load spike (within 2 minutes)
pass_criteria: Memory growth < 50MB over test duration (rule out leaks)
Recovery time to normal latency < 2 minutes after load drop
implementation.failure_handling: Block production deployment; rollback to previous version

implementation.name: Build-Over-Build Comparison in Prod
implementation.trigger: Automatic monitoring after each production deployment
process: Compare p95 latencies in current production to previous 3 deployments
Flag regression if current p95 > max(previous 3 baselines) + 3% (allowing for normal variance)
If regression detected, alert engineering lead with RCA suggestions
baseline_comparison: Deployment N vs N-1 (most recent): expect < 1% variance
Deployment N vs N-2: expect < 2% variance
Deployment N vs N-3: expect < 3% variance (beyond this is outlier)

implementation.name: Weekly Performance Report
analysis: Average p95 latency trend over last 12 weeks
Identify slow regressions (2-3% per week creep)
Correlate with code deployments and feature launches
Recommend optimization targets if trend is negative
implementation.output: Executive summary with charts sent to engineering leadership

executive_summary.test_strategy_scope: Complete performance test strategy covering load, stress, soak, spike, breakpoint, and volume testing

baseline_requirements.sla_targets: Catalog 2.2s, Search 1.2s, Auth 1.2s, Cart 800ms, Checkout 9.0s, Payment 3.5s (all p95)

critical_test_priorities: Database connection pool exhaustion at 5K+ users
Race conditions in stock validation under load
Resource leaks over 24-72 hour soak tests
Graceful degradation vs hard failure at 10x peak
Cache layer eviction and hit rate under sustained load
Payment gateway timeout behavior and recovery
Security control overhead (TLS, bcrypt, JWT) under peak
executive_summary.key_risks: Previous agents identified critical bottlenecks: DB connection pool (breaks at 5K users), race conditions in stock validation, no horizontal scaling strategy for database, 513ms security overhead reducing safety margin in budgets. Soak tests essential to identify resource leaks before production.
executive_summary.test_duration_rationale: Industry best practice requires minimum 24-hour soak (detect hourly/daily patterns), 72-hour soak (catch weekly job impacts, memory leaks), stress tests at 2x-10x peak, breakpoint test to find system limit

soak_test_strategy.description: Sustained load testing to identify resource leaks, degradation patterns, and long-running stability issues

ramp_up_profile.rationale: Gradual ramp prevents sudden connection pool saturation. Allows load balancer and database to establish steady-state before measurement begins.

ramp_down_profile.rationale: Gradual ramp-down prevents sudden connection drops that could mask issues

degradation_acceptance_criteria.metric: p95 response time for each transaction type
degradation_acceptance_criteria.threshold: Response time at hour 24 shall not exceed baseline + 5%
degradation_acceptance_criteria.rationale: 5% degradation threshold prevents runaway memory leaks while allowing normal OS buffer cache changes

memory_usage.target: Stable within +/- 10% of baseline after 2 hours

extended_soak_profile.name: 72-Hour Extended Soak for Leak Detection
extended_soak_profile.rationale: Extended soak catches memory leaks with long periods (e.g., cron jobs, batch processes running daily/weekly). Identifies patterns across multiple 24-hour cycles.

window_3.purpose: Weekly pattern detection + cumulative leak detection

specific_leak_risks.detection_method: Monitor Redis memory; should plateau at ~7.5GB (5000 sessions * 1.5KB * TTL 30min)
specific_leak_risks.expected_behavior: Memory stable after 2 hours; eviction rate < 1% after warm-up

specific_leak_risks.risk: Cart data orphaning (abandoned carts not cleaned)
specific_leak_risks.detection_method: Track cart table row count; implement cleanup job (verify it runs)
specific_leak_risks.expected_behavior: Cart count grows during day, drops after cleanup job (nightly at 2 AM assumed)

specific_leak_risks.risk: Database connection leaks from exception handling
specific_leak_risks.detection_method: Monitor connection pool queue length; spike indicates leak
specific_leak_risks.expected_behavior: Queue length stable < 5 connections throughout soak

specific_leak_risks.risk: Thread pool thread leaks (stuck threads never returned)
specific_leak_risks.detection_method: Monitor thread count per JVM; compare hour 0 vs 72
specific_leak_risks.expected_behavior: Thread count stable within +/- 10 from baseline

specific_leak_risks.risk: Log file unbounded growth (logs not rotated)
specific_leak_risks.expected_behavior: Log rotation triggered daily; no more than 1GB consumed

specific_leak_risks.risk: Cache corruption (stale cache entries not evicted)
specific_leak_risks.detection_method: Track product prices in cache vs database; spot-check for stale data
specific_leak_risks.expected_behavior: Cache TTL honored; no stale data beyond TTL expiry + 10 seconds

near_capacity_soak.name: 12-Hour Soak at 90% Predicted Peak (4500 users)
near_capacity_soak.rationale: Test near production peak load with less resource drain than 24-hour. Used for final production validation.

degradation_acceptance_criteria.p95_target: All SLAs must be met (Catalog 2.2s, Search 1.2s, Auth 1.2s, Cart 800ms, Checkout 9.0s, Payment 3.5s)

stress_test_strategy.description: Test system behavior beyond capacity to identify breaking points, graceful degradation, and recovery behavior

stress_test_2x_peak.name: Stress Test at 2x Peak (5000 concurrent users)
stress_test_2x_peak.rationale: 2x peak = predicted growth target. Tests horizontal scaling assumptions. Should maintain > 95% SLA compliance.

success_criteria.system_recovery: Response times return to baseline within 2 minutes after ramp-down
success_criteria.no_cascading_failures: Payment timeout does not cascade to cart/checkout failure

stress_test_5x_peak.name: Stress Test at 5x Peak (12500 concurrent users)
stress_test_5x_peak.rationale: 5x peak = aggressive growth scenario or DDoS attack. System likely cannot maintain SLAs; test graceful degradation instead.

critical_functions_maintained: Checkout must succeed (p95 < 30s allowed, vs 9s normal)
Payment authorization must not fail due to timeout (retry logic engaged)
Order persistence must be guaranteed (no lost orders due to queue overflow)
acceptable_degradation: Product recommendations disabled (can refresh on next session)
Wishlist operations may queue (execute async, user sees 'Pending' status)
Inventory cache eviction acceptable (fall back to DB with lock)
unacceptable_failure_modes: Session loss (except temporary until cache refill)

stress_test_5x_peak.circuit_breaker_validation: Test that circuit breakers engage under stress: payment gateway overload -> circuit opens -> transactions fail gracefully with 'Service Busy' instead of timeout; system recovers when load drops

stress_test_10x_peak.name: Stress Test at 10x Peak (25000 concurrent users) - Breakpoint Test
stress_test_10x_peak.rationale: Find absolute breaking point: where system completely fails or enters unusable state. Used to establish load shedding thresholds.
expected_failure_modes: Database connection pool completely exhausted (new connections rejected)
File descriptor limits hit (65536 limit on Linux)
Load balancer connection limits reached (~200k concurrent connections, feasible but high)
Payment gateway rate limit hit (assumes 1000 req/min rate limit)
stress_test_10x_peak.hard_limit_testing: Deliberately push until system fails hard. Measure: (1) At what user count does error rate jump to 50%? (2) What's first hard resource limit? (3) How long to recover after shutting down load?

recovery_validation.measure_recovery_time: From 25000 users -> 0, measure time until p95 response time returns to < 2s for each transaction type
recovery_validation.alert_threshold: If recovery takes > 5 minutes, investigate resource leak or stuck connections

spike_test.name: Sudden Spike Test (Flash Sale Scenario)
spike_test.description: Simulate flash sale: baseline 500 users -> 5000 users in 30 seconds
spike_test.rationale: Real-world marketing events (flash sales, viral tweets, press coverage). Tests auto-scaling reaction time and queue behavior.

success_criteria.auto_scaling_latency: First scale-up action triggered within 60 seconds

spike_test.measurement: Compare with baseline; identify how many orders are lost or delayed if auto-scaling fails

volume_test.name: High-Volume Data Test
volume_test.description: Test system with production-like data volumes to verify query performance

test_scenarios.name: Product Search with 100K Products
test_scenarios.rationale: Full-text search must scale with product catalog

test_scenarios.name: Order History with 1M Orders
test_scenarios.rationale: Indexed queries must remain fast even with large table

breakpoint_test.name: Progressive Load to Breaking Point
breakpoint_test.description: Gradually increase load until system breaks, recording metrics at each step
breakpoint_test.output: Graph showing response time vs load; identify knee point where system starts degrading

memory_leaks.risk_description: Long-lived request objects retained in memory after request completes
memory_leaks.leak_mechanism: Request context object holds reference to cart, user session; object never garbage collected
memory_leaks.detection_method: Heap dump at hour 0 vs hour 24; compare object counts (String[], HashMap, etc.)
memory_leaks.test_strategy: Run 24-hour soak with heap profiling enabled; look for non-garbage-collected objects
memory_leaks.mitigation: Code review: ensure request-scoped objects properly nulled on request end; use weak references for caches

memory_leaks.risk_description: Cache memory grows unbounded due to insufficient eviction
memory_leaks.leak_mechanism: Product catalog cached with no TTL; cache eviction policy (LRU) not triggered if memory < maxmemory
memory_leaks.detection_method: Monitor Redis memory usage; if grows > 100MB/hour, indicates leak
memory_leaks.test_strategy: Load 10000 unique products into cache over 24 hours; verify memory does not exceed 20GB
memory_leaks.mitigation: Set Redis maxmemory = 64GB; enable LRU eviction with 1% sample rate; monitor eviction_rate metric

memory_leaks.risk_description: Connections leak from pool due to exception handling not returning connection
memory_leaks.leak_mechanism: Exception thrown during query execution; catch block doesn't call connection.close()
memory_leaks.detection_method: Monitor active_connections metric in connection pool; if grows from 50 to 120 over 24h without load increase, leak detected
memory_leaks.test_strategy: Inject random DB failures (network timeout, constraint violation) during load test; verify connections returned
memory_leaks.mitigation: Code review: enforce try-with-resources or finally blocks for all connection.close() calls; use connection pool monitoring alerts

memory_leaks.risk_description: Threads created but never returned to pool (deadlock or infinite loop)
memory_leaks.leak_mechanism: Request handler enters infinite loop waiting for payment gateway response timeout
memory_leaks.detection_method: Monitor thread_count metric; if grows > 50 over 24h, leak likely
memory_leaks.test_strategy: Stress test with payment gateway timeout (simulate hung connection); verify thread count stable
memory_leaks.mitigation: Set request timeout = 30s; interrupt stuck threads; implement thread deadlock detector

connection_leaks.test_strategy: 24-hour soak with connection pool metrics dashboard; alert if queue length > 5

connection_leaks.risk_description: Connections to payment gateway / shipping API not closed on exception
connection_leaks.leak_mechanism: Timeout exception during API call; response stream not consumed or closed
connection_leaks.detection_method: Monitor open socket count (netstat -an | grep ESTABLISHED | wc -l); if grows > 100, leak likely
connection_leaks.test_strategy: Spike test with payment gateway slow responses (30s delay); verify connection count returns to baseline within 5 min

connection_leaks.risk_description: Redis client connections not returned to pool
connection_leaks.leak_mechanism: Exception during Redis operation; client not returned
connection_leaks.detection_method: Monitor redis connected_clients metric; if grows > 200, leak likely

file_handle_leaks.risk_description: File handles to product images or log files not closed
file_handle_leaks.leak_mechanism: Image serving code opens file without try-finally; exception leaves file handle open
file_handle_leaks.detection_method: Monitor lsof | wc -l per process; alert if > 5000 file handles per process
file_handle_leaks.test_strategy: Load test with request rate 100 req/sec for 24h; monitor file handle count
file_handle_leaks.mitigation: Use try-with-resources for all file operations; implement file handle auditing in monitoring

acceptable_degradation_profile.description: Define what degradation is acceptable over time and under stress

response_time_degradation.baseline: Hour 0-1 steady-state p95 response times (Catalog 2.2s, Search 1.2s, etc.)
response_time_degradation.rationale: Small degradation (1-2%) expected from OS buffer cache warming; >10% indicates resource leak

cpu_memory_degradation.disk_space: Log rotation triggered; no unbounded disk growth

queue_depth_degradation.acceptable_peak: Queue depth spikes < 50 during transient high load
queue_depth_degradation.unacceptable: Queue depth remains > 100 for > 5 minutes (indicates saturation)

customers.rationale: Realistic customer base; some with extensive order history

products.rationale: Realistic product catalog; tests inventory management

orders.rationale: Historical data for order lookup performance; tests query selectivity

reviews.rationale: Product pages display reviews; tests query performance with joins

images.rationale: Tests CDN performance and image caching

steps: Generate 50K customers with realistic email distribution (30% gmail.com, 20% yahoo.com, 50% corporate)
Generate 10K products with realistic pricing ($5-$5000), categories, descriptions
Pre-generate 100K images (actual JPEG/PNG files, not mock)
Load into staging DB; verify indexes created
User sessions: generate random session IDs, carts with 1-10 items
Reviews: generate synthetic reviews with realistic text (use Lorem Ipsum variants)
Orders: simulate historical orders (create_date = rand(365 days ago to today))
Wishlist: 30% of customers have wishlist with 2-20 items
Truncate orders, reviews, sessions (keep customers, products, categories)

edge_case_data.purpose: Tests query performance with large result sets

edge_case_data.case: Concurrent orders for same limited-stock item
edge_case_data.purpose: Tests race condition in inventory validation
Tests pagination and filtering at scale

infrastructure_for_testing.recommendation: Create dedicated performance test environment identical to production (or as close as possible)

load_generator.machine_spec: 16 vCPU, 32GB RAM, 1Gbps network (to avoid generator bottleneck)
load_generator.justification: Load generator must not be bottleneck; 16 vCPU can generate ~50k concurrent connections
load_generator.deployment: On-premise or cloud VM in same VPC as test infrastructure

test_application_servers.configuration: Identical to production (same JVM version, thread pools, connection pools)
test_application_servers.justification: Must match production for results validity; smaller scale (4 vs 6 baseline) acceptable for cost

test_database.justification: Smaller than production (2x 1TB) but sufficient for 10K products, 100K orders. Must have same indexes and schema.

cache_layer.justification: Match production cluster setup for replication/failover testing

monitoring_stack.logs: ELK Stack (Elasticsearch + Logstash + Kibana) or CloudWatch
monitoring_stack.apm: DataDog APM or NewRelic (for transaction tracing)
monitoring_stack.justification: Collect detailed metrics without impacting test results

test_environment_isolation.network_isolation: Dedicated VPC / private network (no internet egress except to external APIs like payment gateway via VPN)
test_environment_isolation.data_isolation: Test database separate from production; do NOT share DB with staging
test_environment_isolation.blast_radius: If test environment crashes, no impact to production (separate AWS accounts recommended)

production_readiness_validation.requirement: Before deploying to production, run same tests on production-like staging environment
checklist: Pass 24-hour soak at 2500 users with < 5% degradation
Pass 2x peak stress test (5000 users) with > 95% SLA compliance
Pass breakpoint test up to 10x peak (25000 users) with graceful degradation
All critical memory/connection leaks fixed (no growth > 10% over 24h)
Monitoring and alerting tested and working

defospam_scenario_mapping.description: Map DeFOSPAM scenarios to performance test scripts

mapping.defospam_scenario: Scenario 1: Customer browses catalog, views product details
mapping.load_generator_code: User loops: 1) GET /catalog, 2) GET /product/{id}, 3) GET /reviews/{product_id}, repeat 10x
User: 1) GET /search?q={keyword}, 2) Click product in results, 3) GET /reviews, repeat 5x
User: 1) POST /login (email, password), 2) GET /dashboard, repeat 10x with concurrent requests

mapping.defospam_scenario: Scenario 4: Customer adds items to cart and modifies cart
mapping.load_generator_code: User: 1) GET /product/{id}, 2) POST /cart/add (quantity, sku), 3) GET /cart, 4) PUT /cart/{item_id} (update qty), 5) DELETE /cart/{item_id}, repeat 5x

mapping.defospam_scenario: Scenario 5: Checkout flow and order placement
mapping.load_generator_code: User: 1) POST /checkout/start, 2) POST /checkout/billing (address), 3) POST /checkout/shipping (method), 4) POST /checkout/payment (card), 5) POST /checkout/confirm, 6) GET /order/{order_id}
User: 1) POST /payment/authorize (card, amount, merchant_ref), 2) Wait for response, 3) POST /payment/confirm if success
mapping.slo: p95 payment < 3.5s; p99 < 7s; authorization timeout handled with retry

mapping.defospam_scenario: Scenario 7: User registration and account creation
mapping.load_generator_code: User: 1) POST /register (email, password, name), 2) Verify email token (async), 3) GET /account
mapping.slo: p95 registration < 1.2s; email delivery < 5 min (async, not in critical path)

mapping.defospam_scenario: Scenario 9: Order history and status tracking
mapping.slo: p95 order list < 1.5s; p95 order detail < 2.0s

mapping.load_generator_code: User: 1) GET /product/{id}/reviews, 2) POST /review (rating, text), 3) PUT /review/{review_id}, 4) GET /reviews/my-reviews
mapping.slo: p95 load reviews < 1.5s; p95 post review < 2.0s

doneness_criteria_and_performance_gates.definition: Exit criteria that must be satisfied before concluding testing and deploying to production

load_test_gates.name: Error Rate Gate
load_test_gates.criteria: During sustained load at 2500 concurrent users, error rate must be < 0.5% for all endpoints
load_test_gates.measurement: Count HTTP 5xx responses; divide by total requests
load_test_gates.failing_action: Investigate root cause; fix bugs; retest

load_test_gates.name: SLA Compliance Gate
load_test_gates.criteria: All features must meet Noyce's SLA targets at baseline 2500 users
load_test_gates.measurement: Measure p95 response time per endpoint; compare to targets
load_test_gates.pass_threshold: All endpoints meet their p95 target
load_test_gates.failing_action: Performance optimization required; identify bottleneck; retry after fix

load_test_gates.name: Database Connection Pool Gate
load_test_gates.criteria: Database connection pool must not exceed 80% utilization at baseline load
load_test_gates.measurement: Monitor active_connections / max_connections during load test
load_test_gates.failing_action: Increase pool size or implement connection pooling middleware (PgBouncer); retest

load_test_gates.name: Memory Stability Gate
load_test_gates.criteria: Application server memory must be stable (not growing) after 2-hour warm-up
load_test_gates.measurement: Sample heap memory size every 5 minutes; check for linear growth
load_test_gates.failing_action: Profile application for memory leaks; fix leaks; retest with memory profiling enabled

stress_test_gates.name: 2x Peak SLA Compliance Gate
stress_test_gates.criteria: At 2x peak (5000 users), SLA compliance must be > 95% (only 5% of requests violate SLA)
stress_test_gates.measurement: Count requests exceeding SLA for each endpoint; divide by total requests per endpoint
stress_test_gates.failing_action: Horizontal scaling issue; add more servers; retest

stress_test_gates.name: Error Rate Degradation Gate
stress_test_gates.criteria: At 2x peak, error rate must be < 5% (transient errors acceptable)
stress_test_gates.failing_action: Identify failure mode (connection pool, payment gateway timeout, etc.); fix; retest

stress_test_gates.name: Recovery Time Gate
stress_test_gates.criteria: After ramp-down from 2x peak (5000 -> 0 users), system must recover to normal response times within 2 minutes
stress_test_gates.measurement: Measure p95 response time during ramp-down and 2 min after; compare to baseline
stress_test_gates.pass_threshold: p95 returns to baseline (within 10%) within 2 minutes
stress_test_gates.failing_action: Investigate resource leak or stuck connections; fix; retest with resource monitoring

stress_test_gates.name: Graceful Degradation Gate
stress_test_gates.criteria: At 5x peak (12500 users), critical functions (Checkout, Payment) must succeed; non-critical functions may degrade
stress_test_gates.measurement: Track order success rate, payment auth rate, verify DB integrity post-test
stress_test_gates.failing_action: Review error handling logic; implement circuit breakers; retest

soak_test_gates.name: 24-Hour Degradation Gate
soak_test_gates.criteria: Response time degradation over 24 hours must be < 5%
soak_test_gates.measurement: Compare p95 response time: hour 0-1 baseline vs hour 23-24
soak_test_gates.failing_action: Memory leak suspected; enable heap profiling; identify leaked objects; fix code; retest

soak_test_gates.name: Memory Leak Detection Gate
soak_test_gates.criteria: Heap memory must not grow unbounded during 24-hour soak
soak_test_gates.measurement: Sample heap size every hour; fit to linear regression; calculate slope (MB/hour)
soak_test_gates.failing_action: Heap dump analysis; identify retained objects; code fix; retest with memory profiling

soak_test_gates.name: Connection Pool Stability Gate
soak_test_gates.criteria: Active database connections must remain stable throughout soak
soak_test_gates.measurement: Monitor active_connections; fit to linear regression
soak_test_gates.failing_action: Connection leak in exception handling; add try-with-resources; retest

soak_test_gates.name: Error Rate Stability Gate
soak_test_gates.criteria: Error rate must not increase during soak (except transient spikes < 1 minute)
soak_test_gates.measurement: Track error rate per hour; should be stable near 0.1%
soak_test_gates.pass_threshold: Error rate remains < 0.5% throughout test; no increasing trend
soak_test_gates.failing_action: Investigate error spike; may indicate resource exhaustion; retest

soak_test_gates.name: 72-Hour Extended Soak Gate
soak_test_gates.criteria: All soak gates plus multi-day stability (memory, connections, performance stable across day boundaries)
soak_test_gates.measurement: Compare hour 0-24 metrics vs hour 24-48 vs hour 48-72
soak_test_gates.pass_threshold: No significant differences; metric slopes < 50% of hour 0-24 slope
soak_test_gates.failing_action: Cumulative leak or pattern-based issue; investigate daily/weekly cron jobs; retest

breakpoint_test_gates.name: Breaking Point Identification Gate
breakpoint_test_gates.criteria: System must clearly demonstrate graceful degradation vs hard failure
breakpoint_test_gates.measurement: Plot response time and error rate vs load; identify knee point
pass_criteria: Error rate < 5% up to 2x peak (5000 users)
System becomes unusable (error rate > 50%) between 5x-10x peak
breakpoint_test_gates.documentation: Produce graph showing breaking points; document which resources exhaust first (DB connections, memory, CPU)

breakpoint_test_gates.name: Resource Limit Identification Gate
breakpoint_test_gates.criteria: Identify and document the first hard resource limit encountered
breakpoint_test_gates.measurement: At peak load, which runs out first: DB connections, memory, file handles, thread pool?
breakpoint_test_gates.pass_criteria: Clear resource limit identified; mitigation path documented (scale out, pool size increase, etc.)
breakpoint_test_gates.documentation: Add to runbooks: 'If error rate increases above X%, check resource Y first'

must_pass: GATE-LOAD-002: All endpoints meet SLA at baseline 2500 users
GATE-LOAD-003: Connection pool stable at baseline
GATE-STRESS-003: System recovers within 2 min after stress
should_pass: GATE-STRESS-004: Graceful degradation validated at 5x peak
overall_test_completion_criteria.overall_success: All 'must pass' gates successful; >= 2 'should pass'; prepared for production deployment

nfr_register.title: Platform Concurrent User Capacity
nfr_register.reasoning: A nopCommerce e-commerce platform needs to support varying concurrent user loads throughout the day and during peak events (flash sales, seasonal shopping). The missing 'Page Load Time and Scalability' NFR identified by DeFOSPAM leaves no baseline for capacity planning. Industry benchmarks for mid-sized e-commerce: 400-600 average concurrent users, 2000-3000 peak during normal operations, scaling to 2500-5000 peak during promotional events. This platform lacks explicit requirements.
nfr_register.recommendation: Add NFR-CAP-001: 'The system SHALL support a minimum concurrent user capacity of 500 average users and 2,500 peak users during standard operations. During promotional periods and flash sales, the system SHALL elastically scale to handle up to 5,000 concurrent users. The platform SHALL monitor concurrent user load continuously and trigger auto-scaling when load exceeds 70% of current capacity. The system SHALL maintain response time SLAs (see NFR-LAT series) at all load levels up to peak capacity.'

nfr_register.title: Transaction Volume Per Feature
nfr_register.reasoning: Shopping Cart (F04) is a write-heavy feature with 2,500 average transactions/hour and potential 10,000 peak. This includes add-to-cart, quantity updates, coupon application, and cart removals. Checkout features (F10-F14) drive order creation at peak rates. Product Search (F05) at 2,000 average queries/hour requires efficient indexing. The system lacks defined transaction rate SLOs per feature, making capacity planning impossible.
nfr_register.recommendation: Add NFR-CAP-002: 'The system SHALL support: (1) Shopping Cart: 2,500 transactions/hour average (add-to-cart, update qty, apply coupon), 10,000 peak. (2) Product Search: 2,000 queries/hour average, 8,000 peak. (3) Checkout: 300 orders/hour average (complete transactions), 2,500 peak. (4) Product Display: 3,000 page views/hour average, 12,000 peak. Transaction processing SHALL be load-balanced across app servers, with write-optimized indexes on shopping_cart and orders tables. Database connection pool SHALL be sized to 200 total connections (120 for app tier).'

nfr_register.title: Database Storage Growth and Retention
nfr_register.feature: Order Management (F18), Product Catalog (F06-F07), Audit Logging (missing NFR)
nfr_register.reasoning: Database storage will grow significantly over 24 months: Orders grow at 30% YoY (100K initial rows to 400K in 24mo = 500GB to 2GB storage). Product images at 100K+ images consume 50GB+ storage. Audit logs (identified as missing NFR) grow at 45% YoY (3M rows, 3GB/year). No data retention policy exists (DeFOSPAM finding), meaning infinite data accumulation. Without growth projections and retention policies, storage costs become unbounded.
nfr_register.recommendation: Add NFR-CAP-003: 'The system SHALL implement data growth projections and storage management: (1) Order data: Support 400K orders (3GB) at 24 months with 30% YoY growth. (2) Product images: Support 100K+ images (50GB) at 24 months with 25% YoY growth. (3) Audit logs: Retain 730 days (2 years) maximum; auto-archive to cold storage after 90 days, delete after 730 days (3GB/year). (4) Reviews/ratings: Support 500K reviews (2GB) at 24 months with 40% YoY growth. (5) Database storage targets: 120GB at 12 months, 350GB at 24 months. (6) Implement automated backups: Daily full backups (retained 7 days), weekly backups (retained 4 weeks), monthly backups (retained 12 months).'

nfr_register.title: Web Server Sizing and Load Distribution
nfr_register.reasoning: Web tier (reverse proxy/load balancer + static content servers) must handle HTTP/HTTPS termination, SSL/TLS overhead, static asset serving (images, CSS, JS), and request routing to app tier. At 500 avg concurrent users with avg response time 200ms, each web server (assuming 4-core, 8GB RAM) can handle ~100-125 concurrent users. 500 avg / 125 = 4 web servers minimum. Peak 2500 concurrent users = 20 web servers needed, but traffic shaping and CDN can reduce this to 8-10 with caching.
nfr_register.recommendation: Add NFR-CAP-004: 'The system SHALL provision: (1) Web servers: 4 minimum (average load), auto-scale to 8-10 at peak (2,500+ concurrent users). Each web server: 4 vCPU, 8GB RAM, 50GB SSD. (2) Load balancer (hardware or cloud-native): Support 10 Gbps throughput, health checks every 5 seconds, connection timeout 30 seconds, request timeout 60 seconds. (3) Static content delivery: Serve static assets (images, CSS, JS) from CDN with 24-hour cache TTL for versioned assets, 1-hour TTL for HTML. (4) SSL/TLS termination: Use TLS 1.2+, terminate SSL at load balancer, enable session resumption. (5) Monitor: CPU <70%, memory <80%, disk I/O <60% at average load.'

nfr_register.title: Application Server Pool Sizing
nfr_register.reasoning: Application servers execute business logic, access databases, call external APIs (payment, shipping, address validation), and process transactions. At 500 avg concurrent users with ~50-100 ms average processing time per request, each app server (8-core, 16GB) can handle ~200-300 requests/second (depending on complexity). 1200 txn/hr avg = 0.33 txn/sec, but peak 8000 txn/hr = 2.22 txn/sec. Assuming 8 txn/sec per fully-loaded app server, need 6-8 app servers for capacity headroom.
nfr_register.recommendation: Add NFR-CAP-005: 'The system SHALL provision: (1) Application servers: 6 minimum (average load), auto-scale to 12-15 at peak. Each app server: 8 vCPU, 16GB RAM, 100GB SSD. (2) Application framework: Use thread-pool-based async or reactive framework to handle concurrent requests efficiently. Thread pool size: 200 threads per app server. (3) Database connection pool: 120 connections total (app tier share), max 20 connections per app server, connection timeout 30 seconds, idle timeout 900 seconds. (4) Session storage: Store sessions in Redis (distributed cache), session timeout 30 minutes. (5) Performance SLOs: p50 response time 100ms, p95 200ms, p99 500ms during average load. At peak load, p95 <1 second. (6) Health checks: Liveness check every 10 seconds, readiness check every 5 seconds, graceful shutdown with 30-second drain period.'

nfr_register.title: Database Primary and Replica Sizing
nfr_register.reasoning: Database is the system bottleneck. Primary handles all writes + read-heavy transactions. Current 15GB grows to 350GB at 24 months. At 1200 txn/hr avg (2.2 txn/min), peak 8000 txn/hr (133 txn/min), database must support sustained IOPS. Shopping cart/order writes are high-frequency and require low-latency storage (NVMe SSD). Replica for read-heavy queries (product catalog, search, reports, order history) reduces primary load.
nfr_register.recommendation: Add NFR-CAP-006: 'The system SHALL provision: (1) Primary database: 16 vCPU, 64GB RAM, 1000GB NVMe SSD. Database: MySQL 8.0 InnoDB or PostgreSQL 14+. (2) Read replica: 16 vCPU, 64GB RAM, 1000GB SSD, async replication lag <1 second. Route read-only queries (product catalog, search, reports) to replica. (3) Storage: Support 120GB at 12 months, 350GB at 24 months. Plan for 500GB by 36 months. Use tiered storage: hot (NVMe) for active data, warm (SSD) for historical orders, cold (archive) for audit logs >90 days. (4) Backup strategy: Daily full backup (7-day retention), weekly backup (4-week retention), monthly backup (12-month retention). RPO: 1 hour, RTO: 15 minutes. (5) Monitoring: Query response time p95 <500ms (primary), p95 <1000ms (replica), lock wait time <100ms, replication lag <1 second. (6) Connection pool: 200 total connections (app tier 120, admin/reporting 30, backup 20).'

nfr_register.title: Cache Layer (Redis) Sizing and Strategy
nfr_register.feature: Shopping Cart (F04), Product Catalog (F06-F07), User Authentication (F02/F17), Session Management
nfr_register.reasoning: Cache layer (Redis) is critical for reducing database load, improving response times, and supporting session management. At 500 concurrent users, assume 1-2MB per session = 500-1000MB session storage. Product catalog (10K products + attributes) = 500MB. Shopping carts (avg 100K carts in-flight) = 500MB-1GB. Total estimate: 2-3GB working set. For peak 2500 users, estimate 10-15GB total cache.
nfr_register.recommendation: Add NFR-CAP-007: 'The system SHALL provision Redis cache layer: (1) Cluster topology: 2 Redis nodes (master-replica) with 32GB RAM per node (64GB total), 50GB SSD per node. (2) Eviction policy: LRU (Least Recently Used) with TTL. (3) Use cases: (a) Session storage: TTL 30 minutes, 500-1000MB. (b) Shopping cart data: TTL 24 hours, 500MB-1GB. (c) Product catalog cache: TTL 12 hours, 500MB (refresh on product updates). (d) Category hierarchies: TTL 24 hours, 50MB. (e) Exchange rates: TTL 6 hours, 5MB. (f) User preferences: TTL 7 days, 100MB. (4) Replication: Async replication from master to replica, replication lag <100ms. (5) Persistence: RDB snapshots every 60 seconds, AOF for critical data (sessions, carts). (6) Monitoring: Hit rate >80%, memory usage <80%, replication lag <100ms. (7) Failover: Automatic failover with Sentinel or Redis Cluster, recovery time <10 seconds.'

nfr_register.title: Content Delivery Network (CDN) Strategy
nfr_register.reasoning: CDN reduces origin load by caching static assets (images, CSS, JS) at edge locations close to users. Product Display (F07) and Product Catalog (F06) are image-heavy, with 100K+ images totaling 50GB+. At 500 avg concurrent users viewing 3-5 images per session = 1500-2500 image requests/hour. CDN cache hit rate >90% can reduce origin bandwidth by 90% and improve page load times by 50-70%. Estimated egress traffic: 100GB/month without CDN, 10GB/month with CDN (90% reduction).
nfr_register.recommendation: Add NFR-CAP-008: 'The system SHALL implement CDN for static content delivery: (1) Provider: CloudFront, Cloudflare, or Akamai with 200+ edge locations. (2) Cacheable content (60% of traffic): Product images (JPEG, PNG, WebP), CSS, JavaScript, fonts, SVG icons. (3) Cache TTLs: (a) Versioned assets (with hash in filename): 365 days. (b) Product images: 12 hours (refresh on product update). (c) HTML: 5 minutes (dynamic content). (d) API responses: 1 minute (for read-heavy endpoints). (4) Cache headers: Set Cache-Control, ETag, Last-Modified for optimal caching. (5) Compression: Enable gzip/brotli for text assets, serve WebP for images. (6) Performance targets: Cache hit rate >85%, edge latency p95 <100ms, origin latency p95 <500ms. (7) Estimated cost: $3,000/month for 100GB egress, 5TB storage. (8) Monitoring: Cache hit ratio, origin request rate, DDoS protection metrics.'

nfr_register.title: Network Bandwidth and Egress Cost
nfr_register.reasoning: Network bandwidth is a significant cloud cost driver. At 500 avg concurrent users, avg page size 2MB, avg session duration 20 minutes = ~300MB data transfer/user/session = 150GB/day = 4500GB/month. With peak 2500 users = 22.5TB/month peak. Without CDN caching (which provides 90% reduction), egress cost is $0.085/GB = $1,912/month for average, $19,125/month for peak. With CDN, egress cost drops to ~$200/month + CDN cost $3000/month = $3200 vs $1900 without optimization. Cost optimization is critical.
nfr_register.recommendation: Add NFR-CAP-009: 'The system SHALL optimize network bandwidth and forecast egress costs: (1) Baseline traffic: 150GB/day average, 750GB/day peak (without CDN optimization). (2) With CDN caching (90% reduction): 15GB/day average origin traffic, 75GB/day peak origin traffic. (3) Monthly egress cost projection: (a) Without optimization: $1,912/month average, $19,125/month peak = $17,000/month annual average. (b) With CDN (preferred): $200/month origin egress + $3,000/month CDN = $3,200/month = $38,400/year. (c) Savings: 82% reduction in egress cost with CDN. (4) Additional costs: DDoS protection ($500/month), IP transit ($200/month). (5) Total network cost budget: $4,000/month ($48,000/year). (6) Optimization strategies: (a) Serve images in WebP format (30% smaller than JPEG). (b) Implement aggressive caching (24-hour TTL for product images). (c) Use CloudFront or Cloudflare for edge caching. (d) Compress text responses (gzip/brotli). (e) Lazy-load images on product pages. (f) Paginate results (product listings, reviews). (7) Monitoring: Track egress bandwidth, CDN hit ratio, page size, image optimization metrics.'

nfr_register.title: Auto-Scaling Policy and Elasticity
nfr_register.reasoning: E-commerce platforms experience highly variable traffic: weekend spikes, seasonal events (Black Friday, holidays), flash sales. Without auto-scaling policies, system either over-provisions (expensive) or underprovisioning (poor UX during peak). Auto-scaling should trigger on CPU >70%, memory >75%, or response time p95 >1s. Scale-down should be conservative (prevent thrashing) with 5-10 minute cooldown.
nfr_register.recommendation: Add NFR-CAP-010: 'The system SHALL implement auto-scaling policies: (1) Web tier (reverse proxy/load balancer): Min 4 instances, max 10 instances. Scale up: CPU >70% for 2 minutes or request latency p95 >500ms for 1 minute. Scale down: CPU <40% for 5 minutes. (2) App tier: Min 6 instances, max 15 instances. Scale up: CPU >75% for 2 minutes or queue depth >10 pending requests. Scale down: CPU <50% for 5 minutes. (3) Database: Min 1 read replica, max 3 replicas. Scale up: Replica lag >2 seconds or CPU >80% for 3 minutes. No auto-scale down for replicas (stateful). (4) Cache tier: Min 2 nodes (master-replica), max 5 nodes. Scale up: Memory >80% or eviction rate >10%. Scale down: Memory <50% for 10 minutes. (5) Scaling launch time: Web tier <2 minutes, app tier <3 minutes (due to JVM warmup), cache <1 minute. (6) Cost optimization: Use reserved instances for 60% of capacity (minimum baseline), spot instances for scale-up (save 70% vs on-demand). (7) Monitoring: Trigger alerts on scale-up/down events, failed scale attempts, cascade failures (e.g., app tier scale fails due to DB connection limits).'

nfr_register.title: Audit Logging Infrastructure Capacity
nfr_register.reasoning: DeFOSPAM identified 'Missing Audit Logging for Sensitive Actions' as a critical gap. Audit logs must record all sensitive actions: customer account changes, payment transactions, refunds, admin discounts, inventory adjustments, admin login/logout. Assuming 1200 txn/hour avg = 1 audit event per transaction + 100 customer account updates/hour = ~1300 audit events/hour = ~31K/day. Each log entry ~300 bytes = 9.3MB/day = 280MB/month = 3.36GB/year. Immutable, tamper-proof storage required for compliance (PCI-DSS, SOC 2).
nfr_register.recommendation: Add NFR-CAP-011: 'The system SHALL provision audit logging infrastructure: (1) Audit log volume: 1,300 events/hour average (1 per transaction + customer updates), 8,000 events/hour peak. (2) Storage: ~3GB at 12 months, 5-10GB at 24 months depending on log retention. (3) Audit log schema: timestamp, user_id, action_type (CREATE, UPDATE, DELETE), resource_type, resource_id, old_value, new_value, ip_address, session_id, status (success/failure), error_message. (4) Immutability: Write audit logs to append-only table or AWS CloudTrail / Google Cloud Audit Logs equivalent. Use cryptographic hashing (SHA-256) to detect tampering. (5) Retention: Hot storage (database) for 90 days, warm storage (S3/archive) for 730 days (2 years per PCI-DSS), cold storage (glacier) for long-term archive. (6) Access control: Audit logs readable only by authorized admins and compliance officers; deletion strictly prohibited. (7) Performance: Audit logging must not block business transactions. Use async/fire-and-forget pattern or separate logging service. (8) Compliance: Support PCI-DSS Requirement 10 (logging and monitoring), SOC 2 Type II audit readiness, GDPR right-to-audit. (9) Cost: Estimate $200/month hot storage, $50/month warm storage, $20/month cold storage = $270/month for audit infrastructure.'

nfr_register.title: Backup and Disaster Recovery Infrastructure
nfr_register.reasoning: Database backup and disaster recovery are essential for business continuity. Database grows from 15GB (current) to 120GB at 24 months. Backup strategy: daily full backup (7-day retention) = 120GB, weekly backup (4-week retention) = 4x120GB = 480GB, monthly backup (12-month retention) = 12x120GB = 1440GB. Total storage: ~2TB for backups. Recovery targets: RPO (Recovery Point Objective) <1 hour (acceptable data loss), RTO (Recovery Time Objective) <15 minutes (downtime acceptable for brief maintenance).
nfr_register.recommendation: Add NFR-CAP-012: 'The system SHALL implement backup and disaster recovery: (1) Backup strategy: (a) Daily full database backup at 2 AM UTC, retained 7 days. (b) Weekly full backup at 1 AM UTC Sunday, retained 4 weeks. (c) Monthly full backup on 1st at 1 AM UTC, retained 12 months. (d) Transaction log backup every 15 minutes, retained 7 days. (2) Backup storage: 2TB total (daily 120GB × 7 = 840GB, weekly 480GB, monthly 1440GB, 7 days transaction logs 50GB). Use tiered storage: local fast storage for 7-day backups, S3/cloud storage for weekly/monthly, glacier for >90-day archives. (3) Backup testing: Monthly restore test to alternate infrastructure, documented and approved. (4) RPO/RTO targets: RPO 1 hour (acceptable data loss), RTO 15 minutes (time to restore from backup). (5) Disaster recovery: Primary data center (current region) with read replica. Secondary data center (different region) with read-only replica, updated asynchronously. Failover time <30 minutes (manual) or <5 minutes (automated). (6) Replication strategy: Async replication from primary to secondary (tolerate <1 hour data loss in worst case). (7) Backup cost: $50/month local storage, $100/month cloud storage (S3), $20/month archive (glacier) = $170/month ($2,040/year). (8) Monitoring: Backup success rate 100%, restore test success 100%, replication lag <1 minute.'

nfr_register.title: Define Revenue-Critical Availability Targets (99.99%)
nfr_register.feature: Checkout Process (All 5 Steps), Payment Processing, Order Confirmation
nfr_register.reasoning: Checkout and payment processing are direct revenue paths. Every minute of downtime directly loses customer transactions and revenue. Industry standard for e-commerce is 99.99%. DeFOSPAM flagged critical gaps: Payment Failure Recovery, Payment Gateway Decline/Failure Recovery, Invalid Payment Gateway Response - all suggest current system cannot tolerate payment service failures. 99.99% target (4 nines) is required.
nfr_register.recommendation: Deploy checkout and payment services in active-active configuration across 2+ geographic regions. Implement multi-region database replication with synchronous writes to ensure zero data loss. Integrate with 2+ payment gateway providers with automatic failover. Implement circuit breaker pattern for payment gateway integration. RTO=5min requires automated health detection and traffic rerouting (DNS failover or load balancer).
implementation_steps: 1. Provision secondary data center in different geographic region
2. Set up synchronous database replication between regions
3. Configure GeoDNS or intelligent load balancer with health checks
4. Integrate backup payment gateway provider
5. Implement payment gateway fallback logic
6. Establish health check monitoring (30-second interval)
7. Run failover drills monthly

nfr_register.title: Define High-Value Service Availability Targets (99.95%)
nfr_register.feature: Product Catalog, Product Search, User Authentication, Shopping Cart
nfr_register.reasoning: Product catalog, search, and authentication enable customer discovery and account access. These services are essential but less time-critical than payment. Downtime prevents customers from discovering products but guests can still checkout (REQ086). Authentication outage impacts returning customers but doesn't immediately lose in-flight transactions. 99.95% (3.5 nines) is appropriate for these services.
nfr_register.recommendation: Deploy catalog and authentication services with active-passive failover. Primary data center handles all traffic. Secondary data center is standby replica with real-time replication. On primary failure, automated failover redirects traffic (RTO 15 minutes). Session data must be persisted to distributed cache or database (not in-memory only on single server). Implement circuit breaker for external catalog data sources.
implementation_steps: 1. Migrate session storage from in-memory to Redis cluster or database
2. Set up read replica database for catalog data
3. Configure automated failover for primary -> secondary promotion
4. Implement health checks for catalog and auth services
5. Set up load balancer failover policy
6. Test failover every 2 weeks

nfr_register.title: Define Secondary Service Availability Targets (99.9%)
nfr_register.feature: Wishlist, Reviews, CMS Content (News/Blog), Customer Support
nfr_register.reasoning: Wishlist, reviews, and CMS content are important for customer experience but non-transactional. Downtime does not prevent commerce. Customer support communication can be queued. 99.9% (2 nines) allows for scheduled maintenance and graceful degradation. RTO of 60 minutes acceptable for these services as they do not block revenue.
nfr_register.recommendation: Use blue-green deployment strategy for these services. Maintain two identical production environments. Deploy updates to inactive environment, run tests, then switch traffic with zero-downtime. Acceptable downtime for maintenance is 60 minutes. Not suitable for handling unplanned failures - for that use backup to secondary data center.
implementation_steps: 1. Deploy blue-green infrastructure for wishlist, reviews, CMS
2. Implement automated smoke tests for green environment
3. Configure instant traffic switch capability
4. Schedule maintenance windows: weekly, Tuesday 2-4am UTC
5. Announce maintenance 7 days in advance
6. Keep blue environment as rollback option

nfr_register.title: Eliminate Single Point of Failure: Payment Gateway Provider
nfr_register.reasoning: DeFOSPAM explicitly flagged: 'Missing Payment Gateway Decline/Failure Recovery' and 'Missing Error Handling for Invalid Payment Gateway Response'. Current requirements (REQ090-091) specify payment processing but provide NO fallback if primary gateway fails. Single payment gateway = revenue-blocking single point of failure. Even 1 minute of payment processing downtime is unacceptable for e-commerce.
nfr_register.recommendation: Integrate 2+ independent payment gateway providers (e.g., Stripe primary + PayPal backup). Implement payment orchestration layer that: (1) Detects payment gateway failures within 10 seconds, (2) Automatically routes to backup provider, (3) Implements retry logic with exponential backoff, (4) Queues payments for later processing if both providers fail. Design for idempotency: retrying payment must not double-charge customer.
implementation_steps: 1. Sign up for 2 payment gateway providers (Stripe + PayPal recommended)
2. Implement payment orchestration service with provider abstraction
3. Add health checks for each provider (ping every 30 seconds)
4. Implement automatic failover logic
5. Queue failed payments to async job system
6. Implement retry mechanism: exponential backoff, max 3 retries
7. Add customer notification: 'payment processing, confirmation email sent'
8. Test failover: simulate primary provider failure, verify traffic routes to backup

nfr_register.title: Define Recovery Time Objective (RTO): 5 Minutes for Revenue-Critical Services
nfr_register.reasoning: 5 minutes RTO for 99.99% availability equals: downtime_budget = 0.44 hours/year = 26.3 minutes/year. 5-minute RTO means on average we can tolerate ~5 failures per year (5 min × 5 = 25 min). RTO of 5 minutes is industry-standard for revenue-critical services. Requires automated health detection (30-60 sec) and traffic rerouting (30-60 sec). Manual intervention cannot meet 5-minute RTO.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement automated failover with RTO of 5 minutes: (1) Health checks every 30 seconds detect failure, (2) Load balancer or DNS removes failed server from rotation within 30 seconds, (3) In-flight requests timeout and retry, (4) Automated traffic shift to healthy region/instance. This requires: health monitoring infrastructure, automated failover orchestration (Kubernetes, Terraform, or custom scripts), and multi-region deployment.
implementation_steps: 1. Deploy health check infrastructure (Kubernetes probes or custom)
2. Configure health check frequency: 30-second interval
3. Set failure threshold: 2 consecutive failed checks = remove from rotation
4. Implement load balancer failover policy
5. Test RTO: simulate server failure, measure time to traffic shift
6. Document failure recovery procedures
7. Run failure drills monthly

nfr_register.title: Define Recovery Point Objective (RPO): Zero Data Loss for Orders and Payments
nfr_register.feature: Order Management, Payment Processing, Order Confirmation
nfr_register.reasoning: Orders and payment transactions are financial records. Losing even one transaction = audit failure, compliance violation, and customer dispute. RPO=0 means: in event of failure, zero transactions can be lost. This requires synchronous replication (both primary and secondary have identical data before committing). DeFOSPAM flagged race condition in order confirmation - suggests current system does not ensure atomic writes to both regions.
nfr_register.recommendation: Use synchronous database replication with strict consistency: (1) Checkpoint-based replication: writes to primary are not committed until secondary acknowledges, (2) Distributed transactions: order write spans multiple data centers atomically, (3) Event-driven consistency: order creation generates event that must be replicated before commitment. Configure database with RPO=0 settings: synchronous replication lag <100ms, write acknowledgment required from 2 nodes, commit waits for quorum.
implementation_steps: 1. Configure database replication: synchronous mode, not async
2. Set replication lag target: <100ms
3. Require write acknowledgment from 2+ replicas before commit
4. Implement transaction journal: persist every order write to WAL
5. Test RPO: simulate secondary failure, verify no data loss
6. Monitor replication lag continuously
7. Alert if replication lag exceeds 1 second

nfr_register.title: Implement Failover Strategy for Payment Decline Recovery
nfr_register.reasoning: DeFOSPAM flagged: 'Missing Scenario for Payment Gateway Decline/Failure Recovery' and 'Missing Outcome: Payment Failure Recovery'. Current requirements (REQ090-091) do NOT specify what happens when payment is declined (insufficient funds, invalid card, etc.) or when payment gateway fails. No recovery procedure means customers get stuck in checkout, transactions are lost, and staff cannot recover them.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement payment failure recovery workflow: (1) When payment declines, capture decline reason and code from gateway, (2) Immediately attempt retry with backup gateway, (3) If both decline, queue payment in durable queue with customer notification, (4) Display clear error to customer with recovery options: try different card, contact support, (5) Implement async retry: retry queued payments hourly for 24 hours, (6) Provide admin dashboard to manually retry failed payments. Design for graceful degradation: payment service failure is not checkout failure.
implementation_steps: 1. Design payment failure state machine: decline, retry, queue, manual_review
2. Implement payment decline response handler
3. Build retry logic: exponential backoff, max 3 attempts
4. Create payment queue: durable queue for failed payments
5. Implement async retry worker: process queue hourly
6. Build admin UI: view failed payments, manually retry/refund
7. Add customer notification: email when payment fails, when retry succeeds
8. Test: simulate payment decline scenarios, verify recovery
