nfr_register.title: Implement Redundancy for In-Flight Order Data (Session/Cart)
nfr_register.reasoning: If session or cart data is stored only in single application server's memory, server failure = session lost = customer must restart checkout. Losing customer's in-progress order is unacceptable for 99.99% availability. Current requirements do not specify session storage or persistence. DeFOSPAM flagged race conditions suggesting in-memory state without distributed coordination.
nfr_register.recommendation: Migrate session and cart storage from in-memory to distributed cache (Redis cluster) or database with cross-region replication. Design for failover: customer session accessible from any server (no sticky sessions dependency). If server fails, customer retries request to healthy server and session is reconstructed from distributed cache. RTO for session loss <5 minutes (customer re-creates cart or logs in again).
implementation_steps: 1. Deploy Redis cluster with 3+ nodes across multiple availability zones
2. Configure Redis replication: master-slave or cluster mode
3. Migrate session storage: application -> Redis
4. Implement session persistence: 30-minute TTL, auto-refresh on activity
5. Remove sticky session requirement: load balancer can route to any server
6. Test failover: kill Redis node, verify sessions survive
7. Monitor Redis cluster health

nfr_register.title: Implement Distributed Locking to Prevent Stock Race Condition
nfr_register.reasoning: DeFOSPAM flagged: 'Race Condition in Stock Validation at Order Confirmation'. Two customers can simultaneously validate inventory, both see product in stock, both complete checkout, but stock quantity only decrements once - resulting in overselling. No distributed lock mechanism specified means system cannot prevent this. This violates inventory accuracy and can cause order fulfillment failure.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement distributed locking for inventory updates using: (1) Database row-level locks (SELECT...FOR UPDATE), (2) Redis locks (SETNX + Lua script), or (3) Optimistic locking with version numbers. At order confirmation, acquire exclusive lock on product inventory, decrement stock atomically, then release lock. If lock cannot be acquired within timeout (5 seconds), transaction fails and customer receives error: 'product no longer available, please refresh'.
implementation_steps: 1. Choose locking mechanism: database locks or Redis locks
2. Implement inventory lock in order confirmation workflow
3. Set lock timeout: 5 seconds (fail if cannot acquire)
4. Add inventory version column for optimistic locking fallback
5. Test race condition: concurrent orders for single item, verify no overselling
6. Monitor lock contention: alert if lock timeouts exceed 1%
7. Design customer error message: clear, actionable (refresh & retry)

nfr_register.title: Implement Asynchronous Refund Processing Workflow
nfr_register.reasoning: DeFOSPAM flagged: 'Missing Completeness: Refund Processing Workflow Not Specified'. Requirements define order display (REQ099-107) but NO specification of customer refund requests, admin approval, payment reversal processing, or restocking. Without refund workflow, failed refunds are not retried, refund status is unknown to customers, and products cannot be restocked - creating operational and financial nightmares.
nfr_register.recommendation: Design refund processing as durable async workflow: (1) Customer or admin initiates refund request (creates refund record), (2) Async worker processes refund: calls payment gateway API to reverse transaction, (3) If refund fails, retry hourly for 24 hours, (4) On refund success: update order status, restock inventory, send confirmation email, (5) Manual intervention queue for failed refunds. Design for idempotency: processing same refund twice must not double-refund.
implementation_steps: 1. Design refund state machine: requested, processing, success, failed, manual_review
2. Implement refund request API: customer/admin can initiate
3. Build async refund worker: process refund queue hourly
4. Implement payment gateway refund API calls with idempotency key
5. Add inventory restock on refund success
6. Build admin dashboard: view refunds, manually retry failed ones
7. Implement customer notification: email on refund status changes
8. Test idempotency: retry same refund, verify no double-refund

nfr_register.title: Implement Tax Calculation Service Resilience with Fallback
nfr_register.reasoning: DeFOSPAM flagged: 'Unpredictable Tax Calculation Timing and Order Total Accuracy'. Tax calculation service (TaxJar, Avalara, etc.) is external dependency that can fail or timeout. If tax service fails and no fallback exists, order total cannot be calculated and checkout fails. Current requirements (REQ089) specify tax calculation but provide NO error handling or fallback strategy.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement tax service resilience: (1) Set timeout of 5 seconds for tax service call, (2) If timeout or failure, use last-known cached tax rate for customer's jurisdiction, (3) Cache tax rates locally with hourly refresh, (4) Implement circuit breaker: if tax service fails 3x in a row, enter degraded mode (use cache only), (5) Implement fallback: if no cache available, display order total as 'tax TBD' and calculate in post-order email.
implementation_steps: 1. Implement tax rate cache: local database with jurisdiction -> tax rate
2. Add hourly cache refresh job: fetch latest rates from tax service
3. Set timeout on tax service calls: 5 seconds, fail-fast
4. Implement circuit breaker: track failures, enter degraded mode after 3 consecutive failures
5. On timeout/failure: use cached rate as fallback
6. Monitor tax service health: alert if degraded mode active
7. Test failover: simulate tax service timeout, verify fallback rate used
8. Document fallback behavior in customer terms

nfr_register.title: Implement Email Service Resilience for Order Confirmations
nfr_register.feature: Order Confirmation, User Registration, Password Reset
nfr_register.reasoning: DeFOSPAM flagged: 'Missing Outcome: Payment Failure Recovery', 'Missing Outcome: Customer Registration Confirmation', 'Missing Outcome: Password Reset Email and Verification'. Email is critical for: order confirmations, registration confirmations, password resets. Email service failures (SendGrid outage, SMTP relay down) can block customer onboarding and leave customers unable to reset passwords. No backup email provider or queueing mechanism specified.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement email service resilience: (1) Use async email queue (never send synchronously), (2) Integrate primary and backup email providers (SendGrid primary, AWS SES backup), (3) On primary provider failure, automatically route to backup, (4) Implement exponential backoff retry: if both fail, queue for later processing, (5) Set email retention: 7 days in queue (customer can re-request), (6) Provide in-app notifications as fallback (show order confirmation in customer account if email fails).
implementation_steps: 1. Implement async email queue (Redis queue or database)
2. Integrate primary email provider (SendGrid, Mailgun)
3. Integrate backup email provider (AWS SES)
4. Implement email worker: process queue asynchronously
5. Add provider failover logic: detect primary failure, route to backup
6. Implement retry logic: exponential backoff, max 5 retries over 7 days
7. Add customer notification: show confirmation in account, email will be sent
8. Monitor email delivery: track success rate, alert on failures >1%
9. Test failover: simulate primary provider outage, verify emails route to backup

nfr_register.title: Critical: 24-Hour Soak Test Required to Detect Memory Leaks Before Production
nfr_register.reasoning: Ada's capacity model indicates 112 vCPU, 272 GB RAM infrastructure. Liskov identified database connection pool as bottleneck (breaks at 5K users). Yao flagged 513ms security overhead. Previous agents did not specify soak testing, yet DeFOSPAM scenarios include 25+ features. Long-running production will expose memory leaks (request objects retained, cache unbounded growth, connection leaks) that short load tests miss. Industry best practice: 24-hour soak minimum before production. nopCommerce must validate no runaway memory growth or connection exhaustion.
nfr_register.recommendation: Execute SOAK-001: 24-hour soak at 2500 concurrent users (peak baseline), gradually ramp up 100->2500 over 30 min, sustain 23 hours, ramp down 10 min. Monitor: heap memory (< 50MB/hour growth after 2h warm-up), database connections (stable at 120), file handles (< 5000), thread count (stable), cache hit rate (> 85%), error rate (< 0.1%). Measure p95 response time hour 0-1 vs hour 23-24; accept max 5% degradation. Retest after any memory leak fixes.

nfr_register.title: Critical: Database Connection Pool Exhaustion Risk at 5K+ Concurrent Users
nfr_register.feature: All features (Shopping Cart, Order Management, Inventory Validation)
nfr_register.reasoning: Liskov's NFR-SCL-007 identified: current pool size 200 is insufficient for 5K users. App tier allocates 120 connections (20/server * 6 servers). At peak 8000 tx/hr = 2.2 tx/sec. If avg query takes 50ms, max throughput = (120 connections * 20 queries/sec per conn) = 2400 tx/hr. But real queries average 100-200ms (including lock wait), reducing effective throughput. At 5K users with longer transactions, connection pool queue builds instantly. New connections rejected with 'Connection pool exhausted' error. This is a hard failure mode, not graceful degradation.
nfr_register.recommendation: IMMEDIATE ACTION: (1) Before scaling to 5K users, increase pool size from 200 to 300-400. (2) Implement PgBouncer (connection pooling middleware) on each app server to multiplex app connections to DB pool. (3) Execute STRESS-001 (2x peak, 5000 users): monitor connection pool utilization; must stay < 80% of 200-connection pool (<=160). (4) Stress test target: at 5000 users, active connections < 160; queue length < 10; no 'pool exhausted' errors. (5) For production: deploy PgBouncer in session mode (multiplexes connections); supports unlimited app-tier connections to single DB connection pool.

nfr_register.title: Critical: Race Condition in Stock Validation Under Concurrent Load Must Be Tested
nfr_register.feature: Shopping Cart, Order Confirmation, Inventory Management
nfr_register.reasoning: Liskov's NFR-SCL-008 flagged critical race condition: 'Multiple customers simultaneously add the same limited-stock product to cart and reach order confirmation. Between cart add validation and order confirmation, stock depletes (stock can go negative).' nopCommerce runs without pessimistic row-level locking (FOR UPDATE clause). At peak 2500 users, ~250 customers may add same product to cart concurrently. Without locking, two customers add last unit; both reach checkout; both are allowed to confirm; inventory goes negative; both receive email 'order placed'; one customer's order becomes 'unfulfillable'. This causes: (1) Overselling, (2) Refund chaos, (3) NPS impact. Must be tested and fixed.
nfr_register.recommendation: Test SPIKE-002: Simulate 100 concurrent users adding same product (stock=5) to cart over 30s (spike). Measure: (1) How many complete checkout successfully? (2) Does inventory go negative? (3) Does 'out of stock' error appear? Execute against current code (will fail); then test after fix (pessimistic locking with FOR UPDATE). Success criteria: no negative inventory, no overselling, graceful 'out of stock' message after limit reached. Retest at 2x peak (5000 users) to ensure lock contention doesn't cause timeout.

nfr_register.title: Critical: 72-Hour Extended Soak Required to Detect Cumulative Leaks and Pattern-Based Issues
nfr_register.reasoning: 24-hour soak catches hourly patterns. 72-hour soak catches daily and weekly patterns (nightly batch jobs, weekly maintenance, scheduled cron jobs). Risk: batch job to clean abandoned carts runs daily at 2 AM; if cleanup fails, carts accumulate indefinitely, bloating cart table and slowing INSERT performance. Similarly, session cleanup job must run hourly; if it fails silently, sessions accumulate in Redis, consuming memory. Session lookup latency increases from 30ms to 100ms+ as Redis hits memory limit and eviction rate spikes. These issues emerge only after 24-48 hours of continuous load.
nfr_register.recommendation: Execute SOAK-002: 72-hour soak at 2500 users. Window 1 (0-24h): establish baseline. Window 2 (24-48h): compare to baseline; identify daily pattern impacts. Window 3 (48-72h): identify cumulative issues. Monitor: (1) Memory trend across 3 windows (should be stable, not drifting up); (2) Cart table row count (should drop after cleanup job runs); (3) Session count in Redis (should be stable ~2500 max); (4) Query latency (should be stable; if climbs from 30ms to 50ms by hour 72, indicates table bloat). Verify all cleanup jobs execute successfully (add logging). Retest after fixes to cleanup job logic.

nfr_register.title: Major: Stress Test at 2x Peak (5000 Users) Must Validate Horizontal Scaling Assumptions
nfr_register.reasoning: Ada's baseline: 4 web, 6 app, 2 DB servers handle 2500 peak users. Target: 5000 users. Liskov recommends scaling to 8 web, 10 app servers. But this is untested. Horizontal scaling has subtle issues: (1) Session affinity: if sticky sessions enabled, session loss on server restart; (2) Cache coherency: product cache updated on server A, server B serves stale data; (3) Load balancer health checks: if LB uses /health endpoint, all servers respond 'healthy' even when database is slow; (4) Connection pool sharing: if all 10 app servers try to use 200-connection pool, contention increases.
nfr_register.recommendation: Execute STRESS-001: Ramp to 5000 concurrent users over 5 min; sustain 60 min; ramp down 5 min. Measure CPU, memory, connection pool, queue depth, response time per endpoint. Success criteria: (1) Error rate < 1%, (2) p95 SLA compliance > 95% (no more than 5% of requests exceed Noyce's budgets), (3) CPU utilization 70-75% on web tier (indicates headroom for spikes), (4) DB connection pool < 180 of 200 (not maxed), (5) p95 response time returns to baseline within 2 min after ramp-down. If any fail: identify bottleneck (CPU saturation? DB pool? Thread pool?); add more servers; retest.

nfr_register.title: Major: Stress Test at 5x Peak (12500 Users) to Validate Graceful Degradation Strategy
nfr_register.reasoning: At 5x peak (12500 users), system cannot maintain all SLAs. Response times will exceed budgets; some features will timeout. But system should degrade gracefully: critical features (Checkout, Payment) must succeed; non-critical features (Recommendations, Wishlists) can fail or queue. Yao flagged missing circuit breakers for payment gateway. If payment gateway times out and no circuit breaker, all checkout requests queue waiting for payment timeout; queue fills; requests drop; customers lose their carts. With circuit breaker, payment timeout triggers circuit open; checkout returns 'Service temporarily busy'; customer retries in 30s; circuit closes; payment succeeds.
nfr_register.recommendation: Execute STRESS-002: Ramp to 12500 users over 10 min; sustain 50 min; ramp down. Measure per-feature success rates. Expected: (1) Checkout success rate > 80% (vs 99% at baseline); (2) Payment auth success > 95% (critical); (3) Product search success rate > 90% (can tolerate occasional timeout); (4) Recommendation success rate > 70% (non-critical, can fail). CRITICAL: test circuit breaker for payment gateway. Inject failure (timeout all payment requests). Measure: (1) Do checkout transactions fail gracefully (show 'retry' button) or hang indefinitely? (2) Does circuit breaker open after N failures? (3) Do new payment attempts fail fast (not queue indefinitely)? Implement circuit breaker if missing.

nfr_register.title: Critical: Breakpoint Test at 10x Peak Identifies Absolute System Limits
nfr_register.reasoning: System will eventually break under extreme load. Better to find breaking point in test than in production. At 25000 concurrent users, first hard resource limit to hit is likely: (1) Database connection pool completely exhausted (all 200 connections in use, new ones rejected), (2) File descriptor limit (Linux default ~65k, each connection uses 2-3 FDs: network socket + database connection), (3) Memory exhaustion (OOM killer terminates process), or (4) Load balancer connection limit (~200k concurrent connections). Knowing which breaks first informs capacity planning (e.g., 'If error rate jumps above 50%, first check DB connection pool').
nfr_register.recommendation: Execute STRESS-003 (Breakpoint Test): Ramp load progressively from 500 users, increment +2500 every 10 min, measure metrics at each step. Target: find the user count where error rate jumps from < 1% to > 50%. Record: (1) User count at breakpoint, (2) Which resource is exhausted first (DB connections? Memory? FDs?), (3) Error messages (Connection pool exhausted? Out of memory? Too many open files?). Produce graph: response time vs load (should show knee at 2x-5x peak). Documentation: 'If production error rate > 50%, restart with reduced load; check DB pool size first; may need connection pooling middleware.' Use this data to set alerting thresholds.

nfr_register.title: Major: Resource Leak Risk - Memory Accumulation in Redis Cache Layer
nfr_register.feature: Session Management, Product Search, Shopping Cart
nfr_register.reasoning: Liskov's NFR-SCL-004 specified: baseline 2 Redis servers, 64 GB total cache. At 2500 peak concurrent users, cache demand ~12-15 GB (2500 sessions * 1.5 KB + product catalog 8-10 GB). LRU eviction policy should maintain cache at max capacity. Risk: if eviction policy not enabled or improperly tuned, cache grows unbounded until memory exhausted. At 24 hours, cache memory should plateau at ~12 GB; if growing > 100 MB/hour, indicates misconfigured eviction or unbounded data structure (e.g., session data not deleted, only inserted).
nfr_register.recommendation: During SOAK-001: monitor Redis memory metrics. Baseline: memory usage should climb 0-2h (warm-up), then plateau. If memory grows > 100 MB/hour after 4h, investigate. Check: (1) LRU eviction policy enabled (CONFIG GET maxmemory-policy should return 'allkeys-lru')? (2) maxmemory setting = 64GB? (3) Session TTL enforced (EXPIRE key 1800 seconds = 30 min)? (4) Product cache TTL reasonable (< 24 hours, not infinite)? Retest after fixes. For production, set Redis memory alert at 50 GB (80% of 64 GB); trigger manual eviction or cache flush if limit reached.

nfr_register.title: Major: Resource Leak Risk - Database Connection Pool Leak from Exception Handling
nfr_register.reasoning: Common Java bug: exception during database transaction; catch block doesn't call connection.close() (or doesn't use try-with-resources). Connection reserved but never returned to pool. Under sustained load, pool slowly accumulates leaked connections. After 24 hours, pool exhaustion causes all new transactions to wait or fail. Detection: monitor active_connections metric in connection pool. Should be stable at ~60-80 at 2500 users; if grows to 150+ by hour 12, leak likely. Risk is amplified if exceptions are common (e.g., inventory validation fails often due to race condition).
nfr_register.recommendation: During SOAK-001: monitor connection pool metrics (active_connections, queue_length) continuously. If queue_length > 5 or active_connections > 140 of 200, trigger alert. Investigate: enable SQL logging to identify which queries fail (constraint violations, timeouts?); review exception handlers to ensure all connection.close() calls in finally blocks or try-with-resources. Static analysis: run SonarQube / FindBugs to find unclosed connections. Retest after fixes. For production: set alert at active_connections > 150 of 200 (75%); immediately investigate root cause.

nfr_register.title: Major: Test Data Requirements Must Match Production Scale for Meaningful Results
nfr_register.reasoning: Test data size dramatically impacts performance. Example: if test uses only 100 products (vs 10K in production), product search returns faster (all products in RAM cache). Query execution time 50ms in test; 500ms in production (index scan, disk I/O). If test uses only 1000 orders (vs 100K in production), order history queries fast; in production they're slow. Many teams test with small datasets, pass all gates, deploy to production, and hit performance wall on day 1. DeFOSPAM describes 25 features; no mention of production data scale. Noyce's performance budgets assume production-scale data; test must validate this assumption.
nfr_register.recommendation: Establish baseline production data volumes: 10K products, 50K customers, 100K orders, 200K reviews, 100K images (from Ada's capacity model). Load this into test database BEFORE soak testing. Verify indexes are present (product_id, order_date, customer_id, etc.). Validate query plans: ensure search queries use index (EXPLAIN ANALYZE in PostgreSQL). During soak test, if p95 response time differs > 20% from budgets, investigate index usage. For volume testing: load 100K products (10x) and measure search latency; must remain < 1.2s p95 (Noyce budget). Document all test data sizes in final report.

nfr_register.title: Critical: Test Environment Must Match Production Architecture for Valid Results
nfr_register.reasoning: Common pitfall: test on smaller infrastructure (e.g., 2 app servers instead of 6) and declare success. But smaller infrastructure has different bottlenecks. Example: on 2 servers, database connection pool exhaustion occurs at 500 users (only 20 connections per server); on 6 servers, exhaustion at 1500 users. Test must use identical architecture to production (or scale-down proportionally). If production is 4 web + 6 app + 2 DB, test should use same setup (or half-size: 2 web + 3 app + 1 DB). Load must also scale: if reducing servers to 50%, reduce concurrent users to 50% (1250 instead of 2500).
nfr_register.recommendation: Provision test infrastructure identical to Ada's recommendations: 4 web (8 vCPU, 8GB RAM), 6 app (8 vCPU, 16GB), 2 DB (16 vCPU, 64GB), 2 Redis (8 vCPU, 32GB). If cost prohibitive, use 50% scale: 2 web, 3 app, 1 DB, 1 Redis. Document all deviations from production. When interpreting test results, scale findings: 'Breaking point at 50% scale = 12500 concurrent users; at 100% scale, estimate 25000 concurrent users.' Include in test report: detailed infrastructure specs, comparison to production, caveat on result extrapolation.

nfr_register.title: Major: DeFOSPAM Scenarios Must Be Mapped to Performance Test Scripts for Coverage
nfr_register.reasoning: DeFOSPAM identified 25 features and created 25 scenarios. Performance budgets (Noyce) defined for critical features (Catalog, Search, Auth, Cart, Checkout, Payment). But no mapping exists between scenarios and test scripts. Risk: test loads only popular features (browse, search) and ignores edge cases (wishlist, reviews, gift cards). If wishlist feature has unoptimized O(n^2) algorithm, load test won't catch it because wishlist usage < 5% of traffic. Must create test scripts for ALL 25 scenarios to ensure comprehensive coverage.
nfr_register.recommendation: Create JMeter/Gatling test script for each of 10+ major DeFOSPAM scenarios: (1) Browse catalog + view product, (2) Search + filter results, (3) Login + register, (4) Add/update cart, (5) Checkout flow, (6) Payment, (7) Order history, (8) Wishlist mgmt, (9) Reviews, (10) Account settings. For each script: define realistic user think time (1-5 sec between actions), measure p50/p90/p95/p99 response times, compare to Noyce's budgets. Weighting: 40% browse, 20% search, 15% cart, 10% checkout, 10% other (realistic traffic distribution). Execute load test with mixed scenarios (not isolated). Validate: each scenario meets its budget under 2500 concurrent users.

nfr_register.title: Critical: Performance Gates and Doneness Criteria Must Be Defined to Exit Testing
nfr_register.reasoning: Without clear exit criteria, testing becomes open-ended. Team runs tests, finds issues, fixes them, retests, finds more issues, repeat endlessly. No definition of 'done'. Must establish gates: 'Testing is complete when all of these conditions are met.' Gates should be measurable, realistic, and linked to business goals. Example gate: 'Error rate < 0.5% at 2500 concurrent users.' Teams can measure this objectively and move forward. Missing gates from previous agents: no SLA compliance gate, no memory stability gate, no recovery time gate.
nfr_register.recommendation: Define 7 exit gates (from test strategy): (1) GATE-LOAD-002: All endpoints meet Noyce's SLAs at baseline 2500 users, (2) GATE-LOAD-003: DB connection pool < 80% utilization, (3) GATE-LOAD-004: Memory growth < 10MB/hour after 2h warm-up, (4) GATE-STRESS-001: 2x peak achieves > 95% SLA compliance, (5) GATE-STRESS-003: System recovers within 2 min after stress, (6) GATE-SOAK-001: 24-hour degradation < 5%, (7) GATE-SOAK-002: No memory leaks detected. 'Must pass' gates (6): gates 1-6. 'Should pass' (3): gates for 5x peak, connection leak, breakpoint. 'Nice to have' (1): 72-hour soak. Overall success: all 6 'must pass' gates. Document in testing plan; share with stakeholders; confirm agreement before testing begins.

nfr_register.title: Major: Security Control Overhead (513ms) Must Be Validated Under Peak Load
nfr_register.reasoning: Yao identified 513ms security overhead: TLS termination (45ms), bcrypt password hashing (200ms), JWT validation (8ms * 50 concurrent logins = 400ms), rate limiting middleware (10ms). Payment authorization budget (Noyce: 3500ms p95) has only 455ms buffer after TLS (70ms) + OAuth introspection (150ms) + payment gateway processing (2500ms typical). Under peak load, if JWT validation threads saturate or bcrypt CPU cores max out, response time spikes. Yao flagged: 'At 2000 req/sec, JWT validation consumes 50% CPU.' At 5000 concurrent users (higher sustained load), security overhead becomes bottleneck.
nfr_register.recommendation: Execute STRESS-001 with security-focused monitoring: measure TLS handshake time, JWT validation latency, bcrypt CPU consumption per login. Target: TLS termination < 70ms p95, JWT validation < 8ms p95 (cache tokens if possible), bcrypt intentional delay 200ms (non-negotiable for security). If JWT validation exceeds 15ms p95 at 5000 users, implement JWT cache (cache parsed/validated tokens for 60s). If bcrypt becomes bottleneck (CPU > 80%), reduce max concurrent logins (rate limit: 50 login attempts per IP per 30 min). For payment: measure authorization latency including TLS + OAuth + payment gateway. Budget 3500ms; security overhead should consume < 500ms.

nfr_register.title: Major: Spike Test Required to Validate Auto-Scaling Response to Flash Sale Events
nfr_register.reasoning: Real-world scenario: nopCommerce runs a flash sale. Users arrive at 500 -> 5000 in 30 seconds (10x spike). Auto-scaling must react: provision new servers, update load balancer, warm up caches. If auto-scaling latency > 60 seconds, queue builds; requests timeout; customers abandon checkout; revenue lost. Turing flagged cart abandonment threshold at 5-10 seconds; spike lasting > 30 seconds = high abandonment. Previous agents did not specify spike test, but it's critical for e-commerce.
nfr_register.recommendation: Execute SPIKE-001: Users 500 (steady) -> 5000 (over 30s) -> sustain 3 min -> ramp down. Measure: (1) When does auto-scaling trigger (CloudWatch alarm threshold, e.g., CPU > 70%)? Should be within 30-60s. (2) How many new servers added? (3) Do new servers join load balancer before requests timeout? (4) Is cache warmed (cold cache = slow first requests)? (5) Error rate during spike: should spike to 5-10% then drop to < 1% as servers online. Success: p95 response time stays < 5s during spike (vs 2.2s normal); no order loss; servers scale back down to baseline when spike ends. Validate: enable auto-scaling in test env; confirm policies trigger; measure scale-up latency.

nfr_register.title: Zero Production Monitoring Infrastructure
nfr_register.reasoning: nopCommerce requirements document contains 153+ functional requirements with ZERO specifications for observability, monitoring strategy, or alerting. Platform is designed to run at 500 avg / 2,500 peak concurrent users with 6 critical SLAs (Catalog 2.2s, Search 1.2s, Auth 1.2s, Cart 800ms, Checkout 9.0s, Payment 3.5s) but has NO real-time visibility into whether these SLAs are met. Without monitoring, platform cannot detect performance degradation, capacity constraints, or user-facing failures until customers complain via support tickets.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement comprehensive monitoring infrastructure across 7 layers: (1) Synthetic monitoring - automated user journey tests every 5 minutes from 3 geographic locations; (2) Real User Monitoring (RUM) - capture actual browser performance metrics (FCP, LCP, CLS, TTI) from 10% of users; (3) Application Performance Monitoring (APM) - distributed tracing to isolate latency to specific components (web, app, DB, cache, APIs); (4) Infrastructure monitoring - CPU, memory, disk I/O, network saturation per tier; (5) Log aggregation - centralized logging for errors, security events, slow queries; (6) Mobile app monitoring - crash detection, ANR tracking, app startup time; (7) Diagnostics - automated CPU profiling and memory heap dumps when performance anomalies detected. Deploy within 2 weeks of go-live. Use Datadog APM or New Relic as primary platform; integrate with PagerDuty for incident escalation.

nfr_register.title: Alert Thresholds Undefined for All SLAs
nfr_register.feature: All 6 critical features (Catalog, Search, Auth, Cart, Checkout, Payment)
nfr_register.reasoning: Noyce defined SLAs (p95 latency targets), but there are NO alert thresholds defined for when p95 approaches or exceeds these targets. When does the on-call engineer get paged? Is it when p95 hits exactly 1.2s (equals SLA)? When it reaches 1.1s (90% of SLA)? Without threshold definitions, alerting strategy is ad-hoc and reactive rather than proactive. Engineers will miss performance degradation until customers notice slowness and churn.
nfr_register.recommendation: Define 3-tier alert escalation for each SLA: (1) WARNING: p95 reaches 75% of SLA threshold - triggers Slack notification to #performance-team, 5-minute window before escalation. (2) CRITICAL: p95 reaches 90% of SLA threshold - pages on-call engineer, 2-minute escalation to engineering lead. (3) INCIDENT: p95 exceeds SLA threshold for > 2 minutes - pages engineering lead + VP Engineering, triggers automated mitigation. Example: Search SLA is 1.2s p95, so Warning = 900ms, Critical = 1100ms, Incident = 1200ms breach for > 3 minutes. Implement in monitoring platform (Datadog, New Relic) and sync alert routes with PagerDuty escalation policies. Document in runbook per feature.

nfr_register.title: Synthetic Monitoring Strategy Missing
nfr_register.reasoning: Synthetic monitoring (automated user journey tests) is the ONLY way to detect performance problems before customers are affected. Liskov identified critical bottlenecks (DB connection pool at 5K users, payment gateway cascades, session management undefined) that could cause outages. Without synthetic tests running continuously, these failures go undetected until production incident occurs. Synthetic tests also serve as early warning system for regressions introduced by deployments.
nfr_register.recommendation: Deploy synthetic monitoring for 7 critical user journeys: (1) Homepage load - measure p95 < 2.0s; (2) Product page load - measure p95 < 2.2s; (3) Search query (4 different search terms to catch index issues) - measure p95 < 1.2s; (4) Login flow - measure p95 < 1.2s; (5) Add-to-cart operation - measure p95 < 800ms; (6) Checkout flow (all 5 steps) - measure p95 < 9.0s; (7) Payment authorization - measure p95 < 3.5s. Run every 5 minutes from 3 geographic regions (US-East, US-West, EU-Central). Use Datadog Synthetics, AWS CloudWatch Synthetics, or New Relic Synthetics. Alert on p95 > warning threshold or error rate > 1%. Include test data pre-seeding (valid test user, test product inventory, test payment tokens).

nfr_register.title: Real User Monitoring (RUM) Not Defined

monitoring.metric: TTFB | FCP | LCP | CLS | TTI | api_latency

nfr_register.reasoning: Turing identified that mobile users and accessibility needs drive UX metrics, but nopCommerce has ZERO capability to measure actual user experience in browsers. Synthetic tests measure from optimal network conditions; RUM captures reality: real network latency, device CPU constraints, browser rendering delays. A user on 3G network might experience 5-10x longer page load than synthetic test shows. Without RUM, product team is blind to actual user experience quality.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement Real User Monitoring (RUM) with Datadog Browser RUM or New Relic Browser. Instrument all pages to capture: (1) Core Web Vitals: Largest Contentful Paint (LCP, target < 2.5s), Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS, target < 0.1), First Input Delay (FID, target < 100ms); (2) Custom metrics: API response time from browser perspective, form submission time, search result rendering time; (3) Session tracking: which pages users visit, how long they spend per page, where they drop off. Sample 100% of error transactions, 10% of successful transactions (adjust per feature importance). Capture by country, region, ISP quality, device type, browser. Set RUM alerts: FCP > 2.5s or LCP > 4.0s triggers warning; > 3.5s or > 5.0s triggers critical alert. Use RUM data to correlate backend performance issues with user experience impact.

nfr_register.title: Mobile App Monitoring Missing
nfr_register.reasoning: Turing specified mobile targets and accessibility budgets, implying mobile app will be developed. Without mobile-specific monitoring, crashes and ANRs (Application Not Responding) go undetected until customers report via store reviews or support. Mobile networks are more variable than web (latency 200-500ms vs 50-100ms on desktop), requiring different performance budgets and timeout values. App startup time and screen load time are critical for mobile UX (users expect < 2s startup).
nfr_register.recommendation: Integrate mobile monitoring SDK: Firebase Crashlytics (free, Android + iOS), Datadog RUM Mobile, or New Relic Mobile. Track: (1) Crash rate and stack traces - alert if > 0.5% (critical > 1%); (2) App startup time - target < 2s, warn if > 2.5s; (3) Screen load times - measure time to first content per screen; (4) ANR (Application Not Responding) rate - alert if > 0.1%; (5) API latency from app - account for mobile network delay (budget 2-3x web latency); (6) Battery drain rate - excessive battery drain indicates infinite loops or unnecessary wake-locks; (7) Memory footprint - measure heap size, alert if > 200MB (typical mobile heap). Capture session data (user ID, session duration, features used) for correlation with web behavior. Set up real-time crash notifications for new crash signatures (auto-alert engineering team). Use mobile metrics to validate performance budgets are realistic for mobile users.

nfr_register.title: APM Instrumentation Points Not Specified
nfr_register.feature: All components (web server, app, database, cache, APIs)
nfr_register.reasoning: Noyce defined SLAs for end-to-end transactions, but without APM tracing, there's NO way to know which component is causing the slowness. If Search p95 latency is 1.2s, is it: Elasticsearch (slow query), Redis cache miss, network latency, or web server serialization? Without APM distributed tracing, troubleshooting becomes guesswork. Engineers waste hours digging through logs instead of pinpointing root cause in seconds.
nfr_register.recommendation: Deploy APM agent library with comprehensive instrumentation: (1) Web server layer - measure HTTP request handling time (target < 300ms), count requests per endpoint, track error responses; (2) Database queries - measure query execution time per statement, track slow queries (> 100ms), monitor connection pool utilization (target < 80%), measure transaction duration; (3) Cache operations - track Redis GET/SET latency (target < 50ms), measure cache hit ratio per cache type, alert on eviction rate spikes; (4) External API calls - measure latency to payment gateway, shipping API, recommendations service, track circuit breaker status; (5) Message queue - measure message processing time, dead letter queue depth (if async patterns used); (6) Background jobs - measure execution time, failure rate, retry attempts. Use Datadog APM or New Relic APM. Configure trace sampling: 100% for errors and slow requests (> 2x baseline), 5% for normal requests (adjust based on traffic volume). Implement custom tags: feature_name, user_tier, geographic_region to enable drill-down analysis.

nfr_register.title: Database and Cache Monitoring Insufficient
nfr_register.feature: Database (F18-F25), Cache (Shopping Cart, Product Catalog, Sessions)

monitoring.alert_critical: Connection pool > 90%, cache memory > 95%, query time > 200ms

nfr_register.reasoning: Liskov identified critical database bottleneck: connection pool reaches 880 queued connections at 5K users (from 200 current pool size). Ada recommended 120 app connections from pool. Without real-time monitoring of connection pool utilization, queue depth, and wait times, this bottleneck goes undetected until checkout slowness reports arrive during flash sale. Similarly, Redis cache (for sessions, cart, products) has no monitoring for memory pressure, eviction rate, or hit ratio - missing signals that would indicate need for additional cache nodes or TTL tuning.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement database and cache monitoring: (1) Database connection pool - measure utilization % (per app, web, admin), queue depth (queued requests), wait time for available connection. Set alerts: Warning at 70% utilization (room to scale), Critical at 90%. Correlate queue spikes with slow checkout to trigger investigation. (2) Slow query monitoring - collect all queries > 100ms (configurable), track query count, execution time, affected rows. Use slow query log to identify optimization targets. (3) Database resource usage - CPU%, memory%, disk I/O (read/write ops), replication lag if read replica present. Alert on replication lag > 10 seconds (data consistency risk). (4) Redis cache - measure memory used %, eviction rate (items removed per second), command latency (p95 for GET/SET, target < 50ms). Alert on eviction rate > 50/sec (memory pressure, need more nodes) or latency spike > 100ms (possible network issue or keyspace contention). (5) Cache hit ratio - measure % of requests served from cache vs. origin. Track per cache type (session cache, product detail cache, search result cache). A hit ratio < 50% indicates misconfigured TTLs or cache sizing issue. Deploy monitoring via APM agent, Prometheus exporters, or cloud vendor tools (RDS Insights, ElastiCache metrics).

nfr_register.title: Alert Fatigue Prevention Not Designed
nfr_register.reasoning: Without alert fatigue prevention, on-call engineers receive hundreds of duplicate/related alerts (e.g., if database goes down, both 'high app CPU' and 'high app latency' and 'checkout failures' fire simultaneously, even though root cause is single database event). Engineers become desensitized to alerts, miss real incidents in noise, and burn out. Industry studies show > 100 unique alerts per week per engineer leads to 80% miss rate on critical incidents.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement 4-layer alert fatigue prevention: (1) AGGREGATION - group related alerts by component (database alerts grouped together, payment alerts grouped together). Use alert tags (app_tier, region, feature) to correlate. (2) DEDUPLICATION - suppress duplicate alerts for 30 minutes once initial alert fired (don't repeat every 5 minutes). Use alert ID or fingerprint to identify duplicates. (3) DEPENDENCY AWARENESS - if database health check fails, automatically suppress downstream 'high app latency' and 'high app CPU' alerts (expected consequence of database issue). Configure dependency rules in PagerDuty. (4) ANOMALY DETECTION - alert on deviation from baseline, not absolute threshold. Use Datadog/New Relic anomaly detection: alert if response time increases 2x normal for that time of day, rather than fixed threshold. Implement 'quiet hours' for non-critical alerts (e.g., suppress 'secondary informational' alerts during incident window if critical alert already fired). Track alert volume metric - if > 50 alerts/hour, investigate alert rules and tune thresholds. Goal: < 10 actionable alerts per engineer per week.

nfr_register.title: Automated Mitigation Actions Not Defined
nfr_register.reasoning: Yao identified 513ms total security overhead + rate limiting needs. Liskov identified payment gateway cascade failure risk. Without automated mitigation, incident response is manual: engineer gets paged, spends 5-10 minutes diagnosing root cause, 5-10 minutes executing mitigation (scale up, restart service, drain traffic). During that 10-20 minute window, customers experience outage, checkout abandonment increases, revenue lost. Automated mitigation can respond in seconds (auto-scale spins up new server in 30-60 seconds, circuit breaker opens in milliseconds).
nfr_register.recommendation: Define and implement 8 automated mitigation triggers: (1) AUTO-SCALE APP TIER - if CPU > 85% for 2 minutes, spin up 2 new app servers; rollback when CPU < 50% for 5 minutes. Max scale: 15 servers (2.5x baseline of 6). (2) CIRCUIT BREAKER FOR PAYMENT GATEWAY - if payment response time > 5s or error rate > 5%, fail-fast: return 'Authorization Pending' to user, queue for async retry. Queue can process up to 1000 retries/hour in background. (3) DATABASE CONNECTION POOL RECOVERY - if pool utilization > 80% for 1 minute, drain idle connections from cache layer, reset stale connections. Alert engineer to investigate root cause. (4) CACHE EVICTION RECOVERY - if Redis eviction rate > 100 items/sec (memory pressure), reduce all cache TTLs by 50% for 30 minutes. Monitor cache hit ratio; if drops > 5%, revert TTL reduction. (5) GRACEFUL DEGRADATION / TRAFFIC SHEDDING - if network saturation > 80% on load balancer for 1 minute, shed non-critical features: defer product recommendations, defer wishlist operations, prioritize checkout and auth traffic. Show users message: 'High demand; some features temporarily unavailable.' (6) SEARCH SLOWNESS RECOVERY - if search p95 > 1200ms AND Elasticsearch GC > 1 second, flush oldest 10% of search result cache; reduce facet aggregation depth. (7) MEMORY LEAK DETECTION - if Java heap grows > 100MB/hour despite GC, trigger heap dump and notify engineer; consider rolling restart if critical. (8) RATE LIMITING ACTIVATION FOR ATTACKS - if login error rate > 0.5% (possible brute-force), auto-enable aggressive rate limiting (2 attempts per IP per 5 minutes vs. normal 5 per 30 min) until error rate drops. Implement via Lambda functions, Kubernetes controllers, or APM platform automation. Test mitigation actions in staging quarterly.

nfr_register.title: Key Dashboards Not Designed for Stakeholders
nfr_register.reasoning: Without purpose-built dashboards, different stakeholders lack visibility: Engineers don't know system health at a glance (need 30+ metric alerts to understand status). DevOps can't track capacity headroom or plan scaling (need to dig through multiple tools). Business/Product teams can't correlate performance to revenue impact or customer abandonment (need to ask engineers). This information asymmetry slows decision-making and incident response.
nfr_register.recommendation: Build 4 purpose-built dashboards: (1) ENGINEERING OPERATIONS DASHBOARD (5-second refresh) - 'SLA traffic light' showing green/yellow/red status for 6 critical features; current p95 response time vs. SLA; active alerts sorted by severity; request rate and latency distribution (p50/p90/p95/p99); CPU/memory/disk gauges per tier; recent incidents timeline. On-call engineer can glance at this and know system health instantly. (2) OPS/DEVOPS INFRASTRUCTURE DASHBOARD (30-second refresh) - server inventory table (count, avg CPU%, memory%, disk I/O); auto-scaling history (events last 7 days with reasons); database health details (connection pool %, slow query count, replication lag); network health (saturation, packet loss, DNS time); log volume and top 10 errors; cost tracking hourly. (3) BUSINESS/PRODUCT DASHBOARD (60-second refresh) - real-time conversions and revenue (orders/min vs 7-day avg); checkout completion rate; payment success rate; average order value; NPS proxies (search bounce rate, session abandonment, page load time); SLA violations in last 24h with estimated lost revenue; traffic/capacity headroom with scaling forecast. (4) STATUS PAGE (60-second refresh, public) - simple traffic light showing all systems operational / degraded / outage; uptime % for last 1 hour (99.98% = green); plain English known issues if any; 7-day historical uptime chart. Dashboards auto-generated from monitoring platform (Datadog, New Relic); ensure mobile-friendly display.

nfr_register.title: Performance Regression Detection Missing
nfr_register.reasoning: Without build-over-build performance comparison, performance regressions slip into production undetected. Example: Engineer optimizes feature A but accidentally introduces 20% slower database query in feature B. Synthetic tests don't catch this (total throughput unchanged). Performance regresses 2-3% per week until, after 3 months, system is 30% slower and customer complaints flood in. By then, root cause is buried in 100+ commits. Regression detection in CI/CD catches these issues before production deployment, saving investigation time and preventing SLA violations.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement 6-phase regression detection strategy: (1) BASELINE COLLECTION (CI stage) - after unit tests pass, deploy to isolated performance test environment, run synthetic load test at 1x peak (2500 users, 8000 tx/hr) for 20 minutes, collect p50/p90/p95/p99 latencies and resource metrics per feature. Store baseline as artifact (baseline-{commit-hash}.json). (2) REGRESSION ANALYSIS (CI stage, post-baseline) - compare new baseline to previous known-good baseline; calculate % regression per feature. Flag if Catalog p95 > 2310ms (5% above baseline), Search > 1260ms, Auth > 1260ms, Cart > 840ms, Checkout > 9450ms, Payment > 3675ms. Block merge if regression > 5%; require engineer to investigate. (3) ROOT CAUSE DRILL-DOWN (CI, automated) - if regression detected, auto-generate HTML report with component-level latency breakdown (which component regressed?), code diff highlighting changed methods/endpoints, allocation analysis (new memory churn?), slow query detection (new queries? changed indexes?), external API calls (new calls added?). (4) STAGING VALIDATION (post-merge) - deploy merged code to staging, run 1.5x peak load test (3750 users, 12000 tx/hr) for 1 hour; ensure no regression > 10% from baseline and error rate < 0.1%. Block production deployment if staging test fails. (5) CONTINUOUS PROD MONITORING (post-deployment) - compare production p95 latencies to previous 3 deployments; alert if current p95 > max(previous 3) + 3%. Monitor for 30 minutes post-deployment (stabilization window). (6) HISTORICAL TREND ANALYSIS (weekly) - track average p95 latency trend over 12 weeks; identify slow regressions (2-3% per week creep); correlate with deployments and feature launches; recommend optimization targets if trend negative. Document findings in weekly performance report to engineering leadership.

nfr_register.title: Security Performance Overhead Not Monitored
nfr_register.feature: User Authentication, Payment Processing, Rate Limiting
nfr_register.reasoning: Yao identified 513ms total security overhead (bcrypt 200ms non-negotiable, JWT 8ms, rate limiting 5ms, TLS 45ms, etc.). These security controls are essential but create latency tax on performance budgets. Without monitoring each security component individually, it's impossible to diagnose where latency is spent: Is login slow because bcrypt is slow? Because session lookup is slow? Because network is slow? Monitoring security overhead as separate metrics enables targeted optimization without sacrificing security.
nfr_register.recommendation: Add security-specific metrics to APM and dashboard: (1) PASSWORD HASHING LATENCY - measure bcrypt computation time per login (target 200ms, non-negotiable for security; bcrypt cost factor 12 is industry standard). Monitor for deviations (if > 250ms, possible CPU contention or hardware issue). (2) JWT TOKEN VALIDATION LATENCY - measure time to validate JWT signature (RS256) per request (target 8ms per validation). If > 15ms, indicates CPU contention or excessive validations per request. (3) RATE LIMITING CHECK LATENCY - measure Redis lookup time to check rate limit counters (target 5ms). Spike in this metric indicates Redis latency issue or excessive rate limit checks. (4) TLS HANDSHAKE TIME - measure time to establish TLS connection (target < 50ms for new connections, < 5ms for session resumption). If > 100ms, indicates TLS library issue or CPU contention. (5) OCSP STAPLING STATUS - measure time to validate certificate (0ms if OCSP stapled successfully, 20-30ms if OCSP check required). Track % of requests with valid stapling (target > 95%). (6) DATABASE ENCRYPTION OVERHEAD - measure time to encrypt/decrypt data at rest (target 2-3ms, hardware-accelerated). If > 5ms, indicates encryption library issue or lack of hardware acceleration. (7) RATE LIMIT VIOLATION RATE - track % of requests rejected by rate limiter (normal < 0.01%, attack scenario > 1%). Correlate with authentication errors to detect brute-force attacks. Monitor these metrics on security-focused dashboard alongside auth latency. Alert if any security metric exceeds baseline by > 20% (indicates possible attack or misconfiguration). Document that these latencies are non-negotiable and baked into SLAs (e.g., Login SLA of 1.2s already includes 200ms bcrypt).

nfr_register.title: Health Check Endpoints Not Designed
nfr_register.reasoning: Health checks are lightweight endpoints used by load balancers and orchestrators (Kubernetes, Docker Compose) to determine if a server is healthy and should receive traffic. Without well-designed health checks, orchestrators may route traffic to failed servers or fail to detect degradation. Health checks must be < 50ms and must check only essential dependencies (database connectivity, cache accessibility) without expensive operations (e.g., no database queries, no external API calls).
nfr_register.recommendation: Design 4-tier health check endpoints: (1) /health (< 10ms) - liveness check; confirms web server responding, configuration loaded. Returns 200 OK {"status": "healthy"}. Called every 5 seconds by load balancer. (2) /health/ready (< 50ms) - readiness check; confirms app can accept traffic. Checks database ping (no query), Redis ping, external API circuit breaker status, minimum resource thresholds (memory > 100MB free, disk > 1GB free). Returns 200 OK {"status": "ready", "components": {"database": "ok", "cache": "ok", ...}}. Called every 10 seconds by orchestrator; if fails, drain traffic and restart. (3) /health/live (< 20ms) - process aliveness; detects deadlocks or infinite loops. Performs quick heartbeat check (thread pool responsive, request queue not stuck). Called every 3 seconds by scheduler; if fails, force restart process. (4) /health/deps (< 200ms) - deep dependency check; used by monitoring system (not load balancer). Checks database connectivity (SELECT 1), Redis GET/SET, payment gateway circuit breaker status, Elasticsearch cluster health. Returns detailed status of all upstream dependencies. Called every 30 seconds by monitoring system; failures trigger alert to engineer. Implement in application startup; ensure health checks don't require full application initialization (can check at boot time). Document expected behavior of each endpoint in runbook.

nfr_register.title: Pre-Production Monitoring Coverage Insufficient
nfr_register.reasoning: Performance problems caught in staging (before production) are 100x cheaper to fix than problems caught in production. Yet staging monitoring is often minimal or non-existent. Engineers optimize features in dev environment where network latency is 0ms and database is local; performance degrades when moved to realistic staging environment. Staging should be production-like: same data volume, same network topology, same infrastructure (or scaled version).
nfr_register.recommendation: Define staging monitoring coverage: (1) SYNTHETIC MONITORING - run every 10 minutes (vs. 5 in prod) from 1-2 regions; same test suite (homepage, search, login, add-to-cart, checkout, payment). Alert thresholds: if staging p95 > 5% above production baseline, investigate regression before allowing merge to main branch. (2) RUM MONITORING - enable 50% sampling (vs. 10% prod) to catch regressions faster. (3) APM MONITORING - 10% trace sampling (vs. 5% prod) - lower sampling acceptable since staging traffic lower. (4) INFRASTRUCTURE MONITORING - full 10-second collection (same as prod) with 30-day retention. (5) PERFORMANCE GATES (automated) - after code merge, run staging load test at 1.5x peak for 1 hour; block production deployment if: p95 regression > 10% from baseline, error rate > 0.1%, memory grows > 50MB over hour (leak detection), recovery time after load drop > 2 minutes. (6) DATABASE/CACHE MONITORING - same alerts as production (connection pool > 80%, cache eviction rate > 50/sec, slow queries > 100ms). (7) LOG RETENTION - 7 days (vs. 30 days hot + 1 year archive in prod). Deploy similar monitoring stack as production (Datadog, New Relic, Prometheus) with cost optimization (lower sampling, shorter retention).

nfr_register.title: Monitoring Rollout Timeline Not Defined
nfr_register.reasoning: Comprehensive monitoring is complex (7 layers, 40+ metrics per feature, 4 dashboards, automated mitigation). Without a phased rollout plan, monitoring rollout will slip or be incomplete, leaving gaps. Critical phase is between code freeze and production launch - best time to add monitoring infrastructure.
nfr_register.recommendation: Deploy monitoring in 3 phases over 9 weeks: PHASE 1 (Weeks 1-2): Health checks + synthetic monitoring. Implement /health endpoints (4 tiers). Deploy synthetic tests for 6 critical journeys from 3 regions, every 5 minutes. Alert on p95 > warning threshold or error rate > 1%. Set up PagerDuty integration. Estimated effort: 2 engineers, 2 weeks. Cost: ~$2K/month (basic synthetic service). PHASE 2 (Weeks 3-5): APM + RUM + Infrastructure. Integrate Datadog APM or New Relic (deploy agent library). Instrument all critical components (web, app, DB, cache, APIs). Deploy Browser RUM SDK on all pages; configure 10% sampling. Set up Prometheus + Grafana for infrastructure (CPU, memory, disk, network). Alert thresholds per feature (warning, critical, incident levels). Estimated effort: 3 engineers, 3 weeks. Cost: ~$5K/month (APM platform + infrastructure monitoring). PHASE 3 (Weeks 6-9): Mobile monitoring + Transaction tracing + Regression detection + Dashboards. Integrate mobile SDK (Firebase Crashlytics or New Relic Mobile). Configure distributed tracing (APM traces all transactions end-to-end). Implement CI/CD regression detection (baseline collection, comparison, root cause analysis). Build 4 dashboards (engineering, ops, business, status page). Test automated mitigation actions (auto-scale, circuit breaker, traffic shed) in staging. Estimated effort: 2-3 engineers, 4 weeks. Cost: ~$8K/month (full monitoring platform + mobile + dashboards). Total effort: 7-8 engineers, 9 weeks (or 2-3 engineers for 9 weeks sequentially). Recommend starting Phase 1 immediately (pre-launch sprint); Phase 2-3 can run in parallel post-launch (critical for continuous monitoring of production).

nfr_register.title: CRITICAL: No Scalability Specifications in 153 Functional Requirements
nfr_register.description: The nopCommerce requirements document (153 functional requirements across 25 features) contains ZERO specifications for scalability, auto-scaling, load distribution, or performance under concurrent load.
nfr_register.impact: Platform has undefined breaking points. Peak load (2,500 concurrent users, 8,000 tx/hr) may exceed current capacity with no planned mitigation. 5,000 user target is unreachable without fundamental architectural redesign.
nfr_register.evidence: Ada's capacity model shows baseline sizing (112 vCPU, 272 GB RAM) but no requirements enforce these limits. DeFOSPAM found zero scalability gaps because scalability was not in scope of 153 FRs.
nfr_register.recommendation: Create NFR document: 'Scalability Requirements' covering (1) target concurrent user capacity (500 avg, 2,500 peak, 5,000 stretch), (2) auto-scaling triggers per tier (CPU, queue depth, latency), (3) horizontal scaling strategies (web/app/cache), (4) database scaling approach (read replicas + future sharding), (5) breaking point analysis per component, (6) geographic distribution plan.

nfr_register.title: CRITICAL: Database Connection Pool is Hard Bottleneck at 5,000 Users
nfr_register.description: Current pool: 200 total connections (120 app, 50 web, 30 admin). At 2,500 peak users, avg request takes 200 ms. At 5,000 users with same query performance, max concurrent requests = 5,000 * (200 ms / 1000 ms) = 1,000 requests. With 120 connections, only 120 requests execute concurrently; 880 queue waiting. Queue wait time spikes from 50 ms to 500+ ms.
nfr_register.impact: Checkout latency degrades exponentially at peak load. Connection pool becomes FIFO queue; requests starve waiting for connection. User experience unacceptable. Abandoned cart rate increases.
nfr_register.evidence: Ada's capacity model acknowledges 200-connection pool but doesn't specify pool exhaustion handling. No requirement addresses dynamic connection pooling.
nfr_register.root_cause: Monolithic connection pool shared across all request types (read-heavy catalog queries, write-heavy order queries, admin queries). High-latency queries (reporting, search) block short queries (login, cart view).
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement PgBouncer middleware: (1) Deploy PgBouncer on each app server. (2) App -> PgBouncer: unlimited connections (session multiplexing). (3) PgBouncer -> DB: 40-50 persistent connections. (4) Use transaction mode (not session mode) to maximize connection reuse. (5) Configure wait_timeout 30s, connection_max_age 600s. (6) Increase baseline pool from 200 to 300; target 400 at 5,000 users.

nfr_register.title: CRITICAL: Race Condition in Stock Validation - Concurrent Overselling Risk
nfr_register.description: DeFOSPAM flagged: 'Multiple customers simultaneously add the same limited-stock product to cart and reach order confirmation. Between cart add validation (SC005) and order confirmation validation (REQ098), another customer completes their order and purchases the last units.' Current requirements silent on stock reservation mechanism.
nfr_register.impact: At peak load (2,500 concurrent users), high-demand products (flash sales, limited editions) oversell by 10-50%. Example: 100 units in stock, 150 customers checkout simultaneously, system allows all 150 to complete, resulting in 50-unit shortage. Financial loss, customer dissatisfaction, fulfillment delays.
nfr_register.technical_detail: Inventory table updated without row-level locking. Concurrent UPDATE inventory SET qty = qty - 1 WHERE product_id = X results in non-serializable execution. Two transactions both see qty=100, decrement to qty=99, but qty should be qty=98.
nfr_register.evidence: Paul (Prediction Analyst) flagged this as unpredictable outcome in DeFOSPAM. REQ030, REQ040, REQ098 mention stock validation but no pessimistic locking specified.
nfr_register.recommendation: Stock reservation strategy: (A) PESSIMISTIC: SELECT * FROM products WHERE id=X FOR UPDATE during order confirmation (acquire row lock, prevent concurrent updates). OR (B) OPTIMISTIC: Implement stock reservation on cart add: INSERT INTO cart_reservations (customer_id, product_id, qty, expires_at) VALUES (...) with 30-min expiration. At confirmation, validate reservation still exists and qty available. If expired/unavailable, offer backorder or cancel. Prefer pessimistic locking for simplicity; implement timeout (1 sec) to fail fast if lock wait excessive.
nfr_register.testing_requirement: Load test: 1,000 concurrent customers adding same product (10 units available). Verify: (1) Max 10 orders complete, (2) Remaining 990 orders rejected or backorder-ed, (3) No negative inventory.

nfr_register.title: CRITICAL: Payment Gateway Failure Has No Circuit Breaker - Cascading Outage Risk
nfr_register.description: DeFOSPAM flagged 'payment gateway failures' without mitigation. Current requirements (REQ090-091) define payment processing but zero error handling for gateway timeout/decline. At peak (2.2 orders/sec), if payment gateway response time increases from 100 ms to 5 sec (or times out), checkout thread pool saturates, cascading to all users (web requests unresponsive, even non-checkout users affected).
nfr_register.impact: Payment gateway outage = platform-wide outage. 100 concurrent customers waiting 30 sec for payment response = 100 app server threads blocked, thread pool queue backs up. All other requests (login, browse, cart view) compete for remaining threads. Timeout rate increases exponentially. User experience collapses even though web/app infrastructure is healthy.
nfr_register.example_scenario: Payment processor (Stripe) experiences 5-second elevated latency (1% of transactions) due to DDoS or maintenance. System has no backoff. Every payment request waits 5 sec, consuming 5 threads per order. At 2.2 orders/sec, 11 threads occupied; still 589 free (no issue immediately). BUT if gateway fully down, all 2.2 orders/sec fail, queue backs up exponentially, leading to customer frustration, retries, cascading failure.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement circuit breaker pattern: (1) Monitor payment API: track success rate, latency, error types. (2) If error rate > 5% in 1-min window, TRIP circuit breaker (stop sending requests for 5 min). (3) Return error to customer: 'Payment gateway temporarily unavailable. Your order is saved. Retry in 5 minutes.' (4) Implement async payment processing: enqueue payment request to Redis queue; worker thread processes async (max 5-min delay acceptable). Return 'Payment pending' to customer. (5) Use idempotency key (same payment amount + customer + timestamp hash) to deduplicate retries if customer resubmits. (6) Implement timeout: abort payment request if > 30 sec (fail fast instead of hanging).
nfr_register.testing_requirement: Chaos engineering: simulate payment gateway failure (timeout all requests, return 5xx errors). Verify: (1) Circuit breaker trips within 1 min, (2) Subsequent requests fail fast (< 1 sec) instead of hanging, (3) Order state preserved for retry, (4) No duplicate charges from retried payments.

nfr_register.title: CRITICAL: Session Management Architecture Undefined - Local vs. Distributed Store
nfr_register.description: Current baseline assumes 2 Redis servers for session storage (per Ada's capacity model), but no requirements specify: (1) session storage location (local memory vs. Redis), (2) session replication behavior, (3) session loss tolerance during failover, (4) session affinity requirements (sticky sessions).
nfr_register.impact: If sessions stored in local web server memory: (1) Web server crash = session loss (user re-login required), (2) Sticky sessions required (request must return to same server), (3) Load balancer complexity increases, (4) Scaling becomes harder (adding servers doesn't distribute load evenly). If Redis fails: (1) Potential session loss (acceptable for 5-min outage), (2) Customer inconvenience (re-login required).
nfr_register.evidence: Ada's model specifies Redis deployment but requirements don't mandate it. nopCommerce default uses local session state (not guaranteed distributed).
nfr_register.recommendation: Session architecture: (1) All web/app servers MUST delegate sessions to Redis (no local session storage). (2) Session format: JSON (human-readable for debugging). (3) Session size: ~1-2 KB per user (user ID, auth token, cart reference). Peak 5,000 concurrent sessions = 5-10 GB memory. (4) Session TTL: 30 min idle (refresh on each request), 8 hr absolute max. (5) Replication: Redis master-slave with auto-failover (Sentinel). (6) Monitoring: track session count, lookup latency (target p95 < 50 ms), eviction rate (alert if > 2%).

nfr_register.title: MAJOR: Auto-Scaling Policies Not Defined - Manual Intervention Required at Peak Load
nfr_register.description: No auto-scaling triggers defined for any tier. Scaling from 2,500 to 5,000 concurrent users requires manual infrastructure changes: provision new web/app servers, update load balancer configuration, adjust database connections. During peak traffic, this is impractical (5-15 min deployment lag while users experience high latency).
nfr_register.impact: Flash sales, marketing campaigns, seasonal spikes cannot be handled automatically. Operations team must manually provision infrastructure during peak load, adding response time. Lost revenue from checkout abandonment during slow response periods.
nfr_register.recommendation: Define auto-scaling policies: (1) WEB TIER: Scale up when CPU > 70% OR latency p95 > 500 ms for 2 min. Scale down when CPU < 30% for 5 min. Min 4 servers, max 10. (2) APP TIER: Scale up when CPU > 75% OR DB conn pool util > 85% OR request queue depth > 50 for 2 min. Scale down when CPU < 30% for 5 min. Min 6 servers, max 16. (3) CACHE: Scale up when memory util > 85% OR eviction rate > 3%. Min 2 shards, max 8. (4) DATABASE: MANUAL (add read replica when replication lag > 5 sec). (5) Cooldown: 60 sec between scale-up events, 300 sec between scale-down events (prevent thrashing).
nfr_register.implementation_tool: AWS Auto Scaling Group (ASG) with target tracking policies OR Kubernetes HPA with custom metrics

nfr_register.title: MAJOR: Read-Heavy Features Lack Caching Strategy - Search and Catalog Queries Unoptimized
nfr_register.description: Product search (F05) and catalog navigation (F06) are read-heavy (peak 6,000-8,000 tx/hr) but no caching strategy specified. Every search query hits database (no Elasticsearch, no query result cache). At peak, catalog database is queried 2.2 times per second for every search/filter/pagination request.
nfr_register.impact: Database CPU spikes during peak load due to repetitive search queries. Catalog response time degrades from 100 ms to 500+ ms. Search becomes unusable during flash sales.
nfr_register.evidence: Ada's capacity model identifies search as 15% of CPU utilization; full-text index mentioned but no Elasticsearch deployment. Product images 50 GB stored in database (inefficient).
nfr_register.recommendation: (1) Implement Elasticsearch for product search. Index: product_name, description, category, SKU, attributes. Cache indexing delay: 30 sec (eventual consistency acceptable). (2) Redis cache layer for query results: category listings, featured products, best sellers. TTL 12 hours (cache invalidation on product update). (3) Lazy-load product details: fetch only required fields initially, load reviews/related products on demand. (4) CDN for product images: move 50 GB images to S3 + CloudFront, serve from edge (~200 ms latency reduction).

nfr_register.title: MAJOR: Inventory Locking Strategy Not Specified - Partial Out-of-Stock Scenarios Undefined
nfr_register.description: DeFOSPAM flagged: 'Out-of-stock detection during checkout' but only addresses complete out-of-stock. No scenario covers partial out-of-stock: customer adds 5 units but only 2 remain at confirmation. No requirement specifies behavior: (1) block order, (2) auto-reduce quantity, (3) split into partial order + backorder, (4) suggest alternatives.
nfr_register.impact: User experience unpredictable during high-concurrency inventory depletion. Customers confused by inventory behavior. Fulfillment process unclear.
nfr_register.recommendation: Define inventory behavior: (1) STRICT (safest): If any item in order is out-of-stock or qty reduced, reject entire order. Customer must remove/adjust item and resubmit. (2) FLEXIBLE: Auto-reduce to available qty; separate remaining into backorder. Send confirmation email: 'Order 12345: 5 units requested, 2 in stock (shipped immediately), 3 backordered (ship when available).' (3) SUBSTITUTE: If exact item unavailable, suggest alternatives; customer approves/rejects. Prefer STRICT for checkout (prevent confusion), offer FLEXIBLE as option for returning customers.

nfr_register.title: MAJOR: Geographic Distribution Not Addressed - Single Data Center is SPOF
nfr_register.description: Baseline architecture assumes single data center (US-East). No requirements address disaster recovery, multi-region deployment, or geographic load distribution. If US-East DC fails (power outage, DDoS, natural disaster), entire platform is down.
nfr_register.impact: RTO (Recovery Time Objective) = undefined (could be hours). RPO (Recovery Point Objective) = undefined (data loss possible). Compliance risk: GDPR requires data residency (EU data must stay in EU); single US DC violates GDPR if customer is EU-based.
nfr_register.recommendation: Multi-region strategy (phased): (1) PHASE 1 (now, 0 cost): Deploy CDN (CloudFront) for static assets (60% of requests). Primary DC: US-East. (2) PHASE 2 (month 12): Add secondary region (US-West) for HA. Database: async replication Primary -> Secondary (30-sec replication lag). DNS failover: switch to Secondary if Primary fails (manual or automated). RTO 5 min, RPO 30 sec. (3) PHASE 3 (month 24): Active-active multi-region (US-East, US-West, EU-Central). Local databases; eventual consistency for non-critical data.
nfr_register.cdn_deployment: CloudFront + S3: serve product images, CSS, JS, homepage. Cache TTL 24 hrs (images), 1 hr (HTML), 5 min (API responses). Estimated bandwidth savings: 60-70%.

nfr_register.title: MAJOR: Load Balancer is Single Point of Failure - HA Pair Required
nfr_register.description: Current baseline: 1 load balancer. Single LB failure = all traffic dropped (no failover). LB must handle 2,500+ concurrent connections + SSL/TLS termination CPU.
nfr_register.impact: LB failure = platform outage (100% user impact, not just gradual degradation).
nfr_register.evidence: Ada's architecture specifies 1 load balancer; no HA pair specified.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement LB HA pair: (1) Primary LB + Secondary LB (active-passive). (2) Heartbeat protocol (VRRP or custom): Secondary monitors Primary health. If Primary fails, Secondary assumes IP address (VIP). DNS points to VIP; failover transparent to users. (3) Failover time: < 10 sec. (4) Health checks: HTTP GET / every 10 sec from both LBs; mark dead after 3 failures.

nfr_register.title: Critical Missing: Page Load Time for Public Product Catalog Pages
nfr_register.sla: 95% of product catalog page requests shall complete within 2200ms (p95) from client click to fully rendered page, measured under standard network conditions (25Mbps broadband). This includes DNS, network latency, CDN, web server response, database queries, and client-side rendering.
nfr_register.reasoning: E-commerce best practice targets 2 seconds for product pages. Slower pages increase bounce rate by 7% per second delay (Amazon data). Product catalog is customer-facing critical path. DeFOSPAM identified zero performance requirements; this is foundational missing NFR. p50 targets typical fast networks; p99 targets international users on slower connections.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement: (1) Static asset CDN (images, CSS, JS) with aggressive caching (1 year TTL for versioned assets). (2) Database query optimization: index product queries by category, cache featured products in Redis (1hr TTL). (3) HTML compression (gzip, brotli). (4) Lazy-load product images below the fold. (5) HTTP/2 push for critical resources. (6) Client-side caching headers (Cache-Control: public, max-age=3600). Monitor with Real User Monitoring (RUM) to track p95 continuously.

nfr_register.title: Critical Missing: Search Response Time and Result Pagination
nfr_register.sla: 99% of search requests returning < 1000 results shall respond within 1200ms (p95). Search operations returning > 1000 results shall respond within 2000ms (p99). Results shall be paginated in batches of 12 items (configurable); subsequent page loads shall complete within 500ms (p95) by caching facets and refining existing result set.
nfr_register.reasoning: User expectation for search is <1 second. Search is revenue-critical: high latency causes abandoned searches. DeFOSPAM notes zero search latency specifications. Autocomplete is not covered here but must be < 300ms for interactive user experience. Pagination optimization reduces latency for subsequent pages.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement: (1) Dedicated search engine (Elasticsearch with 3-node cluster minimum). (2) Search index refresh every 5 minutes. (3) Query timeout of 2000ms hard limit (fail safely). (4) Facet caching: pre-compute common facets (Brand, Category, Price Range) and update hourly. (5) Implement query result pagination with deep pagination limits (max page 100 to prevent resource exhaustion). (6) Add Redis cache for top 100 search queries (24hr TTL). (7) Autocomplete on keystroke: return top 10 suggestions within 300ms from separate autocomplete index.

nfr_register.title: Critical Missing: Checkout Flow Response Time per Step
nfr_register.feature: Checkout (Address, Shipping, Payment Method, Payment Info, Confirmation), Guest Checkout
nfr_register.sla: Each checkout step (Address, Shipping, Payment Method, Payment Info, Confirmation) shall respond within 2000ms (p95) from user interaction to page load. Address step may extend to 2500ms (p95) due to external address validation API. Shipping method selection shall respond within 1500ms (p95) when calculating 3PL rates. Payment authorization shall complete within 3500ms (p99) to account for payment gateway latency.
nfr_register.reasoning: Checkout latency directly impacts conversion: 1 second delay = 7% revenue loss (Deloitte). Each step should feel instantaneous. DeFOSPAM critical finding: zero checkout latency targets. Address validation and shipping rate calculation are external dependencies requiring special budgeting. Payment gateways have inherent latency (typically 1-3 seconds); user expects 5-second timeout max.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement: (1) Session layer for storing cart data in-memory (Redis). (2) Address validation caching: store validated addresses for 90 days. (3) Async shipping rate calculation: show 'Calculating shipping...' while querying 3PL, return previously calculated estimates if API slow. (4) Payment gateway connection pooling: pre-establish connections to reduce handshake latency. (5) Implement 3-second timeout on address validation API with fallback (accept address if API slow). (6) Ship method calculation: cache results per postal code for 24hrs. (7) Lazy-load payment method forms (only render selected method).

nfr_register.title: Critical Missing: User Authentication Response Time (Login/Registration)
nfr_register.sla: User login shall respond within 1200ms (p95) from credential submission to authenticated session. User registration shall respond within 1200ms (p95) from form submission to account creation and auto-login. Password verification shall use intentional delay (bcrypt/Argon2) to prevent timing attacks; total crypto latency budget = 150-200ms. Registration email (welcome/verification) shall be sent asynchronously (outside response time budget) and delivered within 5 minutes (SLA tracked separately).
nfr_register.reasoning: Authentication is security-critical and latency-sensitive. Users expect <1 second response. DeFOSPAM found zero latency specs. Password hashing (bcrypt, Argon2) intentionally slow for security (150-200ms), must be accounted for in budget. Email delivery should not block user (async required).
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement: (1) Use bcrypt with cost factor 12 (target 150-200ms per login). (2) Connection pooling to database (min 10, max 50 connections). (3) Async email queue (email sent via background job, not in HTTP response). (4) Session storage in Redis (cache sessions for 24hr, invalidate on logout). (5) Rate limiting on login endpoint: max 5 failed attempts per email per 30 minutes, then 30-minute lockout. (6) Implement credential check caching: cache valid credentials for 5 minutes with hash comparison to prevent repeated crypto cost. (7) Monitor failed login rate; alert if >10% failure rate indicates brute force attack.

nfr_register.title: Critical Missing: Payment Processing Authorization Latency
nfr_register.feature: Checkout (Payment Method, Payment Info), Payment Processing
nfr_register.sla: Payment authorization shall complete within 3500ms (p95) from customer confirmation. Authorization response MUST be received and processed within 5000ms (p99); if timeout occurs, order MUST be marked 'Authorization Pending' and customer presented with 'Your payment is being verified' message. Payment retry logic shall allow customer 3 retry attempts within 10 minutes; after 10 minutes, order expires and cart is recovered. Authorization response codes (Approved, Declined, Review) shall be logged with full gateway response for debugging.
nfr_register.reasoning: Payment latency is critical and highly variable due to external payment gateway. Average gateways respond in 1-2 seconds, but can reach 5 seconds under load. Timeout strategy essential: must not leave customer in uncertainty. DeFOSPAM identified zero payment latency specs. 3D Secure adds latency (user redirect); budget assumes 3D Secure for 5-10% of transactions.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement: (1) Connection pooling to payment gateway (min 20 connections to handle concurrent checkouts). (2) Payment gateway timeout of 5000ms with exponential backoff retry (1s, 2s, 4s). (3) Implement idempotency keys: send unique request ID to gateway to prevent double-charging if request retried. (4) Async payment monitoring: if auth not received within 5 seconds, mark order 'pending' and poll gateway every 10 seconds for 5 minutes. (5) Fraud detection: implement basic AVS (Address Verification System) checks before gateway call. (6) 3D Secure redirect handling: if required, present user with iframe/redirect; timeout if user takes >5 minutes to complete 3DS flow. (7) Circuit breaker pattern: if payment gateway fails >10 times in 5 minutes, fallback to 'Manual Review' mode (require admin approval).

nfr_register.title: Critical Missing: Shopping Cart Operations Response Time
nfr_register.feature: Shopping Cart Management, Product Search and Discovery
nfr_register.sla: Add-to-cart operations shall respond within 800ms (p95) from button click to cart update. Cart view (full cart page) shall render within 800ms (p95). Update quantity shall respond within 500ms (p95). Remove item shall respond within 300ms (p95). All cart operations may trigger dynamic price recalculation (coupon, tax, shipping); these async operations shall complete within 2 seconds and update page with JavaScript without full page reload.
