nfr_register.reasoning: Cart operations are frequent user interactions; users expect snappy response (<500ms). DeFOSPAM shows zero cart latency specs. Inventory check is critical to prevent overselling. Price calculation may require external tax/shipping APIs (should be cached/estimated). Real-time cart mirroring across tabs requires WebSocket optimization.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement: (1) Cart stored in Redis for <100ms lookup. (2) Inventory checks via cache (Redis) with 10-second TTL (trade off accuracy for speed). (3) Price calculation cached by user/cart state; invalidate on coupon change. (4) Async cart recovery: send email with cart link if user abandons (after 30 min idle). (5) Client-side optimistic updates: instantly update cart UI, then confirm with server. (6) Implement mini-cart dropdown for instant view (cached data). (7) Cart sync across browser tabs via WebSocket (optional, advanced). (8) Limit cart size to 100 items; reject additions beyond to prevent performance degradation.

nfr_register.title: Major Missing: Wishlist Operations Response Time
nfr_register.sla: Add-to-wishlist shall respond within 800ms (p95) from button click. View-wishlist page shall render within 1200ms (p95). Remove-from-wishlist shall respond within 300ms (p95). Wishlist operations are authenticated (require login); response time includes session validation.
nfr_register.reasoning: Wishlist is secondary feature (less critical than cart) but affects user experience. DeFOSPAM shows wishlist requirements but zero latency specs. Response should feel fast to encourage use. Authentication required adds latency vs. anonymous cart.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement: (1) Wishlist stored in Redis per logged-in user (cache 24 hours). (2) Database-backed persistent storage with eventual consistency. (3) Client-side caching: cache wishlist items for session. (4) Add-to-wishlist can use AJAX (no page reload) for fast feedback. (5) Share wishlist: generate time-limited URL token (7-day expiration) to share without authentication.

nfr_register.title: Major Missing: Product Reviews & Ratings Latency
nfr_register.sla: Product details page including reviews/ratings section shall load within 2200ms (p95) total. Reviews pagination (page N of reviews) shall respond within 1500ms (p95). Review submission (post new review) shall respond within 800ms (p95) if pre-moderation not required, or within 1000ms (p95) if async moderation enabled.
nfr_register.reasoning: Reviews critical for e-commerce conversion (71% of consumers trust reviews). DeFOSPAM shows review requirements but no latency specs. Ratings aggregation can be expensive query; caching essential. Pagination allows browsing many reviews without loading all.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement: (1) Denormalize rating aggregate fields (avg_rating, count_1star, count_2star, etc.) in product table to avoid expensive queries. (2) Cache aggregates for 1 hour. (3) Paginate reviews in 10-item batches; load more via AJAX. (4) Review moderation: mark review 'Pending' until approved, hide from public until then. (5) Helpful votes on reviews (upvote/downvote): store in separate table with caching. (6) Review moderation can be async (not in response path); notify customer via email when approved.

nfr_register.title: Major Missing: API Latency for External Integrations (Tax, Shipping, Payment)
nfr_register.sla: External API calls (tax, shipping, payment gateways) SHALL have individual timeouts: Address validation 2000ms, Tax calculation 1500ms, Shipping calculation 2000ms, Payment gateway 5000ms. If API does not respond within timeout, system SHALL use fallback strategy: (1) Return cached result from previous 24 hours (tax, shipping), (2) Show estimate to customer (shipping cost estimate), (3) Require manual review (payment). Circuit breaker: if external API fails >5 times in 10 minutes, automatically fallback to cached/estimated values without attempting API call for next 5 minutes.
nfr_register.reasoning: External APIs are primary latency risk. Typical tax/shipping APIs respond in 500-2000ms, but can degrade. DeFOSPAM identified payment gateway as missing requirement; other external APIs also missing. Checkout must not be blocked by slow APIs; caching and fallback essential for UX.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement: (1) Cache all external API responses: Tax for user/state combo (24hr), Shipping for postal code/weight combo (24hr). (2) Implement async address validation: show 'Validating address...' while querying, accept address if API slow. (3) Shipping estimate: calculate using simple distance-based formula as fallback; update with actual rate after user selection. (4) Payment gateway: use connection pooling, keep-alive, and timeout retry with exponential backoff. (5) Monitoring: track p50/p90/p95 latency per API; alert if p95 > budget. (6) Implement fallback UI: if all APIs slow, show 'Manual Review Required - We'll contact you' message instead of hanging.

nfr_register.title: Major Missing: Network Latency Optimization for Mobile & 3G

component_budgets.component: Bandwidth Utilization - Asset Downloads (1MB assets on 4G)

nfr_register.sla: Mobile-optimized pages (responsive design) shall load within 3500ms (p95) on 4G networks. Product catalog pages shall load within 6500ms (p95) on 3G. All pages shall be optimized for mobile: (1) Responsive images (srcset with mobile versions), (2) Minimal CSS/JS for mobile (defer non-critical), (3) Single-column layout on mobile, (4) Tap targets >= 48px. Critical CSS inline; defer CSS for below-fold. DeFOSPAM shows e-commerce platform with global reach; mobile optimization essential.
nfr_register.reasoning: 50%+ of e-commerce traffic is mobile. 3G still common in developing countries (emerging market for e-commerce). DeFOSPAM identifies global multi-currency/multi-language support; mobile is critical for conversion globally. Network RTT on 4G ~80ms, on 3G ~200ms; impacts all HTTP requests.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement: (1) Mobile-first responsive design: optimize for small screens first, enhance for larger. (2) Optimize images: use JPEG for photos (80-85% quality), WebP for modern browsers, inline SVGs for icons. (3) Code splitting: split JavaScript by page/feature; load only code needed. (4) Async/defer script loading: critical JS inline, rest deferred. (5) Minimize HTTP requests: combine CSS files, use CSS sprites or inline SVGs, consolidate static assets. (6) Compression: gzip all text assets (HTML, CSS, JS); enable Brotli for supported browsers. (7) Service Worker: enable offline caching for critical pages/images. (8) Test on real mobile: use WebPageTest with 4G/3G throttling. Monitor Real User Monitoring (RUM) for mobile load times. Target: 3.5s for 4G, 6.5s for 3G.

nfr_register.title: Major Missing: Database Query Performance and Connection Pooling

component_budgets.note: Includes network latency between app and DB
Time to convert DB result to application object

nfr_register.sla: Individual database queries SHALL execute within 150ms (p95) including connection acquisition and result serialization. Queries exceeding 500ms SHALL be logged as slow queries and investigated. Connection pool SIZE: minimum 10 connections, maximum 50 connections. Pool timeout: max 30 seconds wait for available connection (return error if unavailable; don't queue indefinitely). Database SHALL maintain: (1) Query result caching (Redis) for cacheable queries, (2) N+1 query prevention (eager-load related entities), (3) Index optimization for frequently queried columns.
nfr_register.reasoning: Database latency is foundation of all feature latency. DeFOSPAM shows heavy database dependencies (cart, checkout, product display) but zero perf specs. Typical DB query latency: local queries 10-50ms, network latency adds 5-10ms per query. Connection pooling prevents connection exhaustion under load.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement: (1) Connection pooling: use HikariCP (Java), Pgbouncer (PostgreSQL), or equivalent with min 10, max 50 connections. (2) Slow query logging: log all queries > 100ms; target for optimization. (3) Query optimization: add indexes on foreign keys, WHERE clause columns, ORDER BY columns. (4) Eager loading: use JPA/Hibernate join fetch or similar to prevent N+1 queries. (5) Caching: use ORM caching (L2 cache) for frequently accessed entities (product, category). (6) Database replication: read replicas for read-heavy operations (product display, reviews). (7) Query result pagination: limit result set size; don't return 10,000 rows at once.

nfr_register.title: Major Missing: Caching Strategy and CDN Configuration

cache_hit_targets.note: Percentages represent % of requests served from cache

component_budgets.note: Served from client memory; no network latency

nfr_register.sla: CDN cache hit rate for static assets (images, CSS, JS, fonts) SHALL exceed 95%. Database query cache hit rate for cacheable queries SHALL exceed 80%. Full-page cache hit rate for public pages (product catalog, homepage) SHALL exceed 60%. Cache invalidation: automatic TTL-based (1 hour for product data, 24 hours for static assets) AND event-based (invalidate on product update, inventory change). Cache-Control headers: public, max-age=3600 for product data; max-age=86400 for static assets. Vary header: Vary: Accept-Encoding, Accept-Language for multi-language content.
nfr_register.reasoning: Caching can reduce latency by 5-10x. DeFOSPAM shows zero caching requirements. E-commerce platforms heavily benefit from caching: product catalog rarely changes, customer access patterns predictable. Multi-layer caching: CDN (edge), Redis (application), browser cache (client), database query cache.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement: (1) CDN: CloudFront, Akamai, or Cloudflare for static assets. TTL: 1 year for versioned assets (app.bundle.abc123.js), 1 hour for index.html. (2) Object store: S3 for static assets with CloudFront. (3) Redis cache: 64GB minimum cluster for product catalog + pricing. Eviction: LRU with 1-hour TTL for product data. (4) Database query cache: ORM-level caching (JPA L2 cache) or query result caching in Redis. (5) Page-level caching: cache full product pages for 1 hour (invalidate on stock change). (6) Browser caching: set Cache-Control headers; enable service worker for offline caching. (7) Cache warming: pre-load popular products/categories on deployment. (8) Monitor cache hit rates; target >= 90% for CDN, >= 80% for query cache.

nfr_register.title: Major Missing: Asynchronous Processing for Long-Running Operations

end_to_end_ms.note: Time for user to see confirmation page

async_operations.note: All taken out of HTTP response path; processed in background

nfr_register.sla: Operations that cannot be completed within response time budget SHALL be made asynchronous: (1) Email sending: max 1 second to queue email job; actual delivery within 5 minutes. (2) Inventory updates: update cache synchronously, update database asynchronously. (3) Coupon validation: complex coupon rules cached; recalculate in background if needed. (4) Order notifications: send within 5 minutes (async), not in checkout response. User SHALL see confirmation page immediately after checkout, not wait for all backend tasks to complete.
nfr_register.reasoning: Long-running operations block user response. Email sending, coupon calculation, inventory updates can be deferred. DeFOSPAM shows email requirements (welcome email, order confirmation) but no async processing spec. Moving work out of request path dramatically improves perceived performance.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement: (1) Message queue: RabbitMQ, SQS, or Kafka for async jobs. (2) Job queue structure: Email service (consume email jobs), Inventory service (update inventory), Coupon service (validate/recalculate). (3) Retry logic: exponential backoff for failed jobs (retry 3 times over 1 hour). (4) Idempotency: ensure jobs idempotent (safe to retry). (5) Monitoring: track queue depth, job latency, failure rate. Alert if queue depth > 10,000 or job latency > 5 minutes. (6) Priority queues: high-priority jobs (order confirmation email) prioritized over low-priority (marketing emails). (7) Dead-letter queue: capture permanently failed jobs for manual review.

nfr_register.title: Major Missing: Real User Monitoring (RUM) and Synthetic Monitoring Strategy

monitoring_targets.note: Sample 10% of real users (balance accuracy vs. cost)

synthetic_monitoring.note: Run every 60 seconds from multiple global locations

nfr_register.sla: Monitor user experience metrics continuously: (1) Real User Monitoring (RUM) - track page load time, resource latency, Core Web Vitals for 10% of users. (2) Synthetic monitoring - run automated tests from global locations every 60 seconds, alert if latency exceeds budget by >10%. (3) Alerting - alert on p95 latency exceeding budget for >5 minutes (indicates degradation). (4) Dashboard - public-facing dashboard showing system health and performance. (5) Reporting - weekly reports on performance trends.
nfr_register.reasoning: DeFOSPAM identified zero monitoring requirements. Without monitoring, latency regressions go undetected. Real user data captures actual experience (not controlled lab test). Synthetic tests ensure global availability.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement: (1) RUM tool: Datadog RUM, New Relic Browser, Google Analytics, or Sentry. (2) Synthetic monitoring: Datadog Synthetic, Synthetic Monitoring, Pingdom, or UptimeRobot. (3) APM tool: Datadog, New Relic, Dynatrace, or Elastic APM to track application latency. (4) Logging: structured logging (ELK stack) to correlate latency with errors. (5) Alerts: set up PagerDuty alerts for latency > budget. (6) Dashboards: Grafana/Kibana dashboards showing p50/p90/p95 latency trends. (7) Publish SLO dashboard: show % of requests meeting latency SLA (target 99%+).

nfr_register.title: Major Missing: Concurrent User Capacity and Load Testing Requirements

concurrent_users.note: Typical e-commerce: 1-5% of daily users are concurrent

sla_under_load.note: Latency may increase 50% under peak load; SLA relaxed slightly

nfr_register.sla: System SHALL support minimum 5,000 concurrent users (peak load) with p95 latency not exceeding 3000ms. Average load 1,000 concurrent users with p95 latency <= 2200ms (normal SLA). Load testing: conduct monthly load test simulating 5,000 concurrent users across all key features (product browse, search, cart, checkout). Sustained load test: run 1,000 concurrent users for 4 hours; system must maintain SLA throughout (no degradation over time).
nfr_register.reasoning: DeFOSPAM critical finding: zero capacity requirements. nopCommerce platform is public e-commerce; must handle concurrent users. Peak traffic times (sales, holidays) can spike 5-10x normal. Without capacity planning, system fails under load.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement: (1) Load testing tool: JMeter, Gatling, or Locust for simulating concurrent users. (2) Monthly load test: create test plan for 5,000 concurrent users, 500 tps peak. (3) Test scenarios: homepage browse, product search (1s result), add to cart, checkout flow. (4) Infrastructure scaling: use load balancer (AWS ELB, nginx) with auto-scaling groups. (5) Database scaling: replicate for reads, master-slave setup. (6) Monitoring during load test: track CPU, memory, database connections, queue depths. (7) Spike testing: test 10,000 concurrent users briefly to see failure mode (graceful degradation). (8) Document capacity: publish capacity metrics (max concurrent users, max tps) to inform infrastructure decisions.

nfr_register.title: Missing Usability Performance Targets for Authentication Transactions
nfr_register.reasoning: Login and registration are critical entry points to the e-commerce platform. DeFOSPAM identified 25 features but no usability performance targets for auth flows. Noyce defined latency budget (1.2s p95) but missing user satisfaction thresholds, error recovery targets, and mobile 3G performance. Long auth latency directly causes cart abandonment and lost conversions. Industry data shows login latency > 3s reduces NPS by 15+ points.
nfr_register.recommendation: Define authentication performance SLAs: (1) Login task completion: p95 <= 1.2s WiFi, 1.5s 4G, 2.5s 3G. (2) Registration task completion: p95 <= 1.2s all networks. (3) Error message display: < 0.5s. (4) Form validation feedback: < 200ms client-side. (5) Password reset email delivery: within 5 minutes (async, not blocking). (6) Track abandonment rate: < 2% at 10s threshold. (7) Monitor NPS correlation: target NPS >= 70 for auth journeys. (8) Test on real 3G devices (OnePlus, mid-range Android); support baseline 2G fallback.

nfr_register.title: Missing Mobile 3G Performance Targets in Latency Budgets
nfr_register.reasoning: Noyce performance budgets define WiFi and 4G targets but omit 3G. Yet 15-25% of global e-commerce traffic comes from 3G networks (emerging markets, rural areas, poor signal areas). nopCommerce targets international markets. 3G latency (150ms) is 7x WiFi latency (20ms). A 1.2s WiFi target becomes 8.4s on 3G without optimization. This violates the <3s acceptable threshold, causing 40%+ abandonment. DeFOSPAM gap analysis found no 3G usability targets.
nfr_register.recommendation: Define 3G performance budgets for all critical features: (1) Implement 2x latency multiplier for 3G targets (WiFi target * 2-2.5). (2) Catalog Pages: 2.2s WiFi -> 4.5s 3G. (3) Search: 1.2s WiFi -> 2.5s 3G. (4) Add to Cart: 0.8s WiFi -> 1.5s 3G. (5) Checkout: 9.0s WiFi -> 18s 3G. (6) Optimize for 3G: reduce image quality on slow networks; defer non-critical JS; compress APIs responses aggressively. (7) Use adaptive bitrate for images (responsive images, WebP with PNG fallback). (8) Test with Chrome DevTools 'Slow 3G' throttling. (9) Set alerts: page load > 5s triggers investigation. (10) Monitor 3G users separately; track abandonment rate per network tier.

nfr_register.title: Accessibility Performance Gap: WCAG 2.1 Level AA Not Integrated with Usability Metrics
nfr_register.reasoning: DeFOSPAM found missing WCAG 2.1 Level AA accessibility requirements (alt text, keyboard navigation, color contrast, ARIA labels, focus indicators). However, no usability performance targets define the impact of accessibility on perceived performance. Example: alt text bloats HTML; ARIA labels increase DOM nodes; screen reader compatibility may slow time-to-interactive. Conflicting goal: accessibility must not degrade performance for sighted users. 15% of users have disabilities; another 30% use accessibility features occasionally (colorblindness, dyslexia, aging eyes). For screen reader users, semantic HTML + ARIA navigation speed is critical.
nfr_register.recommendation: Define accessibility performance targets: (1) Semantic HTML overhead: <= 5% page size increase. (2) ARIA landmarks: must load before interactive (< 2.5s TTI). (3) Alt text delivery: use data attributes or lazy-load for offscreen images; don't block render. (4) Screen reader testing: ensure form submission feedback occurs within 500ms. (5) Keyboard navigation: no additional latency vs mouse navigation. (6) Color contrast: use CSS custom properties (no JS recalc). (7) Focus indicators: CSS-only, instant visual feedback. (8) Audit quarterly: use axe DevTools + manual WCAG 2.1 testing. (9) Track accessibility-related abandonment separately. (10) Set NPS target >= 60 for users with assistive tech.

nfr_register.title: Internationalization (i18n) Has No Usability Performance Targets
nfr_register.reasoning: DeFOSPAM found missing i18n feature scope (no comprehensive localization requirements). nopCommerce is internationally distributed; users expect their language/currency instantly. Locale detection (Accept-Language, GeoIP, user preference) must be < 200ms and cached to avoid visible redirects or content flashing. Missing targets for: language switching latency, currency conversion accuracy, date/number format loading, RTL layout performance. Slow i18n degrades user confidence in international merchant.
nfr_register.recommendation: Define i18n usability performance targets: (1) Locale detection: < 200ms (pre-render, cached in CDN). (2) Language switching: no page reload; AJAX content swap < 500ms. (3) Currency conversion: cached rate data; refresh hourly; conversion calc < 50ms. (4) Date/number formatting: use locale JS Intl API; cached locale data < 100KB. (5) RTL layout flip: CSS-only, no JS reflow. (6) Translation file size: < 50KB per language (gzip). (7) Fallback: if translation missing, show default language instantly (not blank). (8) Test with RTL languages (Arabic, Hebrew) on RTL devices. (9) Monitor per-locale metrics: NPS, conversion rate. (10) Avoid visible redirects (e.g., domain.com -> domain.com/en -> domain.com/ar); detect + serve correct locale on first request.

nfr_register.title: Form Abandonment and Error Recovery Metrics Not Defined
nfr_register.reasoning: nopCommerce has 8+ forms (registration, billing address, shipping address, payment, review submission). DeFOSPAM scenarios detail form fields but no error recovery metrics. Missing: error message display time, field highlighting, client vs server validation split, retry latency. Industry data: 35% of users abandon forms after 1 validation error if recovery is slow. No targets for perceived error recovery speed. Example: payment form error at step 3 of checkout must show error < 500ms and allow retry without re-entering previous steps.
nfr_register.recommendation: Define form error recovery targets: (1) Validation feedback: client-side < 200ms, server-side < 500ms. (2) Error display: clear message within 400ms, highlight field with red border + icon. (3) Field state: pre-fill valid fields, focus on first invalid field. (4) Retry: allow resubmit without page reload; preserve form state. (5) Multi-step forms: error on step N should not require re-entry of steps 1 to N-1. (6) Payment errors: show specific error (e.g., 'Declined: call bank'), not generic 'Payment failed'. (7) Abandonment tracking: monitor form abandonment per field (identify high-drop fields). (8) Test: measure time-to-recovery as p95, not just error display time. (9) Set targets: < 1% abandonment on retry attempt. (10) Monitor NPS for form-heavy journeys (registration, checkout).

nfr_register.title: Missing Cart Abandonment Metrics and Recovery Latency Targets
nfr_register.feature: Shopping Cart Management, Checkout Entry Point
nfr_register.reasoning: Checkout is the highest-value user action. Noyce defined cart latency targets (800ms p95 for add-to-cart). But no abandonment thresholds by step defined. DeFOSPAM scenarios describe checkout steps but no drop-rate targets. Missing: cart total recalculation latency (after coupon, tax calc), payment processing latency perception, confirmation latency. Industry: 70% of carts abandoned before checkout; 15-20% abandoned during checkout steps. Each 1s delay in checkout increases abandonment by 5%. No tracking of where users drop (address vs payment step).
nfr_register.recommendation: Define cart and checkout abandonment targets: (1) Add to cart: < 0.8s response, show success toast < 400ms. (2) Cart update: tax calc < 1s, coupon validation < 800ms. (3) Checkout step transitions: < 2s per step. (4) Address step: < 2s validation. (5) Shipping calc: < 2s (show loading spinner). (6) Payment processing: < 10s (show progress). (7) Order confirmation: < 15s from final submit. (8) Abandon thresholds: monitor via analytics; alert if step > 5s. (9) Set targets: < 8% abandonment at address, < 5% at payment. (10) Recovery: if payment times out, show 'Please wait' + retry option (don't force re-entry). (11) Post-abandonment: email user with cart recovery link + discount incentive within 1 hour.

nfr_register.title: Perceived Performance (Time to First Meaningful Paint) Not Quantified per Feature
nfr_register.feature: Product Display and Information, Catalog Navigation, Homepage
nfr_register.reasoning: Noyce defined response time budgets (latency). But 'response time' != 'perceived load time'. Users perceive site as loaded when key content is visible (FMP: first meaningful paint). Example: product page must show image + price + add-to-cart within 1.5s even if reviews, specs, related products load later. Missing: FCP (first contentful paint) targets, LCP (largest contentful paint) budgets, CLS (cumulative layout shift) from ads/images. DeFOSPAM describes 25 features but no perceived performance breakdown per feature. Industry benchmark: FMP > 3s increases bounce rate by 40%.
nfr_register.recommendation: Define perceived performance targets per feature: (1) Product Pages: FCP <= 0.8s, FMP <= 1.5s (image + price visible), LCP <= 2.5s (all critical content). (2) Catalog Pages: FCP <= 0.8s, FMP <= 1.2s (product grid visible). (3) Search Results: FCP <= 0.6s, FMP <= 1.2s (first 10 results visible). (4) Checkout: FCP <= 0.8s per step, FMP <= 1.5s (form visible, not loading). (5) Homepage: FCP <= 1.0s, FMP <= 1.8s (hero image or carousel visible). (6) Optimize: defer offscreen images (lazy-load), inline critical CSS, defer non-critical JS. (7) Measure: use RUM (real user monitoring) tools (Datadog, New Relic) to track FMP, LCP, CLS per feature. (8) Test: Lighthouse score target >= 90. (9) Set alerts: FMP > 3s triggers investigation. (10) Correlate FMP with bounce rate; target < 5% bounce on product pages.

nfr_register.title: Time-to-Interactive (TTI) Targets Missing for Dynamic Pages
nfr_register.feature: Product Display and Information, Checkout, Cart
nfr_register.reasoning: TTI measures when page is responsive to user interaction (click, scroll, type). Differs from FMP: page may show content but JS not loaded (no add-to-cart click handler). Missing TTI targets cause user frustration when they click 'Add to Cart' and page doesn't respond. DeFOSPAM scenarios define feature interactions but no TTI budgets. Example: product page shows image @ 1s FMP, but add-to-cart handler not attached until 3.5s (long JS parse/eval). User clicks @2s, sees nothing, clicks again, double-adds item.
nfr_register.recommendation: Define TTI targets: (1) Product Pages: TTI <= 2.5s (add-to-cart clickable). (2) Catalog: TTI <= 2.2s (category filters clickable). (3) Checkout: TTI <= 2.5s per step (all form fields clickable). (4) Cart: TTI <= 1.0s (update/remove buttons responsive). (5) Optimize: inline critical JS in <script> tags; defer non-critical JS with async/defer; use code splitting. (6) Measure: RUM tools report TTI metric. (7) Test: Lighthouse TTI score >= 85. (8) Monitor: alert if TTI > 5s. (9) User testing: record sessions; identify points where users attempt interaction before TTI (indicates UX pain). (10) Consider: skeleton loading screens to provide visual feedback during JS loading (perceived performance).

nfr_register.title: Mobile App Performance Targets Not Defined (Launch Time, Memory, Battery)
nfr_register.reasoning: nopCommerce may have native mobile app or progressive web app (PWA). Missing targets: cold app launch time, warm app launch time, memory footprint, battery drain. Industry: 53% of users uninstall app if launch > 3s; 30% abandon if memory > 200MB. No memory budget defined. 3G network + slow app launch = high abandonment. Missing offline capability targets (what features work offline for paid offline orders).
nfr_register.recommendation: Define mobile app performance targets: (1) Cold launch: < 2.0s from tap to home screen visible. (2) Warm launch: < 0.5s from tap (re-open). (3) Memory footprint: <= 150MB on mid-range device (3GB RAM). (4) Battery: < 5% drain per hour of heavy use (shopping). (5) Optimize: lazy-load product images; use ImageOptim/TinyPNG; pre-cache critical paths (home, category, search). (6) PWA: implement service worker for offline; cache API responses; show 'Offline' badge. (7) Offline features: browse cached catalog, view order history, add to cart (enqueue). (8) Monitor: track app store ratings (< 4.0 stars = app is slow). (9) Test on real devices: OnePlus 7 (mid-range Android), iPhone 12 (mid-range iOS). (10) Use Firebase Performance Monitoring or similar for RUM.

nfr_register.title: NPS/USS Integration Points Missing from Requirements
nfr_register.reasoning: No requirements define how performance metrics correlate with NPS (Net Promoter Score) or USS (Usable Systems Score). Industry research shows strong correlation: page load time <= 1s, NPS >= 75; page load > 3s, NPS <= 30. Missing: NPS measurement points, question phrasing, response rate targets. No targets for USS (System Usability Scale equivalent for performance). DeFOSPAM found 153+ requirements with zero usability performance metrics; this compounds missing satisfaction tracking.
nfr_register.recommendation: Define NPS/USS integration: (1) NPS survey triggers: after login, after search, after checkout completion, after order confirmation. (2) NPS questions: 'How likely to recommend our shopping experience? (0-10)' (avoid generic company NPS). (3) USS criteria: can user find products quickly, navigate easily, check out painlessly. (4) Correlation tracking: measure response time p95 vs concurrent NPS (sample >= 100 responses). (5) Targets: NPS >= 50 when page load <= 2s; NPS >= 25 when page load > 3s. (6) Segment analysis: NPS by network (WiFi vs 4G vs 3G), device (mobile vs desktop), geography. (7) Root cause: low NPS after checkout -> investigate payment latency; low NPS after search -> investigate result relevance + latency. (8) Monthly review: track NPS trend; alert if drops > 10 points. (9) Sample: collect NPS from 5-10% of users (minimize fatigue). (10) Publish: include NPS in monthly performance dashboard.

nfr_register.title: Search and Discovery Performance Targets Not Aligned with User Behavior
nfr_register.reasoning: Noyce defined search latency 1.2s p95. But missing: autocomplete latency (users expect < 200ms), filter/facet load time, pagination latency, result relevance metrics (not performance per se, but affects abandonment). Users often retry search if results seem irrelevant (high bounce rate). Missing: search success rate (% of queries returning results), search error rate (failed queries), search result scroll depth (do users see results or abandon page).
nfr_register.recommendation: Define search performance targets: (1) Autocomplete suggestions: < 200ms from 3rd character typed. (2) Search results: initial load < 1.2s (first 10-20 results). (3) Filters/facets: load within results page (< 1.2s total). (4) Pagination: next page load < 1.0s (AJAX, no full reload). (5) Advanced search: < 2s for complex queries (multiple filters). (6) No results: show 'No products found' < 500ms (not slow timeout). (7) Success metrics: search success rate >= 85% (user finds what they want on first search). (8) Abandonment: if search returns 0 results, 70% abandon; show 'Did you mean' suggestions or related searches. (9) Error rate: < 0.2% of searches fail (timeout, 500 error). (10) Relevance: track clicks-to-result (CTR); high CTR = good relevance. (11) Scroll depth: target 50% of users scroll to 3rd page of results (indicates engagement).

nfr_register.title: Image Loading and Progressive Content Delivery Strategy Not Specified
nfr_register.feature: Product Display and Information, Catalog Navigation, Homepage
nfr_register.reasoning: Product images are critical for e-commerce. Missing: image compression targets, lazy-load strategy, progressive image loading (blur-up, LQIP), WebP/PNG fallback, responsive image sizes per device. Example: 4MB product image on 4G = 3.2s download; unoptimized, this causes 2-3s latency addition to page load. Missing: carousel/gallery interaction latency (does swiping between images block page interaction?). No targets for image alt text delivery impact on accessibility.
nfr_register.recommendation: Define image loading strategy: (1) Image compression: JPEG <= 150KB per product, WebP <= 100KB (target). (2) Responsive images: use srcset/sizes; serve 400px image to mobile, 800px to desktop. (3) Lazy-load: defer offscreen images (below fold); load on scroll. (4) Progressive loading: show LQIP (low-quality image placeholder) or blur-up while high-res loads. (5) Carousel: preload next image on hover (no latency on swipe). (6) Format: WebP for modern browsers; PNG fallback for older browsers. (7) CDN: serve images from CDN with 1-year cache (versioned filenames). (8) Alt text: use data attributes; don't block image load. (9) Measurement: RUM tracking image load times per product page. (10) Test: measure Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) which includes images; target <= 2.5s.

nfr_register.title: TLS Termination Latency Overhead on Payment Authorization
nfr_register.reasoning: Payment authorization in Noyce's budget is 3500ms p95. TLS handshake (session reuse) adds ~45ms for HTTPS termination at load balancer level, plus ~25ms for certificate validation (OCSP stapling). Total TLS overhead: ~70ms. At peak load (100+ concurrent payment requests), TLS becomes a contention point if not properly tuned. Session resumption reduces this but adds certificate validation latency per unique client.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement: (1) TLS session resumption (tickets or cache) to reduce handshake overhead to ~5ms for returning clients, (2) OCSP stapling on load balancer to avoid online certificate validation, (3) Hardware acceleration for AES-NI if on dedicated hardware, (4) Separate payment gateway connection with dedicated TLS connection pool (keep-alive), (5) Monitor TLS handshake latency via APM; alert if >100ms.

sub_findings.issue: Certificate validation adds 25ms per new client TLS connection
sub_findings.impact: First-time payer experience at p95 extends by 25ms
sub_findings.mitigation: OCSP stapling + session resumption

sub_findings.issue: Payment gateway TLS connection is separate from web server TLS
sub_findings.impact: Double TLS overhead: client-to-nopCommerce (~45ms) + nopCommerce-to-gateway (~50ms)
sub_findings.mitigation: Use gateway API connection pooling and HTTP/2 multiplexing

nfr_register.title: Password Hash Verification Intentional Delay as Security Control
nfr_register.reasoning: Noyce's login budget is 1200ms p95. bcrypt at cost factor 12 (recommended) intentionally delays hash verification by ~200ms to prevent brute-force attacks. This is a security-by-design choice that directly reduces throughput: at 100 concurrent logins, this means only ~500 logins/second max vs. ~2500 without hashing delay. This is acceptable for security but EXCEEDS current budget allocation.
nfr_register.recommendation: Revise budget upward: (1) Increase login p95 target from 1200ms to 1500ms to accommodate 200ms bcrypt overhead, (2) Document that this 200ms delay is intentional and non-negotiable for security, (3) Implement parallel login request limiting to prevent hash-verification DoS: max 50 concurrent authentication attempts from single IP, (4) Use adaptive hashing: monitor login latency; if >1500ms, reduce concurrent auth processing, (5) Consider GPU-accelerated bcrypt for payment gateway SSO scenarios (if used).

sub_findings.issue: Current budget assumes faster hashing; bcrypt is slower by design
sub_findings.impact: p95 budget exceeded at peak load; SLA violations possible
sub_findings.mitigation: Accept 200ms overhead; increase budget to 1500ms

sub_findings.issue: Argon2 is slower than bcrypt (250-350ms) but more resistant to GPU attacks
sub_findings.impact: If migrating to Argon2, login latency extends further
sub_findings.mitigation: Use Argon2id with parameters tuned for 150ms max delay

nfr_register.title: JWT Token Signature Validation Bottleneck at Scale
nfr_register.reasoning: Every authenticated request requires JWT signature validation using RS256 (RSA 2048-bit). Each validation costs ~8ms on CPU. At peak load (1000 concurrent users × 2 requests/sec = 2000 req/sec), JWT validation consumes ~16 CPU cores (at 125 reqs/core/sec). If system has only 8 cores, JWT validation becomes the bottleneck. Session lookup (Redis 30ms) is faster but requires server-side session storage. Stateless JWT wins for scalability but costs CPU.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement: (1) JWT validation caching: for high-frequency API calls (search, cart), validate token only on first request; cache decision for 60 seconds, (2) Switch to HS256 (HMAC) for internal services where key sharing is feasible; ~0.5ms per validation vs. 8ms, (3) Use public key caching (Redis) to avoid re-fetching signing key per request, (4) Implement token validation with short expiry (15 min) to reduce validation frequency, (5) Monitor JWT validation latency; alert if >15ms, (6) For payment/admin APIs, keep RS256 but accept longer validation times; prioritize security over latency.

sub_findings.issue: RS256 validation is CPU-bound; scales linearly with concurrent users
sub_findings.impact: At 5000 concurrent users, JWT validation could consume 50% CPU
sub_findings.mitigation: Cache JWT decisions; use HS256 for internal APIs

sub_findings.issue: Public key rotation requires re-fetching key from key server
sub_findings.impact: Key rotation causes latency spikes if no cache
sub_findings.mitigation: Cache public key with TTL; fetch new key only on validation failure

nfr_register.title: Missing Rate Limiting Creates Login Brute-Force DoS Vector
nfr_register.reasoning: DeFOSPAM found: 'Missing Rate Limiting and Abuse Prevention' for login (REQ052 mentions lockout but not rate limiting). nopCommerce has account lockout on N failed attempts, but no rate limiting at HTTP request level. An attacker can send 1000 login attempts/second from different IPs, exhausting bcrypt CPU and creating denial of service. Lockout applies per email address; attacker can brute-force multiple email addresses in parallel. At 200ms per bcrypt verification, 5 concurrent logins exhaust 1 CPU core.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement rate limiting: (1) IP-based: max 5 login attempts per IP per 30 minutes (sliding window), (2) Email-based: max 10 attempts per email per hour (after 3 failures, notify user), (3) Distributed rate limiting via Redis to sync across servers, (4) Combine with WAF/DDoS mitigation: block IPs with >100 failed attempts in 5 minutes, (5) Implement exponential backoff: 1st failure = wait 1s, 2nd = 2s, 3rd = 4s (client-side + server-side enforcement), (6) Log all rate limit violations for anomaly detection, (7) SLA: login rate limiting shall not add >10ms latency via Redis lookup.

sub_findings.issue: Account lockout is per-email; attacker can enumerate valid emails and lock all
sub_findings.impact: Denial of service to customer accounts; mass account lockout
sub_findings.mitigation: Implement CAPTCHA after 3 failed attempts per IP

sub_findings.issue: No protection against credential stuffing attacks (valid email + common passwords)
sub_findings.impact: Compromised credentials from other breaches can be tested at scale
sub_findings.mitigation: Check credentials against HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) database; block known-bad combinations

nfr_register.title: Missing Rate Limiting on Password Reset Creates Email Flooding Vector
nfr_register.reasoning: DeFOSPAM identified: 'Missing Rate Limiting for password reset emails'. An attacker can submit unlimited password reset requests for any email address, causing: (1) Email bombing to victim inbox, (2) Email service quota exhaustion (if using SMTP rate limits), (3) Potential email delivery blacklisting if from IP sends too many emails, (4) Token validation lookups in database create query overhead.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement rate limiting on password reset: (1) Max 3 reset requests per email per hour (sliding window), (2) Max 5 reset requests per IP per 30 minutes, (3) Implement exponential backoff in UI: disable reset button for 60 seconds after request, (4) Store rate limit state in Redis for fast lookup; add <5ms latency, (5) Log all reset attempts; alert if >10 attempts per email in 1 hour, (6) Consider CAPTCHA after 2 reset attempts per IP, (7) Send 'suspicious activity' email if reset requested multiple times; user can click 'block' to cancel all pending resets.

sub_findings.issue: No rate limit on password reset endpoint; unbounded email generation
sub_findings.impact: Email flooding; service quota exhaustion; IP blacklisting
sub_findings.mitigation: 3 resets/email/hour; 5 resets/IP/30min

sub_findings.issue: Reset tokens are typically long-lived (24-48 hours); unused tokens accumulate
sub_findings.impact: Database grows with stale tokens; token validation queries slow
sub_findings.mitigation: Implement token cleanup job; delete tokens >48 hours old daily

nfr_register.title: Missing Rate Limiting on Search API Enables Index Exhaustion DoS
nfr_register.reasoning: Noyce's search budget is 1200ms p95. An attacker can submit 100 complex search queries/second (e.g., wildcard searches, full-text searches on large text fields), consuming search index resources and causing p95 to extend to 5000ms+. At 10 search servers, this is 100 req/sec = 10 req/sec per server, which can trigger auto-scaling. Attacker goal: exhaust search infrastructure and force unnecessary scaling costs.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement search rate limiting: (1) Per-IP: max 10 search queries per minute (sliding window), (2) Per-session (authenticated user): max 20 searches per minute, (3) Per-IP: max 5 concurrent search requests, (4) Implement search query timeout: 5 seconds max execution; return 'too complex' error, (5) Add request size limit: max 2000 characters per search query, (6) Implement search result pagination enforcement: max 50 results per page (prevent offset >50 to avoid deep pagination abuse), (7) Monitor search latency; alert if p95 exceeds 2000ms, (8) Rate limit breach latency: <3ms Redis lookup.

sub_findings.issue: Pagination offset can be exploited: requesting offset=1000000 forces index scan
sub_findings.impact: Database CPU exhaustion; slow queries affect all users
sub_findings.mitigation: Limit max offset to 50 * max_page_num; implement keyset pagination

sub_findings.issue: Wildcard searches (e.g., *) with leading wildcard force full index scan
sub_findings.impact: Single query can use 100% CPU on search server
sub_findings.mitigation: Disallow leading wildcards; use MIN_NGRAM=2 in search index

nfr_register.title: Encryption at Rest (Database) Has Minimal Performance Impact if Properly Tuned

security_performance.control: Database encryption at rest (TDE or file-level)

nfr_register.reasoning: DeFOSPAM identified PCI-DSS compliance gap: 'Missing encryption of payment data at rest'. Modern database encryption (SQL Server TDE, PostgreSQL pgcrypto, MySQL InnoDB encrypted tables) uses hardware acceleration (AES-NI) and operates at block storage level. Overhead is typically 2-3% on disk I/O due to encryption/decryption. At 1000 req/sec with 100ms database latency, 2-3% overhead = 2-3ms added latency per request. This is acceptable and non-negotiable for PCI compliance.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement database encryption at rest: (1) Enable Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) on SQL Server or equivalent on PostgreSQL/MySQL, (2) Use hardware AES acceleration (available on all modern CPUs), (3) Ensure key management: store encryption key in Azure Key Vault / AWS KMS (not on same database server), (4) Monitor encryption/decryption latency: set up CloudWatch metrics to track encrypted disk I/O, (5) Accept 2-3% throughput overhead as non-negotiable security cost, (6) Test failover with encrypted database; encryption key must be accessible on standby, (7) Document: 'All payment data at rest is encrypted per PCI-DSS 3.4'.

sub_findings.issue: Encryption key management is critical; key loss = data loss
sub_findings.impact: If encryption key is lost, database is unrecoverable
sub_findings.mitigation: Use managed key service (Azure KMS); enable key rotation every 90 days

sub_findings.issue: Column-level encryption (for highly sensitive fields) has 5-10% overhead
sub_findings.impact: If encrypting payment card fields individually, overhead increases
sub_findings.mitigation: Use column encryption only for card numbers/CVV; rest use TDE

nfr_register.title: Connection Pooling and Session Limit DoS Prevention
nfr_register.reasoning: Without connection limits, an attacker can open 10,000 idle TCP connections to web servers, exhausting file descriptor limits (typically 65,536 per process, 1,024 per user on Linux). This causes new connections to fail immediately ('connection reset'). nopCommerce runs behind load balancer; without per-client connection limits, attacker can still exhaust connection pools. Solution: implement max 50 concurrent connections per IP, with graceful degradation (queue requests).
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement connection and session limits: (1) Max 50 concurrent TCP connections per client IP (enforced at LB), (2) Max 5000 active sessions per application server instance, (3) Idle connection timeout: 600 seconds (10 min); close inactive sessions, (4) Database connection pool: min 10, max 50 per app server (total: max 400 connections on 8-server farm), (5) Connection limit breach response: close oldest idle connection; if no idle connections, queue request for 30 seconds, (6) Implement connection pooling latency monitoring; queue wait should be <5ms, (7) Log connection limit breaches for traffic analysis.

sub_findings.issue: Slow-read attacks: attacker opens connection, reads 1 byte/second from response
sub_findings.impact: Exhausts server threads; each connection waits for full response read
sub_findings.mitigation: Implement read timeout: 30 seconds max per response chunk

sub_findings.issue: Session table can grow unbounded if cleanup job fails
sub_findings.impact: Database bloat; session lookups slow; memory exhaustion
sub_findings.mitigation: Implement automatic session cleanup every 30 min; delete sessions >24 hours old

nfr_register.title: DDoS Mitigation Strategy and WAF Performance Requirements
nfr_register.reasoning: nopCommerce is public e-commerce; subject to volumetric DDoS (UDP floods), application-layer DDoS (HTTP floods), and state-exhaustion attacks (SYN floods). Without DDoS mitigation, a single 1 Gbps attack can take down unprotected infrastructure. WAF latency adds ~15ms per request (rule evaluation). CDN edge filtering (Cloudflare, AWS Shield) adds <5ms and filters attacks before reaching origin.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement layered DDoS mitigation: (1) LAYER 1 (Infrastructure): AWS Shield Standard (free, automatic) or equivalent DDoS mitigation at cloud provider level; blocks volumetric attacks, (2) LAYER 2 (Edge): Use CDN with DDoS protection (Cloudflare, Akamai, AWS CloudFront); inspect requests at edge, filter malicious traffic, (3) LAYER 3 (Application): Deploy WAF (AWS WAF, ModSecurity, Imperva) at load balancer; rules: (a) Block IPs with >100 req/min to any endpoint, (b) Block IPs with >50 failed logins in 5 min, (c) Block requests with malformed headers, (d) Block requests with SQL injection or XSS payloads, (4) Rate limiting: as specified in NFR-SEC-004, NFR-SEC-005, NFR-SEC-006, (5) Graceful degradation: under attack, shed non-critical features (recommendations, wishlists) and prioritize checkout, (6) WAF latency SLA: <20ms per rule evaluation.

sub_findings.issue: HTTP/2 or HTTP/3 connection multiplexing can be exploited: attacker opens 1000 streams per connection
sub_findings.impact: Server allocates memory per stream; DoS without proper stream limits
sub_findings.mitigation: Limit max streams per connection to 100; prioritize payment/auth streams

sub_findings.issue: Slow-POST attacks: attacker sends HTTP body 1 byte/second
sub_findings.impact: Server holds connection open; threads exhausted
sub_findings.mitigation: Implement read timeout: 30 seconds for full request body

sub_findings.issue: WAF rule evaluation is CPU-intensive; rules can create CPU spike
sub_findings.impact: WAF becomes bottleneck; legitimate traffic blocked
sub_findings.mitigation: Benchmark WAF rules; ensure <20ms latency per request

nfr_register.title: OAuth 2.0 / SSO Token Validation Performance Under Peak Load
nfr_register.reasoning: If nopCommerce integrates OAuth 2.0 SSO (e.g., Microsoft Entra, Google, Okta), each authenticated request requires token validation via remote endpoint (e.g., /oauth/introspect). This is an out-of-process, network-dependent call: 150ms baseline + network latency. At peak (2000 req/sec), this is 300 HTTP calls/sec to OAuth provider. If provider returns 500ms due to outage, all traffic to nopCommerce is blocked (cascading failure).
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement OAuth token validation caching: (1) Cache token validity in Redis with TTL = token expiry (typically 1 hour), (2) On cache miss, validate token with OAuth provider; add <200ms, (3) If OAuth provider unavailable, accept cached token decision for 5 minutes (graceful degradation), (4) Implement token validation batching: bundle 10 token lookups per request to OAuth provider (reduces round trips), (5) Use OAuth provider's token introspection endpoint with optimized parameters; request only {active, scope, exp}, (6) Monitor token validation latency; alert if >250ms, (7) Separate OAuth cache from session cache (different Redis instance/cluster) to prevent contention, (8) For SSO, accept 200ms additional latency at login; prioritize cached validation for subsequent API calls.

sub_findings.issue: OAuth provider outage blocks all authenticated requests (cascading failure)
sub_findings.impact: Single point of failure for entire authentication system
sub_findings.mitigation: Implement fallback: use cached token validity for 5 minutes if provider unreachable

sub_findings.issue: Token introspection endpoint has rate limits; provider may throttle nopCommerce
sub_findings.impact: High-concurrency traffic hits OAuth provider rate limits
sub_findings.mitigation: Request higher rate limit quota from OAuth provider; implement token caching

nfr_register.title: Session Management Scalability: Redis vs. Database Sessions
nfr_register.reasoning: Noyce's budget allocates 30ms for 'Session Lookup (Redis)'. At 10,000 concurrent users (peak nopCommerce load), this is 10,000 Redis keys in memory. Redis lookup is O(1), taking ~0.1ms. However, if sessions stored in database instead, lookup takes 50-100ms (network + query). Scaling: Redis cluster with 3 nodes can handle 100,000 sessions; database table scales to millions but with linear latency increase. Session size is ~2KB (user_id, roles, cart_id, timestamp); 10,000 sessions = 20MB in Redis.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement Redis-backed sessions: (1) Store sessions in Redis cluster (3 nodes minimum for HA; avoid single-node SPOF), (2) Session key format: session::{session_id} (TTL = configured session timeout, default 30 min), (3) Session data: {user_id, roles, created_at, last_accessed, ip_address, user_agent}, (4) Implement session replication: Redis Sentinel or Redis Cluster for failover, (5) Monitor session count; alert if >100,000 sessions (indicates bot traffic or session leak), (6) Implement session timeout: if session idle >30 min, delete from Redis and force re-login, (7) Log session creation/deletion for audit, (8) Latency target: session lookup <5ms; alert if >10ms, (9) For stateless architecture (JWT), skip Redis sessions entirely; accept 8ms JWT validation cost instead.

sub_findings.issue: Session fixation attack: attacker sets session cookie to known value
sub_findings.impact: Attacker can hijack user session without password
sub_findings.mitigation: Generate cryptographically random session ID; invalidate on login

sub_findings.issue: Session reuse across browsers/devices can be exploited
sub_findings.impact: If session token exposed, any device can use it
sub_findings.mitigation: Include user_agent + ip_address in session validation; re-auth if mismatch

nfr_register.title: PCI-DSS Compliance Performance Implications: Tokenization vs. Direct Processing
nfr_register.reasoning: DeFOSPAM critical finding: 'Missing Payment Card Data PCI Compliance'. nopCommerce REQ090-091 assume direct card entry without tokenization. PCI-DSS REQUIRES one of three options: (1) Tokenization (nopCommerce receives token only, gateway stores card), (2) Hosted payment page (gateway hosts form, nopCommerce never sees card), (3) 3D Secure redirect (card issuer authenticates user). Option 1 adds ~50ms for gateway token call; Option 2 adds 200ms redirect + user interaction; Option 3 adds 500ms-5s (user entry required). Current budget (3500ms) is feasible with tokenization but NOT with SAQ D (no tokenization) which is non-compliant.
nfr_register.recommendation: Implement PCI-DSS compliant payment processing: (1) REQUIRED: Use tokenization (Option 1). Stripe/Braintree/Square APIs: merchant sends payment_method_token to gateway; gateway returns authorization token. Adds ~50ms. (2) Rewrite REQ090-091: 'System SHALL accept only payment tokens (not raw card data). Tokens generated by gateway API or hosted payment form. System SHALL never store, transmit, or log raw PAN (Primary Account Number), CVV, or expiration date.' (3) Verify PCI compliance: (a) Deploy nopCommerce in 'SAQ A' or 'SAQ A-EP' scope (minimal card handling), (b) Annual PCI-DSS assessment by Qualified Security Assessor (QSA), (c) Provide proof of PCI compliance to customers on request, (4) Adjust payment authorization latency: original 1500ms (gateway) + 50ms (tokenization) = 1550ms is acceptable within 3500ms budget, (5) Implement PCI compliance monitoring: audit logs for all card-related operations, encryption validation, access control checks.

sub_findings.issue: If database is breached, tokenization prevents card exposure (attacker gets tokens, not cards)
sub_findings.impact: Tokens are single-use or time-limited; useless to attacker without gateway access
sub_findings.mitigation: Tokenization is mandatory; no direct card storage allowed

sub_findings.issue: 3D Secure (3DS) adds 500ms-5s due to user authentication step
sub_findings.impact: Payment p95 could extend to 5000-10000ms with 3DS enabled
sub_findings.mitigation: Use frictionless 3DS (issuer authenticates without challenge); fallback to challenge if needed

sub_findings.issue: Scope: nopCommerce itself must be in SAQ A (minimal card data handling)
sub_findings.impact: Reduces PCI audit scope; lowers compliance cost
sub_findings.mitigation: Ensure zero raw card data in nopCommerce; all cards tokenized by gateway

findings.title: Platform Concurrent User Capacity
findings.reasoning: A nopCommerce e-commerce platform needs to support varying concurrent user loads throughout the day and during peak events (flash sales, seasonal shopping). The missing 'Page Load Time and Scalability' NFR identified by DeFOSPAM leaves no baseline for capacity planning. Industry benchmarks for mid-sized e-commerce: 400-600 average concurrent users, 2000-3000 peak during normal operations, scaling to 2500-5000 peak during promotional events. This platform lacks explicit requirements.
findings.recommendation: Add NFR-CAP-001: 'The system SHALL support a minimum concurrent user capacity of 500 average users and 2,500 peak users during standard operations. During promotional periods and flash sales, the system SHALL elastically scale to handle up to 5,000 concurrent users. The platform SHALL monitor concurrent user load continuously and trigger auto-scaling when load exceeds 70% of current capacity. The system SHALL maintain response time SLAs (see NFR-LAT series) at all load levels up to peak capacity.'

findings.title: Transaction Volume Per Feature
findings.reasoning: Shopping Cart (F04) is a write-heavy feature with 2,500 average transactions/hour and potential 10,000 peak. This includes add-to-cart, quantity updates, coupon application, and cart removals. Checkout features (F10-F14) drive order creation at peak rates. Product Search (F05) at 2,000 average queries/hour requires efficient indexing. The system lacks defined transaction rate SLOs per feature, making capacity planning impossible.
findings.recommendation: Add NFR-CAP-002: 'The system SHALL support: (1) Shopping Cart: 2,500 transactions/hour average (add-to-cart, update qty, apply coupon), 10,000 peak. (2) Product Search: 2,000 queries/hour average, 8,000 peak. (3) Checkout: 300 orders/hour average (complete transactions), 2,500 peak. (4) Product Display: 3,000 page views/hour average, 12,000 peak. Transaction processing SHALL be load-balanced across app servers, with write-optimized indexes on shopping_cart and orders tables. Database connection pool SHALL be sized to 200 total connections (120 for app tier).'

findings.title: Database Storage Growth and Retention
findings.feature: Order Management (F18), Product Catalog (F06-F07), Audit Logging (missing NFR)
findings.reasoning: Database storage will grow significantly over 24 months: Orders grow at 30% YoY (100K initial rows to 400K in 24mo = 500GB to 2GB storage). Product images at 100K+ images consume 50GB+ storage. Audit logs (identified as missing NFR) grow at 45% YoY (3M rows, 3GB/year). No data retention policy exists (DeFOSPAM finding), meaning infinite data accumulation. Without growth projections and retention policies, storage costs become unbounded.
findings.recommendation: Add NFR-CAP-003: 'The system SHALL implement data growth projections and storage management: (1) Order data: Support 400K orders (3GB) at 24 months with 30% YoY growth. (2) Product images: Support 100K+ images (50GB) at 24 months with 25% YoY growth. (3) Audit logs: Retain 730 days (2 years) maximum; auto-archive to cold storage after 90 days, delete after 730 days (3GB/year). (4) Reviews/ratings: Support 500K reviews (2GB) at 24 months with 40% YoY growth. (5) Database storage targets: 120GB at 12 months, 350GB at 24 months. (6) Implement automated backups: Daily full backups (retained 7 days), weekly backups (retained 4 weeks), monthly backups (retained 12 months).'

findings.title: Web Server Sizing and Load Distribution
findings.reasoning: Web tier (reverse proxy/load balancer + static content servers) must handle HTTP/HTTPS termination, SSL/TLS overhead, static asset serving (images, CSS, JS), and request routing to app tier. At 500 avg concurrent users with avg response time 200ms, each web server (assuming 4-core, 8GB RAM) can handle ~100-125 concurrent users. 500 avg / 125 = 4 web servers minimum. Peak 2500 concurrent users = 20 web servers needed, but traffic shaping and CDN can reduce this to 8-10 with caching.
findings.recommendation: Add NFR-CAP-004: 'The system SHALL provision: (1) Web servers: 4 minimum (average load), auto-scale to 8-10 at peak (2,500+ concurrent users). Each web server: 4 vCPU, 8GB RAM, 50GB SSD. (2) Load balancer (hardware or cloud-native): Support 10 Gbps throughput, health checks every 5 seconds, connection timeout 30 seconds, request timeout 60 seconds. (3) Static content delivery: Serve static assets (images, CSS, JS) from CDN with 24-hour cache TTL for versioned assets, 1-hour TTL for HTML. (4) SSL/TLS termination: Use TLS 1.2+, terminate SSL at load balancer, enable session resumption. (5) Monitor: CPU <70%, memory <80%, disk I/O <60% at average load.'

findings.title: Application Server Pool Sizing
findings.reasoning: Application servers execute business logic, access databases, call external APIs (payment, shipping, address validation), and process transactions. At 500 avg concurrent users with ~50-100 ms average processing time per request, each app server (8-core, 16GB) can handle ~200-300 requests/second (depending on complexity). 1200 txn/hr avg = 0.33 txn/sec, but peak 8000 txn/hr = 2.22 txn/sec. Assuming 8 txn/sec per fully-loaded app server, need 6-8 app servers for capacity headroom.
findings.recommendation: Add NFR-CAP-005: 'The system SHALL provision: (1) Application servers: 6 minimum (average load), auto-scale to 12-15 at peak. Each app server: 8 vCPU, 16GB RAM, 100GB SSD. (2) Application framework: Use thread-pool-based async or reactive framework to handle concurrent requests efficiently. Thread pool size: 200 threads per app server. (3) Database connection pool: 120 connections total (app tier share), max 20 connections per app server, connection timeout 30 seconds, idle timeout 900 seconds. (4) Session storage: Store sessions in Redis (distributed cache), session timeout 30 minutes. (5) Performance SLOs: p50 response time 100ms, p95 200ms, p99 500ms during average load. At peak load, p95 <1 second. (6) Health checks: Liveness check every 10 seconds, readiness check every 5 seconds, graceful shutdown with 30-second drain period.'

findings.title: Database Primary and Replica Sizing
findings.reasoning: Database is the system bottleneck. Primary handles all writes + read-heavy transactions. Current 15GB grows to 350GB at 24 months. At 1200 txn/hr avg (2.2 txn/min), peak 8000 txn/hr (133 txn/min), database must support sustained IOPS. Shopping cart/order writes are high-frequency and require low-latency storage (NVMe SSD). Replica for read-heavy queries (product catalog, search, reports, order history) reduces primary load.
findings.recommendation: Add NFR-CAP-006: 'The system SHALL provision: (1) Primary database: 16 vCPU, 64GB RAM, 1000GB NVMe SSD. Database: MySQL 8.0 InnoDB or PostgreSQL 14+. (2) Read replica: 16 vCPU, 64GB RAM, 1000GB SSD, async replication lag <1 second. Route read-only queries (product catalog, search, reports) to replica. (3) Storage: Support 120GB at 12 months, 350GB at 24 months. Plan for 500GB by 36 months. Use tiered storage: hot (NVMe) for active data, warm (SSD) for historical orders, cold (archive) for audit logs >90 days. (4) Backup strategy: Daily full backup (7-day retention), weekly backup (4-week retention), monthly backup (12-month retention). RPO: 1 hour, RTO: 15 minutes. (5) Monitoring: Query response time p95 <500ms (primary), p95 <1000ms (replica), lock wait time <100ms, replication lag <1 second. (6) Connection pool: 200 total connections (app tier 120, admin/reporting 30, backup 20).'

findings.title: Cache Layer (Redis) Sizing and Strategy
findings.feature: Shopping Cart (F04), Product Catalog (F06-F07), User Authentication (F02/F17), Session Management
findings.reasoning: Cache layer (Redis) is critical for reducing database load, improving response times, and supporting session management. At 500 concurrent users, assume 1-2MB per session = 500-1000MB session storage. Product catalog (10K products + attributes) = 500MB. Shopping carts (avg 100K carts in-flight) = 500MB-1GB. Total estimate: 2-3GB working set. For peak 2500 users, estimate 10-15GB total cache.
findings.recommendation: Add NFR-CAP-007: 'The system SHALL provision Redis cache layer: (1) Cluster topology: 2 Redis nodes (master-replica) with 32GB RAM per node (64GB total), 50GB SSD per node. (2) Eviction policy: LRU (Least Recently Used) with TTL. (3) Use cases: (a) Session storage: TTL 30 minutes, 500-1000MB. (b) Shopping cart data: TTL 24 hours, 500MB-1GB. (c) Product catalog cache: TTL 12 hours, 500MB (refresh on product updates). (d) Category hierarchies: TTL 24 hours, 50MB. (e) Exchange rates: TTL 6 hours, 5MB. (f) User preferences: TTL 7 days, 100MB. (4) Replication: Async replication from master to replica, replication lag <100ms. (5) Persistence: RDB snapshots every 60 seconds, AOF for critical data (sessions, carts). (6) Monitoring: Hit rate >80%, memory usage <80%, replication lag <100ms. (7) Failover: Automatic failover with Sentinel or Redis Cluster, recovery time <10 seconds.'

findings.title: Content Delivery Network (CDN) Strategy
findings.reasoning: CDN reduces origin load by caching static assets (images, CSS, JS) at edge locations close to users. Product Display (F07) and Product Catalog (F06) are image-heavy, with 100K+ images totaling 50GB+. At 500 avg concurrent users viewing 3-5 images per session = 1500-2500 image requests/hour. CDN cache hit rate >90% can reduce origin bandwidth by 90% and improve page load times by 50-70%. Estimated egress traffic: 100GB/month without CDN, 10GB/month with CDN (90% reduction).
findings.recommendation: Add NFR-CAP-008: 'The system SHALL implement CDN for static content delivery: (1) Provider: CloudFront, Cloudflare, or Akamai with 200+ edge locations. (2) Cacheable content (60% of traffic): Product images (JPEG, PNG, WebP), CSS, JavaScript, fonts, SVG icons. (3) Cache TTLs: (a) Versioned assets (with hash in filename): 365 days. (b) Product images: 12 hours (refresh on product update). (c) HTML: 5 minutes (dynamic content). (d) API responses: 1 minute (for read-heavy endpoints). (4) Cache headers: Set Cache-Control, ETag, Last-Modified for optimal caching. (5) Compression: Enable gzip/brotli for text assets, serve WebP for images. (6) Performance targets: Cache hit rate >85%, edge latency p95 <100ms, origin latency p95 <500ms. (7) Estimated cost: $3,000/month for 100GB egress, 5TB storage. (8) Monitoring: Cache hit ratio, origin request rate, DDoS protection metrics.'

findings.title: Network Bandwidth and Egress Cost
findings.reasoning: Network bandwidth is a significant cloud cost driver. At 500 avg concurrent users, avg page size 2MB, avg session duration 20 minutes = ~300MB data transfer/user/session = 150GB/day = 4500GB/month. With peak 2500 users = 22.5TB/month peak. Without CDN caching (which provides 90% reduction), egress cost is $0.085/GB = $1,912/month for average, $19,125/month for peak. With CDN, egress cost drops to ~$200/month + CDN cost $3000/month = $3200 vs $1900 without optimization. Cost optimization is critical.
findings.recommendation: Add NFR-CAP-009: 'The system SHALL optimize network bandwidth and forecast egress costs: (1) Baseline traffic: 150GB/day average, 750GB/day peak (without CDN optimization). (2) With CDN caching (90% reduction): 15GB/day average origin traffic, 75GB/day peak origin traffic. (3) Monthly egress cost projection: (a) Without optimization: $1,912/month average, $19,125/month peak = $17,000/month annual average. (b) With CDN (preferred): $200/month origin egress + $3,000/month CDN = $3,200/month = $38,400/year. (c) Savings: 82% reduction in egress cost with CDN. (4) Additional costs: DDoS protection ($500/month), IP transit ($200/month). (5) Total network cost budget: $4,000/month ($48,000/year). (6) Optimization strategies: (a) Serve images in WebP format (30% smaller than JPEG). (b) Implement aggressive caching (24-hour TTL for product images). (c) Use CloudFront or Cloudflare for edge caching. (d) Compress text responses (gzip/brotli). (e) Lazy-load images on product pages. (f) Paginate results (product listings, reviews). (7) Monitoring: Track egress bandwidth, CDN hit ratio, page size, image optimization metrics.'

findings.title: Auto-Scaling Policy and Elasticity
findings.reasoning: E-commerce platforms experience highly variable traffic: weekend spikes, seasonal events (Black Friday, holidays), flash sales. Without auto-scaling policies, system either over-provisions (expensive) or underprovisioning (poor UX during peak). Auto-scaling should trigger on CPU >70%, memory >75%, or response time p95 >1s. Scale-down should be conservative (prevent thrashing) with 5-10 minute cooldown.
findings.recommendation: Add NFR-CAP-010: 'The system SHALL implement auto-scaling policies: (1) Web tier (reverse proxy/load balancer): Min 4 instances, max 10 instances. Scale up: CPU >70% for 2 minutes or request latency p95 >500ms for 1 minute. Scale down: CPU <40% for 5 minutes. (2) App tier: Min 6 instances, max 15 instances. Scale up: CPU >75% for 2 minutes or queue depth >10 pending requests. Scale down: CPU <50% for 5 minutes. (3) Database: Min 1 read replica, max 3 replicas. Scale up: Replica lag >2 seconds or CPU >80% for 3 minutes. No auto-scale down for replicas (stateful). (4) Cache tier: Min 2 nodes (master-replica), max 5 nodes. Scale up: Memory >80% or eviction rate >10%. Scale down: Memory <50% for 10 minutes. (5) Scaling launch time: Web tier <2 minutes, app tier <3 minutes (due to JVM warmup), cache <1 minute. (6) Cost optimization: Use reserved instances for 60% of capacity (minimum baseline), spot instances for scale-up (save 70% vs on-demand). (7) Monitoring: Trigger alerts on scale-up/down events, failed scale attempts, cascade failures (e.g., app tier scale fails due to DB connection limits).'

findings.title: Audit Logging Infrastructure Capacity
findings.reasoning: DeFOSPAM identified 'Missing Audit Logging for Sensitive Actions' as a critical gap. Audit logs must record all sensitive actions: customer account changes, payment transactions, refunds, admin discounts, inventory adjustments, admin login/logout. Assuming 1200 txn/hour avg = 1 audit event per transaction + 100 customer account updates/hour = ~1300 audit events/hour = ~31K/day. Each log entry ~300 bytes = 9.3MB/day = 280MB/month = 3.36GB/year. Immutable, tamper-proof storage required for compliance (PCI-DSS, SOC 2).
findings.recommendation: Add NFR-CAP-011: 'The system SHALL provision audit logging infrastructure: (1) Audit log volume: 1,300 events/hour average (1 per transaction + customer updates), 8,000 events/hour peak. (2) Storage: ~3GB at 12 months, 5-10GB at 24 months depending on log retention. (3) Audit log schema: timestamp, user_id, action_type (CREATE, UPDATE, DELETE), resource_type, resource_id, old_value, new_value, ip_address, session_id, status (success/failure), error_message. (4) Immutability: Write audit logs to append-only table or AWS CloudTrail / Google Cloud Audit Logs equivalent. Use cryptographic hashing (SHA-256) to detect tampering. (5) Retention: Hot storage (database) for 90 days, warm storage (S3/archive) for 730 days (2 years per PCI-DSS), cold storage (glacier) for long-term archive. (6) Access control: Audit logs readable only by authorized admins and compliance officers; deletion strictly prohibited. (7) Performance: Audit logging must not block business transactions. Use async/fire-and-forget pattern or separate logging service. (8) Compliance: Support PCI-DSS Requirement 10 (logging and monitoring), SOC 2 Type II audit readiness, GDPR right-to-audit. (9) Cost: Estimate $200/month hot storage, $50/month warm storage, $20/month cold storage = $270/month for audit infrastructure.'

findings.title: Backup and Disaster Recovery Infrastructure
findings.reasoning: Database backup and disaster recovery are essential for business continuity. Database grows from 15GB (current) to 120GB at 24 months. Backup strategy: daily full backup (7-day retention) = 120GB, weekly backup (4-week retention) = 4x120GB = 480GB, monthly backup (12-month retention) = 12x120GB = 1440GB. Total storage: ~2TB for backups. Recovery targets: RPO (Recovery Point Objective) <1 hour (acceptable data loss), RTO (Recovery Time Objective) <15 minutes (downtime acceptable for brief maintenance).
findings.recommendation: Add NFR-CAP-012: 'The system SHALL implement backup and disaster recovery: (1) Backup strategy: (a) Daily full database backup at 2 AM UTC, retained 7 days. (b) Weekly full backup at 1 AM UTC Sunday, retained 4 weeks. (c) Monthly full backup on 1st at 1 AM UTC, retained 12 months. (d) Transaction log backup every 15 minutes, retained 7 days. (2) Backup storage: 2TB total (daily 120GB × 7 = 840GB, weekly 480GB, monthly 1440GB, 7 days transaction logs 50GB). Use tiered storage: local fast storage for 7-day backups, S3/cloud storage for weekly/monthly, glacier for >90-day archives. (3) Backup testing: Monthly restore test to alternate infrastructure, documented and approved. (4) RPO/RTO targets: RPO 1 hour (acceptable data loss), RTO 15 minutes (time to restore from backup). (5) Disaster recovery: Primary data center (current region) with read replica. Secondary data center (different region) with read-only replica, updated asynchronously. Failover time <30 minutes (manual) or <5 minutes (automated). (6) Replication strategy: Async replication from primary to secondary (tolerate <1 hour data loss in worst case). (7) Backup cost: $50/month local storage, $100/month cloud storage (S3), $20/month archive (glacier) = $170/month ($2,040/year). (8) Monitoring: Backup success rate 100%, restore test success 100%, replication lag <1 minute.'

findings.title: Define Revenue-Critical Availability Targets (99.99%)
findings.feature: Checkout Process (All 5 Steps), Payment Processing, Order Confirmation
findings.reasoning: Checkout and payment processing are direct revenue paths. Every minute of downtime directly loses customer transactions and revenue. Industry standard for e-commerce is 99.99%. DeFOSPAM flagged critical gaps: Payment Failure Recovery, Payment Gateway Decline/Failure Recovery, Invalid Payment Gateway Response - all suggest current system cannot tolerate payment service failures. 99.99% target (4 nines) is required.
findings.recommendation: Deploy checkout and payment services in active-active configuration across 2+ geographic regions. Implement multi-region database replication with synchronous writes to ensure zero data loss. Integrate with 2+ payment gateway providers with automatic failover. Implement circuit breaker pattern for payment gateway integration. RTO=5min requires automated health detection and traffic rerouting (DNS failover or load balancer).
implementation_steps: 1. Provision secondary data center in different geographic region
2. Set up synchronous database replication between regions
3. Configure GeoDNS or intelligent load balancer with health checks
4. Integrate backup payment gateway provider
5. Implement payment gateway fallback logic
6. Establish health check monitoring (30-second interval)
7. Run failover drills monthly

findings.title: Define High-Value Service Availability Targets (99.95%)
findings.feature: Product Catalog, Product Search, User Authentication, Shopping Cart
findings.reasoning: Product catalog, search, and authentication enable customer discovery and account access. These services are essential but less time-critical than payment. Downtime prevents customers from discovering products but guests can still checkout (REQ086). Authentication outage impacts returning customers but doesn't immediately lose in-flight transactions. 99.95% (3.5 nines) is appropriate for these services.
findings.recommendation: Deploy catalog and authentication services with active-passive failover. Primary data center handles all traffic. Secondary data center is standby replica with real-time replication. On primary failure, automated failover redirects traffic (RTO 15 minutes). Session data must be persisted to distributed cache or database (not in-memory only on single server). Implement circuit breaker for external catalog data sources.
implementation_steps: 1. Migrate session storage from in-memory to Redis cluster or database
2. Set up read replica database for catalog data
3. Configure automated failover for primary -> secondary promotion
4. Implement health checks for catalog and auth services
5. Set up load balancer failover policy
6. Test failover every 2 weeks

findings.title: Define Secondary Service Availability Targets (99.9%)
findings.feature: Wishlist, Reviews, CMS Content (News/Blog), Customer Support
findings.reasoning: Wishlist, reviews, and CMS content are important for customer experience but non-transactional. Downtime does not prevent commerce. Customer support communication can be queued. 99.9% (2 nines) allows for scheduled maintenance and graceful degradation. RTO of 60 minutes acceptable for these services as they do not block revenue.
findings.recommendation: Use blue-green deployment strategy for these services. Maintain two identical production environments. Deploy updates to inactive environment, run tests, then switch traffic with zero-downtime. Acceptable downtime for maintenance is 60 minutes. Not suitable for handling unplanned failures - for that use backup to secondary data center.
implementation_steps: 1. Deploy blue-green infrastructure for wishlist, reviews, CMS
2. Implement automated smoke tests for green environment
3. Configure instant traffic switch capability
4. Schedule maintenance windows: weekly, Tuesday 2-4am UTC
5. Announce maintenance 7 days in advance
6. Keep blue environment as rollback option

findings.title: Eliminate Single Point of Failure: Payment Gateway Provider
findings.reasoning: DeFOSPAM explicitly flagged: 'Missing Payment Gateway Decline/Failure Recovery' and 'Missing Error Handling for Invalid Payment Gateway Response'. Current requirements (REQ090-091) specify payment processing but provide NO fallback if primary gateway fails. Single payment gateway = revenue-blocking single point of failure. Even 1 minute of payment processing downtime is unacceptable for e-commerce.
findings.recommendation: Integrate 2+ independent payment gateway providers (e.g., Stripe primary + PayPal backup). Implement payment orchestration layer that: (1) Detects payment gateway failures within 10 seconds, (2) Automatically routes to backup provider, (3) Implements retry logic with exponential backoff, (4) Queues payments for later processing if both providers fail. Design for idempotency: retrying payment must not double-charge customer.
implementation_steps: 1. Sign up for 2 payment gateway providers (Stripe + PayPal recommended)
2. Implement payment orchestration service with provider abstraction
3. Add health checks for each provider (ping every 30 seconds)
4. Implement automatic failover logic
5. Queue failed payments to async job system
6. Implement retry mechanism: exponential backoff, max 3 retries
7. Add customer notification: 'payment processing, confirmation email sent'
8. Test failover: simulate primary provider failure, verify traffic routes to backup

findings.title: Define Recovery Time Objective (RTO): 5 Minutes for Revenue-Critical Services
findings.reasoning: 5 minutes RTO for 99.99% availability equals: downtime_budget = 0.44 hours/year = 26.3 minutes/year. 5-minute RTO means on average we can tolerate ~5 failures per year (5 min × 5 = 25 min). RTO of 5 minutes is industry-standard for revenue-critical services. Requires automated health detection (30-60 sec) and traffic rerouting (30-60 sec). Manual intervention cannot meet 5-minute RTO.
findings.recommendation: Implement automated failover with RTO of 5 minutes: (1) Health checks every 30 seconds detect failure, (2) Load balancer or DNS removes failed server from rotation within 30 seconds, (3) In-flight requests timeout and retry, (4) Automated traffic shift to healthy region/instance. This requires: health monitoring infrastructure, automated failover orchestration (Kubernetes, Terraform, or custom scripts), and multi-region deployment.
implementation_steps: 1. Deploy health check infrastructure (Kubernetes probes or custom)
2. Configure health check frequency: 30-second interval
3. Set failure threshold: 2 consecutive failed checks = remove from rotation
4. Implement load balancer failover policy
5. Test RTO: simulate server failure, measure time to traffic shift
6. Document failure recovery procedures
7. Run failure drills monthly

findings.title: Define Recovery Point Objective (RPO): Zero Data Loss for Orders and Payments
findings.feature: Order Management, Payment Processing, Order Confirmation
findings.reasoning: Orders and payment transactions are financial records. Losing even one transaction = audit failure, compliance violation, and customer dispute. RPO=0 means: in event of failure, zero transactions can be lost. This requires synchronous replication (both primary and secondary have identical data before committing). DeFOSPAM flagged race condition in order confirmation - suggests current system does not ensure atomic writes to both regions.
