M.reasoning: Noyce defined SLAs for end-to-end transactions, but without APM tracing, there's NO way to know which component is causing the slowness. If Search p95 latency is 1.2s, is it: Elasticsearch (slow query), Redis cache miss, network latency, or web server serialization? Without APM distributed tracing, troubleshooting becomes guesswork. Engineers waste hours digging through logs instead of pinpointing root cause in seconds.
M.recommendation: Deploy APM agent library with comprehensive instrumentation: (1) Web server layer - measure HTTP request handling time (target < 300ms), count requests per endpoint, track error responses; (2) Database queries - measure query execution time per statement, track slow queries (> 100ms), monitor connection pool utilization (target < 80%), measure transaction duration; (3) Cache operations - track Redis GET/SET latency (target < 50ms), measure cache hit ratio per cache type, alert on eviction rate spikes; (4) External API calls - measure latency to payment gateway, shipping API, recommendations service, track circuit breaker status; (5) Message queue - measure message processing time, dead letter queue depth (if async patterns used); (6) Background jobs - measure execution time, failure rate, retry attempts. Use Datadog APM or New Relic APM. Configure trace sampling: 100% for errors and slow requests (> 2x baseline), 5% for normal requests (adjust based on traffic volume). Implement custom tags: feature_name, user_tier, geographic_region to enable drill-down analysis.

M.title: Database and Cache Monitoring Insufficient
M.feature: Database (F18-F25), Cache (Shopping Cart, Product Catalog, Sessions)

monitoring.alert_critical: Connection pool > 90%, cache memory > 95%, query time > 200ms

M.reasoning: Liskov identified critical database bottleneck: connection pool reaches 880 queued connections at 5K users (from 200 current pool size). Ada recommended 120 app connections from pool. Without real-time monitoring of connection pool utilization, queue depth, and wait times, this bottleneck goes undetected until checkout slowness reports arrive during flash sale. Similarly, Redis cache (for sessions, cart, products) has no monitoring for memory pressure, eviction rate, or hit ratio - missing signals that would indicate need for additional cache nodes or TTL tuning.
M.recommendation: Implement database and cache monitoring: (1) Database connection pool - measure utilization % (per app, web, admin), queue depth (queued requests), wait time for available connection. Set alerts: Warning at 70% utilization (room to scale), Critical at 90%. Correlate queue spikes with slow checkout to trigger investigation. (2) Slow query monitoring - collect all queries > 100ms (configurable), track query count, execution time, affected rows. Use slow query log to identify optimization targets. (3) Database resource usage - CPU%, memory%, disk I/O (read/write ops), replication lag if read replica present. Alert on replication lag > 10 seconds (data consistency risk). (4) Redis cache - measure memory used %, eviction rate (items removed per second), command latency (p95 for GET/SET, target < 50ms). Alert on eviction rate > 50/sec (memory pressure, need more nodes) or latency spike > 100ms (possible network issue or keyspace contention). (5) Cache hit ratio - measure % of requests served from cache vs. origin. Track per cache type (session cache, product detail cache, search result cache). A hit ratio < 50% indicates misconfigured TTLs or cache sizing issue. Deploy monitoring via APM agent, Prometheus exporters, or cloud vendor tools (RDS Insights, ElastiCache metrics).

M.title: Alert Fatigue Prevention Not Designed
M.reasoning: Without alert fatigue prevention, on-call engineers receive hundreds of duplicate/related alerts (e.g., if database goes down, both 'high app CPU' and 'high app latency' and 'checkout failures' fire simultaneously, even though root cause is single database event). Engineers become desensitized to alerts, miss real incidents in noise, and burn out. Industry studies show > 100 unique alerts per week per engineer leads to 80% miss rate on critical incidents.
M.recommendation: Implement 4-layer alert fatigue prevention: (1) AGGREGATION - group related alerts by component (database alerts grouped together, payment alerts grouped together). Use alert tags (app_tier, region, feature) to correlate. (2) DEDUPLICATION - suppress duplicate alerts for 30 minutes once initial alert fired (don't repeat every 5 minutes). Use alert ID or fingerprint to identify duplicates. (3) DEPENDENCY AWARENESS - if database health check fails, automatically suppress downstream 'high app latency' and 'high app CPU' alerts (expected consequence of database issue). Configure dependency rules in PagerDuty. (4) ANOMALY DETECTION - alert on deviation from baseline, not absolute threshold. Use Datadog/New Relic anomaly detection: alert if response time increases 2x normal for that time of day, rather than fixed threshold. Implement 'quiet hours' for non-critical alerts (e.g., suppress 'secondary informational' alerts during incident window if critical alert already fired). Track alert volume metric - if > 50 alerts/hour, investigate alert rules and tune thresholds. Goal: < 10 actionable alerts per engineer per week.

M.title: Automated Mitigation Actions Not Defined
M.reasoning: Yao identified 513ms total security overhead + rate limiting needs. Liskov identified payment gateway cascade failure risk. Without automated mitigation, incident response is manual: engineer gets paged, spends 5-10 minutes diagnosing root cause, 5-10 minutes executing mitigation (scale up, restart service, drain traffic). During that 10-20 minute window, customers experience outage, checkout abandonment increases, revenue lost. Automated mitigation can respond in seconds (auto-scale spins up new server in 30-60 seconds, circuit breaker opens in milliseconds).
M.recommendation: Define and implement 8 automated mitigation triggers: (1) AUTO-SCALE APP TIER - if CPU > 85% for 2 minutes, spin up 2 new app servers; rollback when CPU < 50% for 5 minutes. Max scale: 15 servers (2.5x baseline of 6). (2) CIRCUIT BREAKER FOR PAYMENT GATEWAY - if payment response time > 5s or error rate > 5%, fail-fast: return 'Authorization Pending' to user, queue for async retry. Queue can process up to 1000 retries/hour in background. (3) DATABASE CONNECTION POOL RECOVERY - if pool utilization > 80% for 1 minute, drain idle connections from cache layer, reset stale connections. Alert engineer to investigate root cause. (4) CACHE EVICTION RECOVERY - if Redis eviction rate > 100 items/sec (memory pressure), reduce all cache TTLs by 50% for 30 minutes. Monitor cache hit ratio; if drops > 5%, revert TTL reduction. (5) GRACEFUL DEGRADATION / TRAFFIC SHEDDING - if network saturation > 80% on load balancer for 1 minute, shed non-critical features: defer product recommendations, defer wishlist operations, prioritize checkout and auth traffic. Show users message: 'High demand; some features temporarily unavailable.' (6) SEARCH SLOWNESS RECOVERY - if search p95 > 1200ms AND Elasticsearch GC > 1 second, flush oldest 10% of search result cache; reduce facet aggregation depth. (7) MEMORY LEAK DETECTION - if Java heap grows > 100MB/hour despite GC, trigger heap dump and notify engineer; consider rolling restart if critical. (8) RATE LIMITING ACTIVATION FOR ATTACKS - if login error rate > 0.5% (possible brute-force), auto-enable aggressive rate limiting (2 attempts per IP per 5 minutes vs. normal 5 per 30 min) until error rate drops. Implement via Lambda functions, Kubernetes controllers, or APM platform automation. Test mitigation actions in staging quarterly.

M.title: Key Dashboards Not Designed for Stakeholders
M.reasoning: Without purpose-built dashboards, different stakeholders lack visibility: Engineers don't know system health at a glance (need 30+ metric alerts to understand status). DevOps can't track capacity headroom or plan scaling (need to dig through multiple tools). Business/Product teams can't correlate performance to revenue impact or customer abandonment (need to ask engineers). This information asymmetry slows decision-making and incident response.
M.recommendation: Build 4 purpose-built dashboards: (1) ENGINEERING OPERATIONS DASHBOARD (5-second refresh) - 'SLA traffic light' showing green/yellow/red status for 6 critical features; current p95 response time vs. SLA; active alerts sorted by severity; request rate and latency distribution (p50/p90/p95/p99); CPU/memory/disk gauges per tier; recent incidents timeline. On-call engineer can glance at this and know system health instantly. (2) OPS/DEVOPS INFRASTRUCTURE DASHBOARD (30-second refresh) - server inventory table (count, avg CPU%, memory%, disk I/O); auto-scaling history (events last 7 days with reasons); database health details (connection pool %, slow query count, replication lag); network health (saturation, packet loss, DNS time); log volume and top 10 errors; cost tracking hourly. (3) BUSINESS/PRODUCT DASHBOARD (60-second refresh) - real-time conversions and revenue (orders/min vs 7-day avg); checkout completion rate; payment success rate; average order value; NPS proxies (search bounce rate, session abandonment, page load time); SLA violations in last 24h with estimated lost revenue; traffic/capacity headroom with scaling forecast. (4) STATUS PAGE (60-second refresh, public) - simple traffic light showing all systems operational / degraded / outage; uptime % for last 1 hour (99.98% = green); plain English known issues if any; 7-day historical uptime chart. Dashboards auto-generated from monitoring platform (Datadog, New Relic); ensure mobile-friendly display.

M.title: Performance Regression Detection Missing
M.reasoning: Without build-over-build performance comparison, performance regressions slip into production undetected. Example: Engineer optimizes feature A but accidentally introduces 20% slower database query in feature B. Synthetic tests don't catch this (total throughput unchanged). Performance regresses 2-3% per week until, after 3 months, system is 30% slower and customer complaints flood in. By then, root cause is buried in 100+ commits. Regression detection in CI/CD catches these issues before production deployment, saving investigation time and preventing SLA violations.
M.recommendation: Implement 6-phase regression detection strategy: (1) BASELINE COLLECTION (CI stage) - after unit tests pass, deploy to isolated performance test environment, run synthetic load test at 1x peak (2500 users, 8000 tx/hr) for 20 minutes, collect p50/p90/p95/p99 latencies and resource metrics per feature. Store baseline as artifact (baseline-{commit-hash}.json). (2) REGRESSION ANALYSIS (CI stage, post-baseline) - compare new baseline to previous known-good baseline; calculate % regression per feature. Flag if Catalog p95 > 2310ms (5% above baseline), Search > 1260ms, Auth > 1260ms, Cart > 840ms, Checkout > 9450ms, Payment > 3675ms. Block merge if regression > 5%; require engineer to investigate. (3) ROOT CAUSE DRILL-DOWN (CI, automated) - if regression detected, auto-generate HTML report with component-level latency breakdown (which component regressed?), code diff highlighting changed methods/endpoints, allocation analysis (new memory churn?), slow query detection (new queries? changed indexes?), external API calls (new calls added?). (4) STAGING VALIDATION (post-merge) - deploy merged code to staging, run 1.5x peak load test (3750 users, 12000 tx/hr) for 1 hour; ensure no regression > 10% from baseline and error rate < 0.1%. Block production deployment if staging test fails. (5) CONTINUOUS PROD MONITORING (post-deployment) - compare production p95 latencies to previous 3 deployments; alert if current p95 > max(previous 3) + 3%. Monitor for 30 minutes post-deployment (stabilization window). (6) HISTORICAL TREND ANALYSIS (weekly) - track average p95 latency trend over 12 weeks; identify slow regressions (2-3% per week creep); correlate with deployments and feature launches; recommend optimization targets if trend negative. Document findings in weekly performance report to engineering leadership.

M.title: Security Performance Overhead Not Monitored
M.feature: User Authentication, Payment Processing, Rate Limiting
M.reasoning: Yao identified 513ms total security overhead (bcrypt 200ms non-negotiable, JWT 8ms, rate limiting 5ms, TLS 45ms, etc.). These security controls are essential but create latency tax on performance budgets. Without monitoring each security component individually, it's impossible to diagnose where latency is spent: Is login slow because bcrypt is slow? Because session lookup is slow? Because network is slow? Monitoring security overhead as separate metrics enables targeted optimization without sacrificing security.
M.recommendation: Add security-specific metrics to APM and dashboard: (1) PASSWORD HASHING LATENCY - measure bcrypt computation time per login (target 200ms, non-negotiable for security; bcrypt cost factor 12 is industry standard). Monitor for deviations (if > 250ms, possible CPU contention or hardware issue). (2) JWT TOKEN VALIDATION LATENCY - measure time to validate JWT signature (RS256) per request (target 8ms per validation). If > 15ms, indicates CPU contention or excessive validations per request. (3) RATE LIMITING CHECK LATENCY - measure Redis lookup time to check rate limit counters (target 5ms). Spike in this metric indicates Redis latency issue or excessive rate limit checks. (4) TLS HANDSHAKE TIME - measure time to establish TLS connection (target < 50ms for new connections, < 5ms for session resumption). If > 100ms, indicates TLS library issue or CPU contention. (5) OCSP STAPLING STATUS - measure time to validate certificate (0ms if OCSP stapled successfully, 20-30ms if OCSP check required). Track % of requests with valid stapling (target > 95%). (6) DATABASE ENCRYPTION OVERHEAD - measure time to encrypt/decrypt data at rest (target 2-3ms, hardware-accelerated). If > 5ms, indicates encryption library issue or lack of hardware acceleration. (7) RATE LIMIT VIOLATION RATE - track % of requests rejected by rate limiter (normal < 0.01%, attack scenario > 1%). Correlate with authentication errors to detect brute-force attacks. Monitor these metrics on security-focused dashboard alongside auth latency. Alert if any security metric exceeds baseline by > 20% (indicates possible attack or misconfiguration). Document that these latencies are non-negotiable and baked into SLAs (e.g., Login SLA of 1.2s already includes 200ms bcrypt).

M.title: Health Check Endpoints Not Designed
M.reasoning: Health checks are lightweight endpoints used by load balancers and orchestrators (Kubernetes, Docker Compose) to determine if a server is healthy and should receive traffic. Without well-designed health checks, orchestrators may route traffic to failed servers or fail to detect degradation. Health checks must be < 50ms and must check only essential dependencies (database connectivity, cache accessibility) without expensive operations (e.g., no database queries, no external API calls).
M.recommendation: Design 4-tier health check endpoints: (1) /health (< 10ms) - liveness check; confirms web server responding, configuration loaded. Returns 200 OK {"status": "healthy"}. Called every 5 seconds by load balancer. (2) /health/ready (< 50ms) - readiness check; confirms app can accept traffic. Checks database ping (no query), Redis ping, external API circuit breaker status, minimum resource thresholds (memory > 100MB free, disk > 1GB free). Returns 200 OK {"status": "ready", "components": {"database": "ok", "cache": "ok", ...}}. Called every 10 seconds by orchestrator; if fails, drain traffic and restart. (3) /health/live (< 20ms) - process aliveness; detects deadlocks or infinite loops. Performs quick heartbeat check (thread pool responsive, request queue not stuck). Called every 3 seconds by scheduler; if fails, force restart process. (4) /health/deps (< 200ms) - deep dependency check; used by monitoring system (not load balancer). Checks database connectivity (SELECT 1), Redis GET/SET, payment gateway circuit breaker status, Elasticsearch cluster health. Returns detailed status of all upstream dependencies. Called every 30 seconds by monitoring system; failures trigger alert to engineer. Implement in application startup; ensure health checks don't require full application initialization (can check at boot time). Document expected behavior of each endpoint in runbook.

M.title: Pre-Production Monitoring Coverage Insufficient
M.reasoning: Performance problems caught in staging (before production) are 100x cheaper to fix than problems caught in production. Yet staging monitoring is often minimal or non-existent. Engineers optimize features in dev environment where network latency is 0ms and database is local; performance degrades when moved to realistic staging environment. Staging should be production-like: same data volume, same network topology, same infrastructure (or scaled version).
M.recommendation: Define staging monitoring coverage: (1) SYNTHETIC MONITORING - run every 10 minutes (vs. 5 in prod) from 1-2 regions; same test suite (homepage, search, login, add-to-cart, checkout, payment). Alert thresholds: if staging p95 > 5% above production baseline, investigate regression before allowing merge to main branch. (2) RUM MONITORING - enable 50% sampling (vs. 10% prod) to catch regressions faster. (3) APM MONITORING - 10% trace sampling (vs. 5% prod) - lower sampling acceptable since staging traffic lower. (4) INFRASTRUCTURE MONITORING - full 10-second collection (same as prod) with 30-day retention. (5) PERFORMANCE GATES (automated) - after code merge, run staging load test at 1.5x peak for 1 hour; block production deployment if: p95 regression > 10% from baseline, error rate > 0.1%, memory grows > 50MB over hour (leak detection), recovery time after load drop > 2 minutes. (6) DATABASE/CACHE MONITORING - same alerts as production (connection pool > 80%, cache eviction rate > 50/sec, slow queries > 100ms). (7) LOG RETENTION - 7 days (vs. 30 days hot + 1 year archive in prod). Deploy similar monitoring stack as production (Datadog, New Relic, Prometheus) with cost optimization (lower sampling, shorter retention).

M.title: Monitoring Rollout Timeline Not Defined
M.reasoning: Comprehensive monitoring is complex (7 layers, 40+ metrics per feature, 4 dashboards, automated mitigation). Without a phased rollout plan, monitoring rollout will slip or be incomplete, leaving gaps. Critical phase is between code freeze and production launch - best time to add monitoring infrastructure.
M.recommendation: Deploy monitoring in 3 phases over 9 weeks: PHASE 1 (Weeks 1-2): Health checks + synthetic monitoring. Implement /health endpoints (4 tiers). Deploy synthetic tests for 6 critical journeys from 3 regions, every 5 minutes. Alert on p95 > warning threshold or error rate > 1%. Set up PagerDuty integration. Estimated effort: 2 engineers, 2 weeks. Cost: ~$2K/month (basic synthetic service). PHASE 2 (Weeks 3-5): APM + RUM + Infrastructure. Integrate Datadog APM or New Relic (deploy agent library). Instrument all critical components (web, app, DB, cache, APIs). Deploy Browser RUM SDK on all pages; configure 10% sampling. Set up Prometheus + Grafana for infrastructure (CPU, memory, disk, network). Alert thresholds per feature (warning, critical, incident levels). Estimated effort: 3 engineers, 3 weeks. Cost: ~$5K/month (APM platform + infrastructure monitoring). PHASE 3 (Weeks 6-9): Mobile monitoring + Transaction tracing + Regression detection + Dashboards. Integrate mobile SDK (Firebase Crashlytics or New Relic Mobile). Configure distributed tracing (APM traces all transactions end-to-end). Implement CI/CD regression detection (baseline collection, comparison, root cause analysis). Build 4 dashboards (engineering, ops, business, status page). Test automated mitigation actions (auto-scale, circuit breaker, traffic shed) in staging. Estimated effort: 2-3 engineers, 4 weeks. Cost: ~$8K/month (full monitoring platform + mobile + dashboards). Total effort: 7-8 engineers, 9 weeks (or 2-3 engineers for 9 weeks sequentially). Recommend starting Phase 1 immediately (pre-launch sprint); Phase 2-3 can run in parallel post-launch (critical for continuous monitoring of production).

E.title: Critical: 24-Hour Soak Test Required to Detect Memory Leaks Before Production
E.reasoning: Ada's capacity model indicates 112 vCPU, 272 GB RAM infrastructure. Liskov identified database connection pool as bottleneck (breaks at 5K users). Yao flagged 513ms security overhead. Previous agents did not specify soak testing, yet DeFOSPAM scenarios include 25+ features. Long-running production will expose memory leaks (request objects retained, cache unbounded growth, connection leaks) that short load tests miss. Industry best practice: 24-hour soak minimum before production. nopCommerce must validate no runaway memory growth or connection exhaustion.
E.recommendation: Execute SOAK-001: 24-hour soak at 2500 concurrent users (peak baseline), gradually ramp up 100->2500 over 30 min, sustain 23 hours, ramp down 10 min. Monitor: heap memory (< 50MB/hour growth after 2h warm-up), database connections (stable at 120), file handles (< 5000), thread count (stable), cache hit rate (> 85%), error rate (< 0.1%). Measure p95 response time hour 0-1 vs hour 23-24; accept max 5% degradation. Retest after any memory leak fixes.

E.title: Critical: Database Connection Pool Exhaustion Risk at 5K+ Concurrent Users
E.feature: All features (Shopping Cart, Order Management, Inventory Validation)
E.reasoning: Liskov's NFR-SCL-007 identified: current pool size 200 is insufficient for 5K users. App tier allocates 120 connections (20/server * 6 servers). At peak 8000 tx/hr = 2.2 tx/sec. If avg query takes 50ms, max throughput = (120 connections * 20 queries/sec per conn) = 2400 tx/hr. But real queries average 100-200ms (including lock wait), reducing effective throughput. At 5K users with longer transactions, connection pool queue builds instantly. New connections rejected with 'Connection pool exhausted' error. This is a hard failure mode, not graceful degradation.
E.recommendation: IMMEDIATE ACTION: (1) Before scaling to 5K users, increase pool size from 200 to 300-400. (2) Implement PgBouncer (connection pooling middleware) on each app server to multiplex app connections to DB pool. (3) Execute STRESS-001 (2x peak, 5000 users): monitor connection pool utilization; must stay < 80% of 200-connection pool (<=160). (4) Stress test target: at 5000 users, active connections < 160; queue length < 10; no 'pool exhausted' errors. (5) For production: deploy PgBouncer in session mode (multiplexes connections); supports unlimited app-tier connections to single DB connection pool.

E.title: Critical: Race Condition in Stock Validation Under Concurrent Load Must Be Tested
E.feature: Shopping Cart, Order Confirmation, Inventory Management
E.reasoning: Liskov's NFR-SCL-008 flagged critical race condition: 'Multiple customers simultaneously add the same limited-stock product to cart and reach order confirmation. Between cart add validation and order confirmation, stock depletes (stock can go negative).' nopCommerce runs without pessimistic row-level locking (FOR UPDATE clause). At peak 2500 users, ~250 customers may add same product to cart concurrently. Without locking, two customers add last unit; both reach checkout; both are allowed to confirm; inventory goes negative; both receive email 'order placed'; one customer's order becomes 'unfulfillable'. This causes: (1) Overselling, (2) Refund chaos, (3) NPS impact. Must be tested and fixed.
E.recommendation: Test SPIKE-002: Simulate 100 concurrent users adding same product (stock=5) to cart over 30s (spike). Measure: (1) How many complete checkout successfully? (2) Does inventory go negative? (3) Does 'out of stock' error appear? Execute against current code (will fail); then test after fix (pessimistic locking with FOR UPDATE). Success criteria: no negative inventory, no overselling, graceful 'out of stock' message after limit reached. Retest at 2x peak (5000 users) to ensure lock contention doesn't cause timeout.

E.title: Critical: 72-Hour Extended Soak Required to Detect Cumulative Leaks and Pattern-Based Issues
E.reasoning: 24-hour soak catches hourly patterns. 72-hour soak catches daily and weekly patterns (nightly batch jobs, weekly maintenance, scheduled cron jobs). Risk: batch job to clean abandoned carts runs daily at 2 AM; if cleanup fails, carts accumulate indefinitely, bloating cart table and slowing INSERT performance. Similarly, session cleanup job must run hourly; if it fails silently, sessions accumulate in Redis, consuming memory. Session lookup latency increases from 30ms to 100ms+ as Redis hits memory limit and eviction rate spikes. These issues emerge only after 24-48 hours of continuous load.
E.recommendation: Execute SOAK-002: 72-hour soak at 2500 users. Window 1 (0-24h): establish baseline. Window 2 (24-48h): compare to baseline; identify daily pattern impacts. Window 3 (48-72h): identify cumulative issues. Monitor: (1) Memory trend across 3 windows (should be stable, not drifting up); (2) Cart table row count (should drop after cleanup job runs); (3) Session count in Redis (should be stable ~2500 max); (4) Query latency (should be stable; if climbs from 30ms to 50ms by hour 72, indicates table bloat). Verify all cleanup jobs execute successfully (add logging). Retest after fixes to cleanup job logic.

E.title: Major: Stress Test at 2x Peak (5000 Users) Must Validate Horizontal Scaling Assumptions
E.reasoning: Ada's baseline: 4 web, 6 app, 2 DB servers handle 2500 peak users. Target: 5000 users. Liskov recommends scaling to 8 web, 10 app servers. But this is untested. Horizontal scaling has subtle issues: (1) Session affinity: if sticky sessions enabled, session loss on server restart; (2) Cache coherency: product cache updated on server A, server B serves stale data; (3) Load balancer health checks: if LB uses /health endpoint, all servers respond 'healthy' even when database is slow; (4) Connection pool sharing: if all 10 app servers try to use 200-connection pool, contention increases.
E.recommendation: Execute STRESS-001: Ramp to 5000 concurrent users over 5 min; sustain 60 min; ramp down 5 min. Measure CPU, memory, connection pool, queue depth, response time per endpoint. Success criteria: (1) Error rate < 1%, (2) p95 SLA compliance > 95% (no more than 5% of requests exceed Noyce's budgets), (3) CPU utilization 70-75% on web tier (indicates headroom for spikes), (4) DB connection pool < 180 of 200 (not maxed), (5) p95 response time returns to baseline within 2 min after ramp-down. If any fail: identify bottleneck (CPU saturation? DB pool? Thread pool?); add more servers; retest.

E.title: Major: Stress Test at 5x Peak (12500 Users) to Validate Graceful Degradation Strategy
E.reasoning: At 5x peak (12500 users), system cannot maintain all SLAs. Response times will exceed budgets; some features will timeout. But system should degrade gracefully: critical features (Checkout, Payment) must succeed; non-critical features (Recommendations, Wishlists) can fail or queue. Yao flagged missing circuit breakers for payment gateway. If payment gateway times out and no circuit breaker, all checkout requests queue waiting for payment timeout; queue fills; requests drop; customers lose their carts. With circuit breaker, payment timeout triggers circuit open; checkout returns 'Service temporarily busy'; customer retries in 30s; circuit closes; payment succeeds.
E.recommendation: Execute STRESS-002: Ramp to 12500 users over 10 min; sustain 50 min; ramp down. Measure per-feature success rates. Expected: (1) Checkout success rate > 80% (vs 99% at baseline); (2) Payment auth success > 95% (critical); (3) Product search success rate > 90% (can tolerate occasional timeout); (4) Recommendation success rate > 70% (non-critical, can fail). CRITICAL: test circuit breaker for payment gateway. Inject failure (timeout all payment requests). Measure: (1) Do checkout transactions fail gracefully (show 'retry' button) or hang indefinitely? (2) Does circuit breaker open after N failures? (3) Do new payment attempts fail fast (not queue indefinitely)? Implement circuit breaker if missing.

E.title: Critical: Breakpoint Test at 10x Peak Identifies Absolute System Limits
E.reasoning: System will eventually break under extreme load. Better to find breaking point in test than in production. At 25000 concurrent users, first hard resource limit to hit is likely: (1) Database connection pool completely exhausted (all 200 connections in use, new ones rejected), (2) File descriptor limit (Linux default ~65k, each connection uses 2-3 FDs: network socket + database connection), (3) Memory exhaustion (OOM killer terminates process), or (4) Load balancer connection limit (~200k concurrent connections). Knowing which breaks first informs capacity planning (e.g., 'If error rate jumps above 50%, first check DB connection pool').
E.recommendation: Execute STRESS-003 (Breakpoint Test): Ramp load progressively from 500 users, increment +2500 every 10 min, measure metrics at each step. Target: find the user count where error rate jumps from < 1% to > 50%. Record: (1) User count at breakpoint, (2) Which resource is exhausted first (DB connections? Memory? FDs?), (3) Error messages (Connection pool exhausted? Out of memory? Too many open files?). Produce graph: response time vs load (should show knee at 2x-5x peak). Documentation: 'If production error rate > 50%, restart with reduced load; check DB pool size first; may need connection pooling middleware.' Use this data to set alerting thresholds.

E.title: Major: Resource Leak Risk - Memory Accumulation in Redis Cache Layer
E.feature: Session Management, Product Search, Shopping Cart
E.reasoning: Liskov's NFR-SCL-004 specified: baseline 2 Redis servers, 64 GB total cache. At 2500 peak concurrent users, cache demand ~12-15 GB (2500 sessions * 1.5 KB + product catalog 8-10 GB). LRU eviction policy should maintain cache at max capacity. Risk: if eviction policy not enabled or improperly tuned, cache grows unbounded until memory exhausted. At 24 hours, cache memory should plateau at ~12 GB; if growing > 100 MB/hour, indicates misconfigured eviction or unbounded data structure (e.g., session data not deleted, only inserted).
E.recommendation: During SOAK-001: monitor Redis memory metrics. Baseline: memory usage should climb 0-2h (warm-up), then plateau. If memory grows > 100 MB/hour after 4h, investigate. Check: (1) LRU eviction policy enabled (CONFIG GET maxmemory-policy should return 'allkeys-lru')? (2) maxmemory setting = 64GB? (3) Session TTL enforced (EXPIRE key 1800 seconds = 30 min)? (4) Product cache TTL reasonable (< 24 hours, not infinite)? Retest after fixes. For production, set Redis memory alert at 50 GB (80% of 64 GB); trigger manual eviction or cache flush if limit reached.

E.title: Major: Resource Leak Risk - Database Connection Pool Leak from Exception Handling
E.reasoning: Common Java bug: exception during database transaction; catch block doesn't call connection.close() (or doesn't use try-with-resources). Connection reserved but never returned to pool. Under sustained load, pool slowly accumulates leaked connections. After 24 hours, pool exhaustion causes all new transactions to wait or fail. Detection: monitor active_connections metric in connection pool. Should be stable at ~60-80 at 2500 users; if grows to 150+ by hour 12, leak likely. Risk is amplified if exceptions are common (e.g., inventory validation fails often due to race condition).
E.recommendation: During SOAK-001: monitor connection pool metrics (active_connections, queue_length) continuously. If queue_length > 5 or active_connections > 140 of 200, trigger alert. Investigate: enable SQL logging to identify which queries fail (constraint violations, timeouts?); review exception handlers to ensure all connection.close() calls in finally blocks or try-with-resources. Static analysis: run SonarQube / FindBugs to find unclosed connections. Retest after fixes. For production: set alert at active_connections > 150 of 200 (75%); immediately investigate root cause.

E.title: Major: Test Data Requirements Must Match Production Scale for Meaningful Results
E.reasoning: Test data size dramatically impacts performance. Example: if test uses only 100 products (vs 10K in production), product search returns faster (all products in RAM cache). Query execution time 50ms in test; 500ms in production (index scan, disk I/O). If test uses only 1000 orders (vs 100K in production), order history queries fast; in production they're slow. Many teams test with small datasets, pass all gates, deploy to production, and hit performance wall on day 1. DeFOSPAM describes 25 features; no mention of production data scale. Noyce's performance budgets assume production-scale data; test must validate this assumption.
E.recommendation: Establish baseline production data volumes: 10K products, 50K customers, 100K orders, 200K reviews, 100K images (from Ada's capacity model). Load this into test database BEFORE soak testing. Verify indexes are present (product_id, order_date, customer_id, etc.). Validate query plans: ensure search queries use index (EXPLAIN ANALYZE in PostgreSQL). During soak test, if p95 response time differs > 20% from budgets, investigate index usage. For volume testing: load 100K products (10x) and measure search latency; must remain < 1.2s p95 (Noyce budget). Document all test data sizes in final report.

E.title: Critical: Test Environment Must Match Production Architecture for Valid Results
E.reasoning: Common pitfall: test on smaller infrastructure (e.g., 2 app servers instead of 6) and declare success. But smaller infrastructure has different bottlenecks. Example: on 2 servers, database connection pool exhaustion occurs at 500 users (only 20 connections per server); on 6 servers, exhaustion at 1500 users. Test must use identical architecture to production (or scale-down proportionally). If production is 4 web + 6 app + 2 DB, test should use same setup (or half-size: 2 web + 3 app + 1 DB). Load must also scale: if reducing servers to 50%, reduce concurrent users to 50% (1250 instead of 2500).
E.recommendation: Provision test infrastructure identical to Ada's recommendations: 4 web (8 vCPU, 8GB RAM), 6 app (8 vCPU, 16GB), 2 DB (16 vCPU, 64GB), 2 Redis (8 vCPU, 32GB). If cost prohibitive, use 50% scale: 2 web, 3 app, 1 DB, 1 Redis. Document all deviations from production. When interpreting test results, scale findings: 'Breaking point at 50% scale = 12500 concurrent users; at 100% scale, estimate 25000 concurrent users.' Include in test report: detailed infrastructure specs, comparison to production, caveat on result extrapolation.

E.title: Major: DeFOSPAM Scenarios Must Be Mapped to Performance Test Scripts for Coverage
E.reasoning: DeFOSPAM identified 25 features and created 25 scenarios. Performance budgets (Noyce) defined for critical features (Catalog, Search, Auth, Cart, Checkout, Payment). But no mapping exists between scenarios and test scripts. Risk: test loads only popular features (browse, search) and ignores edge cases (wishlist, reviews, gift cards). If wishlist feature has unoptimized O(n^2) algorithm, load test won't catch it because wishlist usage < 5% of traffic. Must create test scripts for ALL 25 scenarios to ensure comprehensive coverage.
E.recommendation: Create JMeter/Gatling test script for each of 10+ major DeFOSPAM scenarios: (1) Browse catalog + view product, (2) Search + filter results, (3) Login + register, (4) Add/update cart, (5) Checkout flow, (6) Payment, (7) Order history, (8) Wishlist mgmt, (9) Reviews, (10) Account settings. For each script: define realistic user think time (1-5 sec between actions), measure p50/p90/p95/p99 response times, compare to Noyce's budgets. Weighting: 40% browse, 20% search, 15% cart, 10% checkout, 10% other (realistic traffic distribution). Execute load test with mixed scenarios (not isolated). Validate: each scenario meets its budget under 2500 concurrent users.

E.title: Critical: Performance Gates and Doneness Criteria Must Be Defined to Exit Testing
E.reasoning: Without clear exit criteria, testing becomes open-ended. Team runs tests, finds issues, fixes them, retests, finds more issues, repeat endlessly. No definition of 'done'. Must establish gates: 'Testing is complete when all of these conditions are met.' Gates should be measurable, realistic, and linked to business goals. Example gate: 'Error rate < 0.5% at 2500 concurrent users.' Teams can measure this objectively and move forward. Missing gates from previous agents: no SLA compliance gate, no memory stability gate, no recovery time gate.
E.recommendation: Define 7 exit gates (from test strategy): (1) GATE-LOAD-002: All endpoints meet Noyce's SLAs at baseline 2500 users, (2) GATE-LOAD-003: DB connection pool < 80% utilization, (3) GATE-LOAD-004: Memory growth < 10MB/hour after 2h warm-up, (4) GATE-STRESS-001: 2x peak achieves > 95% SLA compliance, (5) GATE-STRESS-003: System recovers within 2 min after stress, (6) GATE-SOAK-001: 24-hour degradation < 5%, (7) GATE-SOAK-002: No memory leaks detected. 'Must pass' gates (6): gates 1-6. 'Should pass' (3): gates for 5x peak, connection leak, breakpoint. 'Nice to have' (1): 72-hour soak. Overall success: all 6 'must pass' gates. Document in testing plan; share with stakeholders; confirm agreement before testing begins.

E.title: Major: Security Control Overhead (513ms) Must Be Validated Under Peak Load
E.reasoning: Yao identified 513ms security overhead: TLS termination (45ms), bcrypt password hashing (200ms), JWT validation (8ms * 50 concurrent logins = 400ms), rate limiting middleware (10ms). Payment authorization budget (Noyce: 3500ms p95) has only 455ms buffer after TLS (70ms) + OAuth introspection (150ms) + payment gateway processing (2500ms typical). Under peak load, if JWT validation threads saturate or bcrypt CPU cores max out, response time spikes. Yao flagged: 'At 2000 req/sec, JWT validation consumes 50% CPU.' At 5000 concurrent users (higher sustained load), security overhead becomes bottleneck.
E.recommendation: Execute STRESS-001 with security-focused monitoring: measure TLS handshake time, JWT validation latency, bcrypt CPU consumption per login. Target: TLS termination < 70ms p95, JWT validation < 8ms p95 (cache tokens if possible), bcrypt intentional delay 200ms (non-negotiable for security). If JWT validation exceeds 15ms p95 at 5000 users, implement JWT cache (cache parsed/validated tokens for 60s). If bcrypt becomes bottleneck (CPU > 80%), reduce max concurrent logins (rate limit: 50 login attempts per IP per 30 min). For payment: measure authorization latency including TLS + OAuth + payment gateway. Budget 3500ms; security overhead should consume < 500ms.

E.title: Major: Spike Test Required to Validate Auto-Scaling Response to Flash Sale Events
E.reasoning: Real-world scenario: nopCommerce runs a flash sale. Users arrive at 500 -> 5000 in 30 seconds (10x spike). Auto-scaling must react: provision new servers, update load balancer, warm up caches. If auto-scaling latency > 60 seconds, queue builds; requests timeout; customers abandon checkout; revenue lost. Turing flagged cart abandonment threshold at 5-10 seconds; spike lasting > 30 seconds = high abandonment. Previous agents did not specify spike test, but it's critical for e-commerce.
E.recommendation: Execute SPIKE-001: Users 500 (steady) -> 5000 (over 30s) -> sustain 3 min -> ramp down. Measure: (1) When does auto-scaling trigger (CloudWatch alarm threshold, e.g., CPU > 70%)? Should be within 30-60s. (2) How many new servers added? (3) Do new servers join load balancer before requests timeout? (4) Is cache warmed (cold cache = slow first requests)? (5) Error rate during spike: should spike to 5-10% then drop to < 1% as servers online. Success: p95 response time stays < 5s during spike (vs 2.2s normal); no order loss; servers scale back down to baseline when spike ends. Validate: enable auto-scaling in test env; confirm policies trigger; measure scale-up latency.
